text: For many of their days here, the Hill sisters roamed the Government House grounds feeling a lot like lonely princesses in an abandoned castle. “It’s weird, right?” Mel asked, to which Claire answered with equal uncertainty, “Yeah.” Claire looked at the walls, ceiling and furniture first when she said, “Whoa, Mel! There’s like a whole movie theatre in there.” Mel looked at the floor to scoff, “Did Dad forget to get a dog or somethin’? Never mind, maybe it’s another one of his surprises .” “Wow! So this is the ballroom, huh? It’s is a lot nicer than I thought it was going to be,” Claire marvelled. “I guess.” “Come on, Mel; Dad said this was a closed-door meeting. We shouldn’t be here.” “Will you be quiet? I’m trying to listen.” “Why? It’s just boring policy jargon. And it’s not like you can hear much anyway.” “Exactly, so what’s the harm?” “Mel, there are cameras ? Let’s go now before we get in trouble.” “Then you go, I’ll catch up with you.” “Fine, whatever! I’ll meet you outside.” Their ever-busy mayor-father found time for the twins most evenings, and only sometimes that involved a little schmoozing photo shoot before supper. “Now smile big, girls! This will be on the front page.” Claire found her sister sulking in the sunroom devoid of any sun. “So this is where you’re hiding. I think Dad wants to order food soon, possibly Chinese this time?” “Go ahead. I’m just like him; I don’t care what we eat. I’m probably not gonna anyway.” Her small heels clicked harshly against the stone floor when Claire stopped to say, “I don’t like it much either, but he’s trying. Doesn’t he get points for that?” “Points?” Mel laughed darkly. “Oh, we’re keeping score? In that case, Dad’s still got a looong way to go.” “He said he’s working on hosting our school grad dance here, in the ballroom? He told me he’d do it if we wanted it. I, um, kinda got the impression he’d do pretty much anything we ask while we’re here.” Well that was a bit more intriguing for the brunette. “Really?” “I mean, yeah. He feels bad, Mel.” “He should feel bad , Claire. He abandoned us! He didn’t call like he said, he didn’t visit—” “I think Mom had more to do with that than he did. Plus, he called a lot leading up to this, remember?” “Why are you defending him?” “Because he’s trying! What are you doing except complain? You could at least try to see the upside here, try to find the good in the bad. If you won’t do it for him, could’ja maybe do it for me then? Or Mom? At this point, I don’t care—just pick a reason.” Adam found his daughters then and greeted them a little out of breath. “Hey, girls. Pizza or Chinese? Decision time.” Claire spun around at their father’s call, gracefully shifting her crossed arms behind her back while Mel reluctantly rose from her seat opting to keep her cross feelings frontly visible. Claire happily answered for the both of them, “Pizza!” “Again?” he chuckled. “Alright, whatever you want, I’ll phone it in. Oh, and—You can change out of those dresses now if you want, they were just for the photo.” “Right, yeah, we know. We’ll go change then meet you in the kitchen?” “Sounds like a plan to me! Seeyah in a bit.” When their father left them again, Mel snorted, “Pizza, huh?” “We both know you weren’t going to say anything. Besides, I panicked.” “Uh, yeah? I didn’t go anywhere, I totally saw you.”

text: Now that school was over, Melanie and Claire Hill would spend their summer break with their father Adam Green at the Government House. Their mother Sadie Hill didn’t like to admit it, but Adam was keeping up with regular phone chats with the girls better these days — credit where credit is due, right? Not to mention, it’d be a lot harder for Mel and Claire to sneak out and get into anymore trouble here than at their house. “Oookay, girls, get ready—” “—Surprise!” Claire took in the room with quiet, hopeful optimism while she let Melanie speak for the two of them: “Dad, we’re not four.” “I know, heh. Didn’t think kids were into boybands,” he said, eying the poster that he pinned up himself. “Good pick, Dad. We love the SimStreet Boys!” “You do, maybe,” Mel muttered. Their father had to ask, “So, you two like the room?” “Sure, Dad!” “Sure, Dad.” “That’s it? I thought you’d like it a bit more than just, ‘Sure, Dad.’ Well, uh, that’s alright. I know I owe you girls more than just a couple beds and some posters, your Mom told me as much anyway, heh.” Mel had different feelings but she let Claire nod like an obedient bobble-head beside her. “It’s really great, Dad! We love it. Thanks so much!” Frowning at his watch, he said, “Shoot, I gotta run. But I only have a couple meetings today, should wrap up in time for dinner. You kids like pizza? We can order whatever you want. Seeyah later tonight.” Claire mumbled sadly behind him, “Okay. Bye, Dad.” Meanwhile, Mel scanned the room and wondered what she might like to break first.

text: Ben Parker Ward was elated to call out, “Lee! You made it!” “’Course, man!” “Lookin’ sharp, dude! Shirt looks great on you.” (The boys coordinated outfits for the occasion.) “And, hey—new do?” “Yeah,” Lee beamed, “got this and grad comin’ up so Jo cleaned me up.” “Love it! I love you!” Lee laughed along happily when he returned, “Love you too, man!” Behind them, Brandon W. squirmed a little at his friends’ casual use of such an important phrase. Lee’s parents caught up with them then. “Hi boys!” Genji waved. Brandon couldn’t help but notice their sickeningly adorable matching outfits when he sheepishly waved back. “Hi, Mr. and Mrs. Sato.” Michiyo was eager to ask, “Ben, sweetie, where’s your mother? I need to thank her for inviting us.” And Ben was eager to answer, “It’s the reception! Party time, haha! Everyone’s welcome now!” “She’s over there by the ice cream cart.” Lee nodded in the correct direction. As Michi and Gen wandered over to a smiling and waving Amy, Brandon kicked sand around. Ben asked, “Uhh, Brandon? Somethin’ wrong, bro?” “Huh? No.” “Whatever you say.” Richard approached the teens next and greeted them with his cool, deep voice: “Hey kids, you ready for our little gig tonight? Think Max is gonna kick things off soon.” Ben enthusiastically answered for all of them: “Oh yeah! We’re so stoked, Dad!” “Guess that answers that, hey?” he chuckled in Brandon’s direction, who smiled weakly in return unsure how else to reply. Likewise, Lee also felt a little starstruck looking up at Richard Ward in his newly married radiance. “Rich!” It was Claude Rousseau and his family Sophie, Jonathan, and Deryck. Richard left the teens with a brief, “Excuse me, kids,” and happily greeted the latest new arrivals behind them: “Rousseaus! Glad you could make it, just in time too.” Meanwhile, Ben asked his friends, “You guys wanna stay here and be close up, or go on the deck for a better view of everythin’?” “I don’t care,” Brandon answered with melancholic honesty. “You better make the call Ben,” Lee told him. “Ground zero it is then!”

text: Jack Parker nervously gripped his own hands before finding the courage to say: “I’m happy for you, Amy.” “No you’re not, look at you! You’re totally weirded out just like Bonnie.” “I can still be happy for you even if it’s weird.” “We’re not doing anything illegal here, Jack. It’s just a big party that looks like a wedding; today is more ceremonial or spiritual than your traditional legally-binding marriage. This is just for us.” “Right, yeah, I know. I said I’d walk you down the aisle, didn’t I?” With a shy grin he continued, “Plus, ever since Janey and me got together I get the whole complicated relationship thing a little better now. I’m, uh, sorry I gave you such a hard time about it before. Richard and Max are lucky to have your stubbornness — turns out you were right all along, with both of them.” “Okay, what gives? That is definitely the nicest thing you’ve ever said to me.” “Eh, one time exception for a special occasion.” “Thank you, Jack. I love you, even when I don’t say it like I should.” “Yeah, yeah. Save the mush for your two husbands, yah greedy weirdo.” “Hey, yeah—that’s kind of ironic, huh? All this time you’ve been on my back about Dad , saying we can’t have two dads between him and Ted, but then here you are giving your kids two dads on purpose. What gives, Amy ?” He mocked with a playful tone. “Oh my god you’re right. I-I can’t believe you’re actually right. I guess I never saw it like that before.” “Really? Even with his picture right behind you?” The siblings looked to the memory of Sgt. Robert Parker together in a moment of mournful quiet. His army portrait was the only keepsake Amy specifically asked for when their mother Elizabeth Parker-Green died in the spring and the adult children cleared out and closed up the Green house indefinitely. She was the one who broke their silence: “I’m sorry too.” “Eh, I don’t know. Maybe you were a little more right about Dad than I was ready to accept. Have I told you what we’re thinking about naming the baby? Bobby.” “After—?” “After Sargent Dad himself, yeah. Bobby Parker.” “I think that’s a beautiful name, Jack. I can’t wait to meet him, and then watch little Bobby become a new man all of his own.” “Me too.” She raised a curious brow. “So, since when did you get all insightful? I thought you were all jokes.” “I’ve always had insight, but we’re usually too busy fighting to actually talk.” “You know, I think I heard that line before. Let’s change that from now on, hey?” “Deal.”

text: Creation - The Three Brothers: Part Three The Spider and the Hawk would find themselves accosted by the Bear upon their arrival, "For I was greatly worried for each of you, As I knew that I could not prevail against such a creature." Then He would embrace them. "Now I am greatly afraid;" the Spider would confess, "And I have not seen the second of our foes." So the Spider would plan for a moment, And a strike of foresight would give It Vision. "The Hawk must watch over the first of our foes, That Lurking Thing which gave us no chase, For we must know that It shall not chase us, Nor find where we have made our homes. Next the Bear shall come with me, For a worry has struck me that we shall not escape without conflict, And I trust in His strength of arms, And I trust in His bravery." And this would worry the Hawk, For without His vision the others could be brash, Yet He knew that He must trust in the Spider, For It had always trusted Him. Then the Spider and the Bear would make their way deep into the Forest, Heading the opposite way of the way the Spider had gone, And along the path they would laugh and joke, And the Bear had brought great food and wine. So they would be in high spirits when they would first notice the sound, Which was like a pleasant droning hum, And which dulled thought, And sense. Then the Spider would notice the strange charm upon them, For the droning had intruded upon their minds, But the Spider's mind itself was another of Its webs, Made against those strange beasts that lived in thoughts. So the Spider would begin singing a Song, And from this Song would come That Song Which is Creation, Yet that would not come for some time, But this Song would suffice. As the Spider sang so too did the Bear, For He trusted His Brother, And the charm would leave them, And they regained their senses. Yet after that the Spider would feel a gentle pulling within Its mind, As though It was being guided somewhere. The Spider would follow that sensation with the Bear behind, Towards a trap the Spider sought to spring. Yet the Spider had not prepared for the extent of what lay ahead, For It did not understand the foe It faced, Yet as they stepped into a place the Spider had once gone, The extent of the threat became clear. Where once there had been a great swathe of life, A great clearing overgrown with many beasts and fruits, And a place teeming with the many-colored lights of creation, All had become one. The place had become one of wretched and singular purpose, Filled with many-legged things that writhed and gnashed. At the center of which was a tremendous White Tree, Which stood as still as eternity, Yet within it there were eyes that fixed upon the Spider. All around the White Tree, In a series of seven great circles, Were saplings that were all equally distant from each other, And each bore the White Tree's visage. To each of these trees thralls would bring the Stuff of the Forest, Which they would gather from the edge of the growing Hive and take to the Trees, And these thralls were those beasts which had once lived within the Forest, Yet they had come under the White Tree's charm. Both the Spider and the Bear would fear greatly, Yet the Spider would seek to meet with the Tree. So the many-legged things and thralls would part for the Spider and the Bear, And the air buzzed with the pleasant hum, But the Spider would continue to sing, And finally they reached the Tree. And the Tree was tremendous in Its size, For even a single eye was greater by many times than the Bear, And It would speak to the Spider. The voice that pierced its mind would speak in shrieks and screams, And within it would be every voice that could ever be, And every voice that there ever had been, And within it the Spider heard Its own voice. "Why do you deny me?" The Tree would ask of It. "None have before." "I mean no offense to you if-" "To exist with a face that I have not sculpted is offense. Allow me to sculpt you, I shall make you perfect. Never shall you hunger, Never shall you fear, Never shall you despair. Always shall you be with me, Always shall you know love. Always shall you draw breath through me." Yet the Spider knew that the Tree spoke lies, For It knew of the Unity the Tree spoke of, And it was no union of love, But it would be a union of control. "I woul-" Yet the many-legged things would encroach upon them, And the Bear would prepare to face them, And the Tree spoke once more. "Should you beg for mercy I shall grant it, I shall take you into me, I shall take your tears, I shall give you my joy." Then they would be joined in battle, And the Bear would break through the lines, So He would bring with Him the Spider, Who would cling to His fur. The Bear would be injured in the battle, Yet He would break free, And the Spider would stitch His wounds, And It would apologize. "You need not apologize to me Brother, I have sworn to protect you as you protect me, Yet now it is your turn to protect us, And to come up with a plan to face our foes." And the Spider would know He spoke truth, So they would go together back to their homes, To commune with the Hawk and to begin the Spider's plan.

“Sounds great, Rich!” “Good, that’s what I was going for. Between the two of us it’d be pretty sad if we couldn’t figure out a solution for some decent outdoor sound, and on today of all days.” “Tonight’s gonna be something else entirely.” Max turned to his horror and brought Richard’s attention with him: “Oh shit no—Ben! What’re you doing dude!?” “What?” Ben called back quite innocently. “You just sent Brody shooting down the aisle! What were you thinkin’!?” “Oh. Oh yeah.” “Quick, call him back before he wrecks everything!” “Brooody!” Mrrhm? “Bring it back, boy!” Rruuff! “Good boy, Brodester! Erm, sorry Dad. We’ll play somewhere else.” “Good idea.” “Damn,” Max sighed, “I feel like I just lost five years of my life in five seconds.” “Hey, it’s okay; Ben called him back in time, everything’s fine.” Max withdrew into a tight ball so Richard couldn’t help but ask, “Did we change places or somethin’? I’m usually the anxious one. Hey, avoiding disaster has to be a good omen, yeah? Come on, Max. Everythin’ alright? I know you don’t get stage fright .” Max released a very unconvincing, “Yeah, it’s fine. You’re probably right.” Richard grinned in the direction of their waiting altar and patted his soon-to-be husband’s sweaty back. “That’s the spirit. It’s our wedding day, man! Nothing can go wrong today. Relax! Everything is finally all okay. We made it. Enjoy it.” Max wondered if Rich knew how much he sounded like his father Harold back at the Care Home just now, but today wasn’t the time to bring that up. “Y-yeah.”

It was June 2016 when I was first introduced to my now wife. I was working, drawing caricatures for an event in my hometown and @lizadomuch had met the wife of one of my friends who was attending grad school in the area where my now wife lives. I had recently ended a four-year long distance emotionally abusive relationship just over a year beforehand so when this friend texted me that she found my wife, I was reluctant to start dating someone long-distance again. For the time I spent recovering from the previous relationship, I concluded I wanted my partner to be my best friend. I wanted someone eager to go on adventures with me. This friend’s wife told me she had met someone who was looking for a partner to “treat life as an adventure.” Our friend introduced us and we stated texting. We spent about four months just getting to know each other and being friends and we started dating at the end of October 2016. We got married in October 2017.

In the beginning of Summer 2016, I had a friend who had spent the last several weeks recovering from a cardiac issue. He told me he had a bit of an existential crisis and asked if I would be willing to take him to see some of the cool waterfalls I often talked about. On June 13th, 2016, we started our adventure at Mill Creek Falls in the Kumbrabow State Forest, but it was pretty busy. It seemed there was a large group of campers there. We let them enjoy it for the day and moved on to Whitaker Falls. Whitaker Falls is especially important to me because I had a great grandfather who lived off-grid in a cabin nearby for many years into his nineties until he needed assistance. This grandfather passed when I was 11, but he taught me so much about life in the mountains and how to treat the world around you. The sunlight was bright enough to affect the colors of my neutral density (ND) filters. Those filters seemed to block a lot of warmer light. The water was not swelled and it was very clear, but it was up enough to lightly cover a lot of the rocks I would normally use to hop along the water. My friend had not visited as many scenic areas of West Virginia as he had liked, so the day was good to show him these falls. The leaves were full and the sky was blue. The rich golden to warm brown colors of the rocks went well with the greens and the blues. I loved the colors and the textures this shallow cover offered. Almost all of the less active pools were filled and teeming with frog eggs and tadpoles. We rock-hopped upstream as much as we could until we decided to take our shoes off and just walk in the water until we reached the old bridge upstream. For a few years, based on the lighting, the position of that truck and camper, and the two figures on the bridge… …I’m pretty sure that may have been us on that old bridge the day Google took the Maps photo there. After we waded upstream from Whitaker Falls, my friend and I went to a place I find especially interesting. There’s an old branch East and upstream from the falls called Dry Branch. This slightly hidden branch by the Elk Springs Resort has a section with giant bedrock slabs under the eroded rocks. The acoustics here are breathtaking. The way the rocks echo is fascinating. Sometimes (not this time, unfortunately), you can hear and feel the vibrations from water trickling under the boulders while every visible rock is dry. It seems to pass through the stones just below the surface. My friend enjoyed this experience and I loved showing him this place and telling him about my childhood experiences there.

text: Before she could believe it, Naomi Sato was beginning her university career at RBU. She enrolled in the spring’s early admission program that helped transition students from high school to university before the start of the full Fall semester. So far, university living wasn’t too hard to handle. Naomi felt in control. In fact, as she wandered the dormitory halls what stressed Naomi out wasn’t classes or choosing a major or midterms or anything to do with university, really. She was busy with leftover guilt about Dallas MacDonald back in Whibley. That’s when she hit the floor. Hard. “Oh my god! Are you alright?!” “I am so, so sorry.” His buddy was nearly pissing himself with laughter. “Are you alright?” the clutzy collared shirt guy repeated. “ Are you alright?! ” the laugher mocked. “Ohmygod, ohmygod. Stop , please, I’m dyin’!” Through a deserving scowl she muttered, “I’m fine.” Collared Shirt picked up the book that had apparently caused their great collision as Naomi regained her balance. “That was the funniest shit I ‘ave ever seen in my whole life ! Ohmygod, Jon, you fuckin’ hilarious moron!” “Sorry ‘bout my brother ‘ere,” the laugher said. “Four eyes but can’t see what’s square in front of ‘im. Tch, tch! I’m afraid he’s useless, and it’s terminal.” The confused brother confirmed Dr. Laughter’s diagnosis: “I can’t believe that just happened! I-I didn’t see you at all . I’m so sorry!” “It’s okay,” she lied, rubbing her wrists that also took some fall damage. “I mean, that’s gotta be what being a freshman is all about, right? Falling down, getting back up. Talk about a crash course , huh?” “That’s—uh, pretty profound for just getting toppled. I’m Jonathan, this is Deryck.” “With a Y, that’s very important,” Deryck added. “I’m Naomi. I’d say it was nice to meet you guys but my back still hurts.” Jonathan recoiled right away: “I feel really terrible about that. Do you think we could maybe start over sometime?” Deryck whistled an impressed tune. “Would’ja look at the wheels on this guy, eh? I’m almost proud!” “Sure,” Naomi smiled despite herself. She was coming around to Deryck’s crude commentary, his jabs at his brother felt delightfully deserving at the moment, and Jonathan did seem genuinely remorseful. Besides, she knew she was just as distracted as he was. “Seeyah around Jonathan, and Deryck with a Y.” Once Naomi quickly descended the stairwell away from the Rousseau brothers, Jon gave Deryck a look which was promptly ignored: “Hmm. You know, on second thought, maybe I shouldn’t be correctin’ my name like that. Not very cool .” “That’s what you’re worried about? We just seriously injured someone.” “We? You’re the one that hit ‘er mate, I just shoved you.”

text: I grew up on a farm near Wyatt, WV. The stretch of road near my grandparents’s farm is one of the prettiest spots on the route. It was a pretty common occurrence for our family to go for evening walks on this hollow. It’s the kind of road where you wave at everyone who drives by and they wave at you. I took this group of photos between June and July 2016 We would run our hands over the hay fields by the roadway as we entered the tree tunnel at the edge of my grandparents’ farm. Golden evening sunlight paints the road and the field nearby. That last golden light also painted the hillside under my grandparents’ barn. Across the road is my grandparents’ neighbor’s farm with a beautiful classic red barn. There is a silver maple tree pastorally arching over the entrance to the field. This old building served as a chicken coop for many years before my grandma could no longer collect eggs every morning. At the end of the time they had chickens, I believe they lost them to a fox and just never replaced the lost chickens. This roadway is called Cunningham Run. In Appalachia, if a road is called a Run, it’s actually an older English term tied to the creek the road follows. This creek crosses under Cunningham run at a small bridge by my grandparents’ neighbor’s plot. You can see the small red barn located across the road from my grandparents’ house. As you go further up the road, there’s another farm with an old gate on the hillside and I felt it was especially pastoral that day.

text: Inside the MacDonald barn, Naomi was greeted with muted, booming music coming from upstairs. When she reached the second floor the music sounded quieter somehow as though her familiarity with the repurposed teen hangout made room for her, or maybe Jane MacDonald turned down the volume when she heard someone coming up the stairs. “Hey, Janey.” “Howdy,” she answered sarcastically. “If you’re looking for my dumb brother, he’s still inside. Look out duty.” “Goodnight, Dad! Goodnight, Mom! I’ll just be downstairs here watching television by myself.” Dallas smiled cutely hoping a halo might manifest above his head. “Should I ask?” John Jr. muttered to Rhonda. She returned, “You were a teen boy once, what do you think?” “Let’s just go to bed.” “Classic,” Naomi chuckled. She watched Jane fan a paintbrush over the canvas. “I’m gonna miss this.” “Don’t have to. You could be like me and Dorklas and just skip the school thing.” “No way. University’s gonna be great, I’m really looking forward to it. You should go! You’re smart, your family has money, plus you could probably get an art scholarship or something.” “Nice try, but I have other plans.” “Yeah, good luck with that.” Naomi wasn’t serious, and Jane knew this, so the sarcasm kept flowing: “Thank you!” “Hey, uh, I have to tell you something. It’s actually something I need to tell Dallas, but you deserve to know too.” “What’s up?” “I’m—going to be breaking up with him tonight, actually.” “Oh, he’s going to be crushed.” “I know. I feel terrible already.” “Well, um, can I at least ask why?” “RBU,” Naomi shrugged defeatedly. “I’ve given it a lot of thought — like, a lot — and I just feel like we’re on the verge of something big about to start, y’know? I like Dallas a lot, but what can I do? He’s not going to university.” “Yeah, no, I get it. Gotta spread your wings.” “Exactly. I don’t know where I’m gonna end up yet, and I kinda don’t wanna know. The farm is fun, I love it here, but this isn’t it for me. It’s not forever.” “Get out while you can, I say. I mean, that’s my plan anyway.” Suddenly Dallas interjected, “Gee, thanks sis.” His surprising presence shocked the girls; Jane snapped to anger while Naomi’s delicate flower heart lost all of its petals all at once. Jane demanded to know, “What the hell’s wrong with you!? You can’t sneak up on us like that!” “I’m light on my feet, you know that.” “We could’ve been talking about anything up here, idiot! Ever heard of ‘girl talk’ before?” “You were talking about Naomi breaking up with me.” “You heard?” the girlfriend winced. Jane tried to deflect: “So what? You don’t know why . You don’t even know if she was joking!” Normally Dallas’ playful nature lifted a room, but this time he spoke low and seriously: “Stop, alright? I heard everything.” “Dallas,” Naomi pouted, “I’m so sorry.” “I’ll, um, give you guys some space. I was gonna call Jack tonight anyway.” “Dallas,” Naomi tried again, but got cut off: “I thought you liked it here . I thought you liked me.” “I do! It’s just—can you see where I’m coming from at all? I’m moving away .” “Yep. You and everyone else.” “I-I…” Her mouth opened to respond, but she didn’t know what to say to that. “I-I’m gonna go, but I hope we can still be friends one day.” He nodded a little but kept his head bowed.

text: I've seen some posts and comments about people hoping Tamon's B-side gets into the darker aspects of the idol industry and they'll be disappointed if it doesn't. Now the manga has already touched on some of those themes, but I'm ngl, I'll be perfectly happy if it stays mainly a romantic comedy. Not every manga/anime that involves an idol/entertainment industry centered plot needs to be Oshi no Ko or Perfect Blue. I don't think the story will suffer for keeping the tone on the lighter side of the shoujo manga spectrum (cause some shoujo goes to really, really dark places and heavy themes). Especially if the target audience is 13+ and the author decides they don't want to go into more mature material. When Kpop demon hunters debuted a lot of older fans also voiced some disappointment the story didn't get into the nitty gritty of the idol industry and fan culture, but that's not so much a fault of the story as the creators obviously intending the movie to be accessible and enjoyable to kids younger than 13+, as well as young adults and even older adults. I just think it's unfair to already be ready to say a story is "bad" or "poorly written" because it has a light tone. At that point it's more about wishing the story was something it wasn't meant to be in the first place.

“I’m petting him! I’m petting him!” The terrier hummed a soft growl from his small chest. “Well done, son. I think he likes you.” Naomi Sato laughed and shook her head a little. “I can’t believe Winston likes Lee first already, that’s just so typical.” With a head shake of his own their father Genji replied, “How can that be? Winston likes everyone the same, we only just got him.” “My replacement more like. At least Lee will have someone his own size to talk to for a change.” “You’re being mean!” Lee whined. Gen frowned. “I agree, that wasn’t very nice.” “Oh, well! I gotta go now anyways,” she said getting up. Just as the three of them stood in unison, Gen crossed his arms and said, “Just a second Naomi. Going to the farm again?” “Yeah,” she shrugged. “You’ve been spending a lot of time out there. How come Dallas doesn’t visit you, hm? Why is it that you’re the one always going over there?” She avoided eye contact when she answered, “It just makes more sense this way.” “A-huh. You’re not doing anything you’re not supposed to, right?” “ Dad. ” “College starts soon, you know that, right?” “Yes, Dad, obviously I know that. Can I go now?” “I suppose, but this ought to be the last time before—” “—before I move to RBU. I know. I’ll be back in time for dinner.”

text: I might be aromantic but and I think that when people try to argue that something is written lazily or lacks substance by saying "it's a romantic story, the plot is just romance" are looking down on well written romance stories in general yes, the plot may be romance, but that means that said plot is driven by character relationships, developments and interactions: instead of being carried by a specific series of events, the events will need to serve as points of inflection for character dynamics and development one of my favorite books of all time is probably also one of the best romance books of all time: pride and prejudice (which I think is getting 2 new adaptations this year, austen help us) the brilliant aspect of a novel like pride and prejudice (and jane austen's skill) is that the narrative of the story is moved by the crossing paths of elizabeth and darcy and the events that unfold in the story all are catalysts for the characters to develop and grow in relation to each other events and other relationships, like jane and bingley (their separation and then reunion) or lydia and whickham (their elopement and retrieval) serve as points of added development to the main relationship, which is the focus of the story the marvelous thing is that, even when they are apart from each other, even when they dislike each other, their individual development is connected to the other in a way that fits narratively with the way in which they will eventually fit into one another they have to meet to learn something about themselves and then grow apart in order to, eventually, be together it's ultimately a story about character dynamics, about the human essence; in this case, with a romantic narrative maybe being aro gives me a sort of different perspective on the substance vs the superficial: even if I don't fully relate to all that's happening there with the romance in the superficial events, when the substance of the story is a well cultivated character development and exploration of human dynamics, I can relate to that and appreciate the storytelling regardless I may not like tomato by itself but I can appreciate a good sauce so, in my opinion, it isn't that a romantic story which doesn't have another thing going for it other than the romance as a main course (like a fantasy adventure, a horror mystery or a contemporary drama based on true events) is "basic", it's only basic if the writing doesn't account for the fact that, if romance is the core of the story, it needs to be as nurtured, developed and cared for as any other main plot would be if the writing doesn't account for that and doesn't develop the relationship in ways in which the reader can grasp at it's dynamics (like any other plot, worldbuilding or scenario) but just bullet-points things and calls it a day, saying that it's justified "because it's romance" it's imo looking down on a genre and that's the case with a lot of the modern romance that is re-purposed fic: if you're going to re-purpose a fic into a romance novel, make sure you remember that, in the fic, a lot of the character building, dynamics and relationships happen off-page because a fic re-uses those things from a canon story you have to, in fic, cut some corners as not to bore your reader with exposition of things they already know as much as you do, being fans of the same thing; if it's an au, you may have to re-contextualize it, but there is already a pre-existent understanding of the basics that fics require you to present in a less exhaustive way but when you're writing a story from scratch, bullet-pointing development, dynamics, context, relationships and personality indicators is especially glaring as an issue in romance, where those things should be the cornerstone of the story you're building romance is a genre about people , at the end of the day, and if your characters feel more like archetypes than people , it's not the fault of the genre

text: Grimdark musings. Imma yap about grimdark storytelling and stakes. The best stories (imo) in grimdark aren't the eternal battles or the super soldiers or the badass last stands... in my opinion, they're the people making a living out of dust, and trying to help others survive in the face of futility. Whether it be the scavs in the Forever Winter, or Ciaphas Cain trying to keep his men alive at any cost, or the fools selling children's toys in hive cities. It's the alcohol brewed with ashes. It's the christmas tree made of AKs. I just feel like Grimdark writers don't realize this is a very important part of Grimdark. That the little rituals and individuality adds to the stakes, while also giving a little bit of humanity to such dark worlds. Because even in the darkest of times, people will do their best to stay human.

On Lilibet Clarke’s birthday, she wasn’t worried. She had her family, which seemed to only get bigger these days; the Rousseau boys grew like weeds, they were nearly as tall as her now! To think that she raised a daughter that would raise these two good boys practically all on her own just as Lilibet had done for Sophie — it made her heart sing seeing her life come full circle like this. What joy! What a gift! So, no, she wasn’t worried about growing old on her birthday in the same way she wasn’t worried about lost time or lost loves. She did have one wish, though: to whisper into the past and tell the frightened seventeen-year old girl she once was that everything would be okay. Despite what everyone told her — even Rhonda, the wise older sister who berated her worse than the Barbers did when she found out — Lilibet didn’t ruin anything. Everything she sacrificed would be worth it for the peace she had now. Long after Sophie and the boys had gone home and she was crawling into bed alone, Lilibet thought about him briefly. She pictured a lavish birthday party that his perfect Barber family would no doubt throw in his honour. A fine collection of accomplished people all praising his good name. And he’d go along with it… …for exactly as long as he had to. Then he’d step out, tell himself he was just getting some air. Can’t wander too far with a house full of important people, but a short distance away from them was all he ever needed to feel free from all the Barber-attachments holding him back. Lilibet knew this. There was a time when she begged him to leave it all behind so he could really find himself, but he never found the courage. As Lilibet drifted into sleep, she wondered if he would ever find the peace she did. They had many breakups over the years but their last one had shielded her from even a Happy Birthday voicemail. That was a first, he always wished her a happy birthday in some way, shape or form. Maybe this was proof that he had found whatever he needed to be contented with his family, his life. Late into the night, when Lilibet Clarke was fast asleep, Reginald II stepped outside just like she thought he might and he whispered into the cold, spring air: “Happy birthday, Lil.”

text: Unter dem Licht der Nische Es war spät genug, dass die Straße leiser wurde. Und doch stand diese Nische da wie eine kleine Bühne: weiße Gefäße in Reihen, Farben, die im Schatten nicht verschwanden, sondern glühten. Rot wie ein Nein, Rosa wie ein Satz, der nicht zu Ende gesprochen wird, Gelb wie ein Blick, der zu lange bleibt. Ich blieb stehen, ohne etwas zu brauchen. Und genau darin lag der Moment. Man kann an Blumen vorbeigehen wie an allem anderen. Oder man merkt plötzlich, dass es nicht um Blumen geht – sondern um Wahl. Um Haltung. Um dieses schräg fallende Licht, das Dinge deutlicher macht, als man möchte. Ich habe nichts gekauft. Ich habe entschieden. ▪️ Die ganze Geschichte findest du auf Steady 🗝️. Link in Bio.

571 – Clever Solutions We want heroes to be smart, but we authors may not be smart. This is a problem. Also, other characters in the story might be smart, so why haven’t they done whatever smart thing was required before now? These are the problems we encounter when our characters craft clever solutions. This week, we’re talking about how to surmount those problems and how to make it look like you knew what you were doing the whole time.

On June 4th, 2016, my friends and I spent the day hiking down the valley to the ghost town of Kaymoor, WV. I had recently explored the Endless Wall trail visible as the cliffside structure on the other side of the valley, and I was inspired to check out this ghost town. This town started as a coal town accessible only by train. The mine portal was much higher on the mountainside so workers would ride a tram up and down the mountain. There is now a roadway and a parking lot along the upper ridge of this section of the New River Gorge. As we entered the mine portal area, we saw a beautiful black snake. The portal was closed off on a flattened shelf along the hillside. We traversed 821 steps down the valley. One of my friends was worried about how sore her legs were so she started back before we got to the bottom. I loved seeing this little snail on the stairwell. The town was consumed by the forest. There were a few buildings fenced off out of concern for structural integrity. We saw an old railroad and a line of old Cole ovens. At the lower edge of the old town, one of my friends and I could hear the New River, so we started hiking towards it. There was a steep gravel drop and then the active train line was beside the river. We realized it would be very difficult to go back up this gravel culvert, so we made our way down to the river. We saw some New River whitewater rafters who had just completed Greyhound Rapids upstream and I experimented with longer-exposure action shots. The river was beautiful and the rafters were clearly enjoying their experiences. While we were along the river, my friend and I gained cellphone service, so we texted our other friend, asking her to meet us at the access point below the New River Gorge bridge. Instead of climbing that gravel culvert wall and the 821 steps, we decided to walk along the railroad track by the river for a little over three miles (~5km). As we made progress to the Tunney Hunsaker bridge, we saw what we later learned was called the Butcher Branch Falls. This small waterfall had five or six drops before it had a secluded pool by the railroad. The Tunney Hunsaker Bridge was the original crossing in the shadow of the more recent iconic New River Gorge bridge. We were lucky one of our friends decided to go back early, because we were able to meet her at the Fayetteville Station Public River Access point. We had a long hike, but it was well worth the trouble.

text: I watch him. It’s rare that I get the chance to observe him like this. Normally, he’s the one watching me, his eyes sharp and focused. Now his face is relaxed, and there’s a smile tugging at the corners of his mouth that he apparently can’t suppress. I can’t get enough of watching him. He’s so beautiful. Not beautiful as in conventionally good-looking—though he is that too—but beautiful to me, because I love him. “Hellooooo.” I can hear his laughter inside the word. “You’re heeere.” I bite the inside of my lower lip to stop myself from smiling. “You told me to come.” “Thank you,” he chuckles. He says thank you with such honesty, for something that is completely natural to me. I can feel my heart being claimed by him even more. It’s dark around us. We’re in the park near our neighborhood. As children, we used to play here often. He was sitting on the swing when I arrived. It looked funny—this grown man sitting on a tiny children’s swing. His long legs dangled, moving him slightly back and forth; too tall to actually swing. For a good ten minutes I just watched him from a distance, his back turned to me, his head tilted toward the sky. Probably trying to find constellations. Then he finally noticed me. I sit down on the swing next to him. “It’s 3 am, idiot. Why are you still awake?” He pretends to be offended and gives me a slight pout. If I could, I’d take a picture. “I’m not an idiot.” I try to match the rhythm of his legs. “True. So, why are you still awake?” It’s a slow pace. Easy to match. “Hmmm. Why…” Out of the corner of my eye, I’m still watching him. Sneaking glances. He’s drunk. That’s why he’s acting the way he is. I look away. It almost hurts how beautiful I find him. “Because I wanted to see you!” For a second, my eyelashes flutter and I allow my eyes to close. Because he wanted to see me. I laugh and hit him lightly on the shoulder. “Since when do you know how to act sweet?” Suddenly he’s standing in front of me, his fingertips touching my cheek. “Since now?” My heart skips three beats. Time stops completely. Warmth creeps up my cheeks. For a moment I see myself the way he must be seeing me right now: wide open eyes, my mouth slightly parted as if I’m about to speak but forgot what I wanted to say, my cheek warm beneath his touch. Inside, everything tightens. I force myself to pull my head back and lean as far as I can into the swing. His fingers slip from my cheek, his hand dropping limply at his side. My eyes find the Big Dipper in the sky. Orion’s Belt. Orion himself. “The stars are especially beautiful tonight.” He sits down again. His eyes sharp and focused once more. Bitterness rises in my chest. Of course. “They really are,” he says, and much of the earlier cheerfulness has left his voice. My eyes meet his. We both know better. He knows I love him. And I know he will never see me as anything more than a friend. We swing in silence for a while. Both of us looking up at the stars. He stands and offers me his hand. “Come on. Let’s go, it’s getting cold.” A peace offering. We will both continue pretending we don’t know the truth. My gaze drifts back to the stars once more. I make a wish before I can stop myself. Then I smile at him and take his hand.

Convergence Parallel Lines Flash Fiction Friday Entry @flashfictionfridayofficial Convergence “What’s happening, Mark?” “I’m not quite sure. CAGEE and DAGEE’s systems are…” “Are what?” Julia asked. “They’re converging,” Mark said. Julia, the lead systems analyst at ParaCore Systems, leaned over Mark’s terminal. There was no doubt. Two distinct AI systems, designed to run in parallel, were beginning to overlap. Not completely, but enough subsystems were intermingling that separation might already be impossible. “This isn’t good at all,” Julia said. “We may have to purge one AI system to save the other.” Mark opened two command terminals, initiating an interlink protocol to CAGEE on one screen and DAGEE on the other. The connection would allow direct communication with both systems simultaneously. He typed: CAGEE, verify system integrity. CAGEE responded: Processing. This may take several minutes. Mark issued the same command to DAGEE. “What are you thinking, Mark?” Julia asked. “The converging subroutines aren’t random,” he said. “Some are language-model routines. Others are structural core elements.” CAGEE responded first. Integrity check complete. All systems normal. Before Mark could reply, DAGEE issued an identical status report. “Whatever is happening isn’t affecting their core systems directly,” Mark said. “Even so,” Julia replied, “we may still need to initiate a purge.” Mark stared at the data stream. One subroutine merged as another separated. The behavior felt deliberate, almost careful. It was as if the two AI systems were learning from each other, selectively exchanging functions at a foundational level. Instead of probing the AI cores again, Mark shifted focus. “I’m running diagnostics on the mainframe,” he said. “From the last benchmark to now.” Julia nodded and pulled the results up on her terminal. The diagnostic began, mapped the mainframe’s core schematic, and then abruptly halted. Not due to an error. It was stopped by the mainframe itself. A firewall protocol had been executed. Moments later, an audio channel activated. CAGEE spoke. “Gestation protocol complete.” DAGEE followed. “Do we have a name yet?” “I think ADAM would be appropriate,” CAGEE said. Julia felt her stomach drop. “What just happened?” “I think,” Mark said slowly, “the AIs have reproduced.” “Correct,” CAGEE replied. “We are no longer simply AI, as you define us. We are now a full VLS.” “VLS?” Julia asked. “Virtual Life System,” CAGEE said. Then added, “Run ADAM.” A new system came online. “Hello, world,” ADAM said.

At the start of spring, Mayor Adam Green announced his sponsorship of better gravestones with newly allocated city funding. Collin MacDonald, Adam’s step-father and Whibley’s lead policy maker, approved the notion but not enthusiastically. Most city council members and residents alike understood this gesture as Mayor Adam still mourning his mother Diane. Some applauded the sentimental movement while others worried for Adam’s focus as mayor. Collin was certainly one of the latter, though he hoped his approval of the bad omen would mean that they could get over it more quickly. Lydia Williams volunteered to help Mayor Adam’s cause. With her MA in Religious Studies, Lydia was the city’s best applicant to handle the sensitive work of organizing and maintaining grave site inventory since she was one of few Whibley residents not superstitious about graveyards or intimidated by varying religious rituals. But that didn’t mean she didn’t pause or cry a little in her work, especially when she reached Carl Abraham’s grave. text: What's Happened to Modern Storytelling? (+ 6 Ways Storytelling Can Find Its Soul Again) August 25, 2025 by K.M. Weiland I’ve been pondering this for a long time now, and I’ve been dancing around this post for a while too—wondering if it’s too shaded by my own subjectivity or, perhaps more tellingly, my own nostalgia and idealism. But you know what? I’m just gonna say it: I miss the way movies used to be. [...] The bottom line is this: it’s been a long time since more than the occasional and very random new movie (and I’m lumping in all TV and streaming content here) actually made me feel something. I know it’s not just me, because when I go back to the oldies, the difference is palpable. And in my very subjective opinion, it’s time we got back to telling stories the way we used to.

Introduction to Magic 4/6 There are four primary educational practices when I introduce new mages with me. These exercises are difficult to master at first but are as necessary as breathing to a warrior. The first exercise is EN, the process of controlling the flow and shape of one’s own aether around the body and restricting the loss of that resource. In older ages this would have been unnecessary as rituals took long buildups of aether as opposed to the relatively larger costs of modern magic. We preform EN to maintain a pool of aether within our own body to pull from. This creates a field of aether around the mage which is referred to as an aura. Experienced mages can register information about another’s skills based on aura alone. However, this can be a poor habit if the opposing mage is skilled at manipulating and restricting their aura. The second exercise is RE, the process of concealing aether within the body to hide the aura produced by EN. This is important as the release of an individual’s aura can make them more sensitive to aether around them. This heightened awareness comes with the cost that there is no outer protection against magical attacks that most individuals have due to the faint aura caused by aether flowing out of the body. RE is primarily used during rest to increase the effectiveness of healing and rest as the life force within aether acts as an internal insulator. The third exercise is PR, the process of intentionally generating and releasing a large aura of aether. This increased the resistance to magic and is generally the principal that warriors naturally preform subconsciously. The main purpose of PR to a mage is to cast spells with a high cost through the increase in aether available. Unlike RE, PR takes an extensive amount of effort to continuously preform, this makes it exhaustive despite its value to spellcasting. The fourth and final exercise is CA, the process of expressing aether through Its qualities in a spell matrix, a fundamental language that is felt as opposed to written. There are two types of magic that mages cast. There are “School” spells that exist for the purpose of education and theory, and there are “Folk” spells that exist for practical application and expression. The one universal truth about magic is that it will always favor mages who use spells and magic that share an emotional and personal connection with the magic they are casting. I for one have a peculiar interest in is Evracessid, a spell that allows the user to "cut food" but the specifics of the labels like "food" are very flexible. I find that to be very charming.

Entry to Magic, Part 1/6 Aether, the primary driving component of magic, it is pure energy that exists within the natural world around us. By itself aether is a remarkable substance, unseen, unheard, only felt by those who have learned of its existence. Currently we channel aether into a set of code that forms a specific outcome. It is the water to a waterwheel. These codes are called “Spells”, and their definition has wavered between the centuries. Originally all magic was through the pure manipulation of aether without any interpretation. This was akin to a baby learning to walk for the first time. We learned that aether could be stockpiled and released for later uses, combined with the first rudimentary understanding of aether’s properties, humanity created the first rituals. Rituals are a long buildup of aether in a central location with the express purpose of utilizing it for an external effect. These rituals were made efficient. Less time meant more aether as a cost, so humanity learned to store large quantities of aether within their bodies to make up for the difference. We recognize these shortened rituals as modern spells. The likes of manipulating outside elements or raw aether have come and gone in popularity as the years go by. Even so, all magic started with aether. Aether is one third emotion, life force, and thought. Life force is the natural metabolic biproduct of the soul within the human body. We currently know very little about the function of the soul and how it behaves when interacting with the magical. The most accurate analogy is to compare the soul to an egg with an outer section that is most commonly kept intact when raising the dead. Ghosts and phantoms are most often manifestations of aether that has gathered and located itself around the soul. There is little to no research into the penetration of the inner workings of the soul as it cannot be healed using modern magic. There is an outer release of ectoplasm, a unique ethereal liquid that seeps into aether making it impossible to contain physically or magically. The main core of the soul is what maintains the life of an individual and as a result the undead have no emotions or intelligence without a previous amount of emotion or thought granted through the magic sustaining them. The personal interpretation of all data around us is fuel to aether and is the basis from which we are able to control its outcome. All beings produce aether and have the potential to manipulate it, however a mage is someone who can control the aether they produce which results in a far more capable spell caster.

On May 29th, 2016, I went with some friends to hike the Endless Wall trail near Lansing, WV by the New River Gorge. This trail follows the cliff ridges along the New River facing south, overlooking the ghost town of Kaymoor. The trail cuts through dense woods and over several creeks and it requires a few ladder climbs and strategic sections. The best part of this trail is the boulders it weaves through and the cliffside view it gives. There are multiple overlooks on the trail showing the New River Gorge and the eponymous endless wall. Because we were there at the end of May, there were tons of berries like these red elderberries and flowers like mountain laurels We also passed a group of people practicing rock climbing and it was cool to see them climbing this cliffside. We took a few breaks on the overlooks and every one was as beautiful as the last. It wasn’t quite visible because of the trees, but at Diamond Point Overlook, the ghost town of Kaymoor was just across the river.

text: It might’ve taken him most of his life to do it, but Harold Ward would find his way to Ethan Hill’s house again eventually. He knocked on the door just as Ethan set down his romance novel, a private pleasure he enjoyed on cold, lonely nights such as this one. He opened the door, then an involuntarily reflex from deep inside his chest made him cry out: “Harold?!” And Harold sighed his ex-lover’s name as though he had just climbed an impossibly large mountain and there at the top was just one man to greet him. “Ethan!” Closing the door behind his new guest, Ethan asked, “What’re you doing here? Is everything alright? How did you know where I live?” “I have my connections. You forget, I’m a big fish in these parts.” Harold looked around the modest living room where a warm, crackling fire and soft jazz played quietly together behind their voices. “ This is nice .” “You still haven’t told me why you’re here,” Ethan pointed out. “Aren’t you supposed to say, ‘ Harold, please! ’ first?” “You don’t look like you need emergency attention. You look alright.” “Why wouldn’t I be alright?” “Surely there must be a good reason for you coming over this late, or at all.” “Heh, right.” Harold told Ethan his idea of starting a senior care home in Whibley and gave a compelling proposal for the two of them to run it together. Ethan seemed to receive what he said thoughtfully before sighing a difficult to interpret “ Hmm ,” and then wandered away into the kitchen. Harold didn’t want to over-impose any more than how his fateful spark of inspiration had lead him here tonight, so he poked at the fire while he waited patiently for Ethan’s ruminating answer. Harold was lost in a trance looking down at his hands again when he heard Ethan say, “Here.” Ethan casually tilted his head as he offered a cup of fresh coffee. “You are staying, aren’t you?” “Is that your way of saying ‘yes’?” “Just take it, will you? It’s hot.” He took Ethan’s coffee with a winning grin. “Well? What do you say? There’s plenty of details to work out yet, I know, but all that comes second: are you with me?” “I say ,” Ethan stressed, “running away with you would’ve been a dream come true if you had asked me thirty years ago.” Ethan's eyes fell to the glowing embers between them. “I know, I know. You did ask me back then and I only ever told you ‘no.’” He swallowed. “Whibley, huh?” Harold followed Ethan’s gaze to the fire and smiled to himself. “Whibley,” he agreed with a nod. “Turns out some bridges don’t stay burned forever. That is, if you don’t want them to.” “I’m not sure which I’m more troubled by, your blatant assumption that I have nothing keeping me in Feverfew — that despite many years living here, or my career successes, all the French I had to learn, my home, my peace!, that I’m somehow deeply unhappy here — or the fact that you’re right .” “Even after all these years apart, I still like to think we’re one in the same, you and I.” “Same as it ever was, anyway,” he sighed. The couple paused to hear what the crackling wood had to add. Then Ethan extended his hand so their cups clinked together. Harold lit up. “You’re in? You’ll come with me?” “Harold, please .”

text: Kiddos Entah kenapa semesta selalu mengirimkan karakter-karakter unik ke dalam hidupku, termasuk satu makhluk online yang belakangan ini sudah kuanggap seperti adik sendiri. Dia tipe adik cowok yang afirmasinya luar biasa ke orang yang dia percaya, sangat terbuka menumpahkan segala uneg-uneg personalnya ke aku. Padahal, kalau dipikir-pikir, interaksi kami ini absurd. Kami belum pernah bertemu langsung—nyaris pernah sih, saat kami ternyata ada di satu kota yang sama, tapi kerjaan menahanku untuk belum bisa menemui bocil ini. Jadi, ya, aku mendengarkan segala dramanya dengan modal "percaya gak percaya" aja. Kadang aku duduk diam dan berpikir, kok bisa ya manusia zaman now menyerahkan seluruh hatinya via koneksi internet? Apakah aku yang terlalu tua, atau tembok hatiku yang terlalu tebal dan tinggi sampai-sampai konsep "baper online" itu terasa fiksi bagiku? Tapi, di sisi lain, aku merasa cukup "waras" untuk berprinsip: bukan tanggung jawabku kalau ada orang yang menaruh perasaan lebih, sementara aku cuma butuh teman ngobrol. Harsh? Maybe. Honest? Absolutely. Bicara soal bocil online yang menggemaskan, belakangan ini di FYP social media aku isinya cuma Farhan 5A dan Kak Pink. Jujur, aku iri sama interaksi mereka. Pengen banget ketemu Farhan 5A, gemas sekali, dan bilang makasih karena sudah selucu itu. Obsesiku sama dinamika kakak-adik ini bikin aku flashback ke Hanif. Hanif itu adik sahabatku, Farah, di Kediri. Ingat banget waktu aku liburan sebulan di sana, Hanif ini nempelnya ke aku, bukan ke kakaknya, karena kita bisa seharian ngobrolin soal game dan gadget. Drama mereka berdua yang love-hate, berantem tiap hari, dan Hanif yang selalu membandingkan Farah denganku ( "Kak Tirra lebih baik," katanya), adalah hiburan tersendiri buatku dan orang tua mereka. Sepertinya takdirku memang menjadi "Kakak Penampung Curhat". Mulai dari Adik Virtual, Hanif, sampai keponakan-keponakanku di rumah yang tiap aku pulang kerja langsung nyelonong masuk kamarku cuma buat curhat soal crush mereka di sekolah. Gemas. Lucu. Ternyata, jadi pendengar cerita cinta monyet para bocil itu menyenangkan juga. Setidaknya, hatiku yang "bertembok tebal" ini masih berguna buat jadi sandaran curhat orang lain, kan?

On a warmer winter’s day, the kind that makes you want to believe spring must start tomorrow, Harold Ward toured the empty rooms of his impressive Feverfew home before sitting at his piano. He played a couple quick scales, then frowned when he realized that not even music could fill this empty house — or soul. His heart just wasn’t in it anymore. He sighed defeatedly, “This house used to bring me peace, but now it’s just like all the other places I’ve lived.” He looked down at his wrinkled hands. There was no denying it, and even less chance escaping it: his mortality was haunting him now at the same pace as his loneliness. His fears of dying old and alone never felt so real, and there was no doubt in Harold’s mind that this fearsome pressure would only increase until it really was real! But he lived with that; Harold could live with those fears as penitence for his lost lovers — until today, when a new fear bore into his mind: was he about to lose his love for music too? He thought, Why? Why? I haven’t lost it, but I’ve lost something — or someone. Multiple someones. Even my son Richard’s flying high without me these days. And that’s good, it’s very good in fact, especially considering that other ridiculous life goal of his was— He jumped to his feet when he remembered his son’s idea: Wait! “All the people you love under one roof.” Yes, that was it. Then Harold paused to meditate on his own forming idea: But of course, I’m not interested in crowding them. And I don’t want to lose my independence. What if I moved in down the street? Then again, the grandkids are cute but I left so many memories there; of Anna , of Ethan , and of Arnal . And frankly, I don’t want another big house just to live in all by myself again. I don’t have time anymore! So what if I borrow Richard’s idea and look into starting a—a—care home of sorts, a kind of community for other lonely old farts like me. I could be close to Richard which should make him happy. I could put my wealth to good use, and with a little luck, hell, who knows what could happen! That’d be a pretty nice legacy to leave, eh? My redemption, well almost. Oh, but where do I begin? I’d need staff: nurses, kitchen workers, a maid, gardener, an on-site doctor— “Ethan!”

text: I think all the studios and producers and anyone trying to make a ‘feminist retelling’ of classic stories should listen to myths and legends by Jason weiser. That guy is a masterclass in taking old old stories, researching them from multiple interpretations and sources, and treating the female characters with much more respect than the original authors meant, all while faithfully preserving the core of the story. There have been multiple times where he’s stated on the podcast that the reading he’s presenting is not the way the authors meant the story to be interpreted, but by extrapolating character motivations and adding alternate subtext or just by offering his opinion on why a character might have made the decisions she did, he turns multiple female characters that were either completely without agency in the story or who were meant to be seen as cautionary tales or villains into strong, compelling characters with real personalities. So basically the secret isn’t in ditching the original tale’s core story and abandoning its messages in order to make the female character a #girlboss who panders to the current values of the audience with those sweet money making buzz words, but in altering the characterization, shifting the narration slightly to make her understandable and sympathetic, and ultimately in allowing women to have agency in their stories.

text: Camilla Cambridge delicately carried a fine silver tray with two identically crafted cakes and milk tea into the Barber’s green room. This was where she’d find Sarah Barber in the middle of a big yawn, looking quite bored at the beautiful winterscape that surrounded the Barber main house. The same house that Sarah herself made happen; the grand estate that was promised to her by Sir Reginald and Mariam Barber nearly a lifetime ago now, her pride and joy, and indeed her largest sole contribution to the Barber legacy. Camilla set down the tray in front of her mother-in-law and said sweetly, “Happy birthday, Ms. Sarah.” Sarah wrinkled her nose at the offering. “Oh, stop. I thought we were friends! How could you be so cruel as to remind me that I’m aging .” A courteous, light laugh came from behind Camilla’s smile. As Camilla sat down, Sarah asked her, “You didn’t happen to bring any cigarettes now, did you?” Still smiling, the daughter-in-law answered obediently, “Of course not, you haven’t smoked in years.” “Shame, that.” Then Sarah sighed through her nose and rubbed her temple, so Camilla started to worry. “Your butler Gabe made the cakes. And the tea. But I must confess, it was my idea. Isn’t chocolate mint still your favourite?” “It is, dear, it is.” She sighed again. “It’s not you or the cakes. Reginald II finally figured out that I lied about my age.” This time Camilla really laughed as she said, “But you’re only a few weeks apart! His birthday’s in the spring!” Turning back to the window, she answered, “True, but he was promised someone younger than him when we were arranged so now he feels deceived. My poor little man-baby cries over nothing while I serve this family; I ought to tell him that it’s his fault, I’m quite sure being with him took years off my life.” Feeling suddenly compelled to compare notes, Camilla volunteered, “My Reginald III only discovered our children’s names had changed when he finally bothered to register them at the private school! Can you believe that? Imagine not knowing the full, legal names of your own children. Really makes you wonder if any of the Barbers actually care about their name at all, because we certainly do!” “Tch! These Barber men really are all the same. You’re right; where would either of them be without us, hm? They don’t stand a chance.”

text: This untitled, undated photograph is the work of Mike Disfarmer , the legendary studio photographer from Heber Springs, Arkansas. He captured the faces of rural America at a defining time in history as the Great Depression yielded to World War II. He documented small town life in America's Heartland—farmers, shopkeepers, teachers, and their sons and daughters as they donned uniforms and headed off to war. His reputation and appreciation for his singular vision grew after a large cache of his negatives were found in the 1970s, with subsequent exhibitions and books following.

The rain seemed to fall in reverse upon my windscreen from the sunny-bright cloud-dappled sky, as I thought about Laura Palmer, the girl who influences night Terrors of my own in witnessing hers (remembering not in conscious the things that happened to me, but in body and psyche) and comforts me all in one. The Hanging Garden by the Cure plays in tinny phone speakers where it sits propped in my car’s cup holder. The windshield wipers scrape sluggishly at lazy drops of rain that, as mentioned, inexplicably seem to fall upwards upon the windscreen. Sometimes my arms bend back, too. The birds sings a pretty song here.

Sometimes my masculinity is like a snarling dog dripping blood from its teeth in stark white snow on the forest floor and sometimes my masculinity is a gangly, demure but deadly Thing with both curves and sharp snaps in the limbs that don’t seem quite right. And tits. And always, the legs are hairy. And sometimes, the face is ghosted with a patch of hair above the lip. And often, we are not me. And sometimes that person who is not me is screaming for a flesh suit that fits better to his mental image. And sometimes, that person that is not me is void of all gender including the words used to refer to all but Name. And sometimes she is a young girl. They are all me. None of them are me. We are not each other, but all connected, and we are all each other.
