text: growing up, i spent every friday at my nana and grandpa’s house. they have a farm, with some cows, and there was always a huge garden. they had a dog named tippy who is in one of my first real memories, and i smile thinking to when she was a puppy and was being lightly scolded for peeing on the kitchen floor. grandpa’s tractor is what mom calls the putt-putt. it’s old and squeaky and doesn’t always work, but give the engine a few smacks and she’ll be up and running. he’d attatch the wagon and take me and my sisters into the woods with him to grab logs he’d cut, singing all the way. nana always had the best treats. the only pancakes better than moms are hers. she also gives great hugs and the best good-night kisses. they are old, my grandparents. he’s 88 in remission and she’s 83 with parkinsons. we’re on borrowed time. but no matter what, i wouldn’t trade nana fridays for the world. there was nothing better than waking up and knowing you’d spend the whole day being loved and learning to love what you work for.

“to combat the resistances of language you must keep talking” –Anne Carson I write most everyday. Since the end of last August, I have filled up two 150-page notebooks, completed close to 80 short poems. I have written, if not so obsessively as now, since I was 15. I write poetry, with the occasional venture into essays like this one. I have trouble with narrative, one event leading into another befuddles me, as does conversation between people. So I do not write fiction. Yet, I do have an interior running commentary on the narrative I am living, snipes and admonitions on my life as it unfolds. To push back against this cruel eviscerating voice, which adheres tightly within my skin, I write. I write to explain the world to myself, to explain myself to myself, to resist the world, which is lain upon me by the world. I write to resist the temptation to settle into myself without a thought. I am uncomfortable in most social situations. It’s discomforting when others try to define me, or attempt to interpret me from my writing. Yes, I am aware that all writer’s expose their minds in their writing. Even writers of fiction expose themselves through their fictional characters. Nietzsche wrote that in the end we only experience ourselves. Yet, I believe there is also a separation from oneself, a leap into the universal other, which occurs when one writes: a transubstantiation of individuality into a larger third person narrator, who watches and observes with more objective, more just, eye. Of course, I also know this is pure bullshit. I am as clotted with my biases and situation as anyone. But it is through writing, through the transformative nature of writing, where a third space can open, and one can enter along with whomever can follow into a changed world, a different, perhaps better place, if only for the time it takes to read the poem. And to keep from being defined, trapped even in these new spaces, I continue to write, to find a way to exist with myself. (February 28, 2017)

One thing I really like about the way @asexualjournal is run is that even though the whole volume comes out at one time, the editor spotlights a different article with a different pull-out quote every week so that it stays top of mind and can be easier to digest. The other week I saw a quote that ignited my curiosity, that the “right person” is the right person for a reason . Yes, I thought, this is an idea I’d like to read about. This is something that could really be fleshed out, could be a pithy way of pushing back on one of the most frequent responses we get. The article was a first-person account of an awkward situation where the narrator is trapped in a car with a driver who bulldozes over the author without any thought given to the idea that her idea of a good relationship and the author’s might just be different. I want to be clear: it wasn’t a bad article. As I was reading it, though, the penny finally dropped for me why it is I don’t actually read much of The Asexual , why I am more and more frequently disappointed with Carnival of Aces entries, why Twitter makes me so frustrated. I read and I read and I get to the end and think, “what’s the point?” I know the answer, though. They are recounting this experience to have others say, “yes, that happened to me too.” To make an environment where others can say, “I recognize this feeling.” The point is for the author to be seen. --Wait, let me back up. Last spring I watched all of my man Gerald on Shortland Street . No, I don’t mean I re-watched for the umpteenth time the bits that have been up thanks to Ace Hero SootmouthNZ for 8+ years. I mean I watched every second he was on the screen. It started as an offhand comment to my girlfriend--who of course had no idea it even existed--but as I was going through collecting the links, I noticed how all of the episodes of Shortland Street are on YouTube now. But like often happens to me, a whim turns into an obsession. I was watching late into the night for weeks, going to work every day exhausted and not quite sure what I was doing. It was interesting to see the bits both before and after the “Asexuality Storyline” vids, especially because I was vaguely aware at how mad people were at the time over the “gay representation” being pulled out from under them, but also because after asexuality-as-a-plot ended, he was still around. Being a person. It was right here that I realized why I felt the need to go back to this show for the umpteenth time right at this point in my life: I was trying to be in a relationship as an asexual person for the first time, and I needed to see it could be done. Even though I’d been over it again and again in my own fiction, someone else’s fiction made it more tangible. Someone else’s character standing in a kitchen ripping into a homophobe--I could take courage from that. I know what it’s like to need to see, and I know what it’s like to be seen, but. But. When I was in 3rd grade, I lived just down the street from my school; Google Maps tells me it was 10 houses away. Because it was a side entrance, there were rarely any people or cars around, especially when I was walking home. But if I heard a car coming, I would run and hide behind one of the big oleander bushes, braving the bees over a stranger’s eyes. Now that I’m an adult, that’s still very much the case. I’ll fumble my key into the lock in panic if I hear a neighbor’s footsteps or hide behind a column until a respectable distance is between me and a coworker leaving the office at the same time. I’ve even attempted (without complete success) to banish my family from the kitchen if I’m cooking because even having them in the same room is too distracting. I don’t want to be seen. I come from an internet age where you never put up your real name or picture, and then an age after that where revealing yourself made you open to attack. Yet, I still do write incredibly personal things on the internet. And I’ve moved more and more towards the “personal narrative” to do it. I’ve done it because it’s safer in a way to tell one’s own story on one’s own terms. Instead of being told, for example, that if only I knew any ace women who dated women, I would totally agree that my ideas were wrong. Instead of watching people tell stories of how they personally were attacked with bear repellent and therefore you should trust them when they say asexuality is homophobic. Instead of couching thoughts of one’s own masculinity in removed language and then having male voices completely miss that crucial detail when responding. It tears at me to do so, but I rend myself in order to sew the pieces together in a new way, that gives form--makes meaning. That means someone coming after me might be able to put them on and be protected or empowered . My history in the asexual community is almost turning into a barrier for me. With my girlfriend, I had so many things I needed to discuss, so many options and ideas. We sat down one night with @queenieofaces ​’s Five Factor Model open on my laptop, but how do you have a relationship discussion with someone when you’ve got 10 years’ head start on the nuances of things like what infidelity is, or what a non-romantic couple looks like--especially when those things are so subjective and also who says you’re right ? But, you know, she hadn’t been involved because she hadn’t seen the point. “ Same hat!! ” is nice, and sometimes important, even life-changing, but it only gets you so far. And yet, while I struggle with this, I see people on twitter say things like “people keep telling us to go make our own community. Maybe we will!” and I think, where have you been thus far? I think, what was the point of letting myself be seen? Being in this community is not going to stop hurting me, but I’ve got enough maturity and experience and perspective to know where my limits are. This community is important to me, even if it means I have to take my own actual face to a pride parade or send my own actual name to LinkedIn connections and government agencies and Japanese city councilors . It’s worth it. It’s worth it but--in the rush of “visibility visibility” maybe keep in mind what it costs. The other day @grison-in-space ​ reblogged a post about metafandom, and it made me feel some... complicated feelings. On the one hand, I do miss metafandom ( and not because people there cared about what I had to say about asexuality for some reason ), but on the other, I wonder, how would it work? That phase of the internet is over, and you can’t go back; it’s folly to try and go back. Today is today, not yesterday or 2011 or 2007. And what I notice is the desperate need to be seen: face and haircut and name and story. And that visibility is leverage now--a Boomer got mad at me in May for refusing to put up a selfie for a political cause. You win hearts and minds by standing in front of someone and saying, “Here I am. I am a person. Will you scorn me now that you’ve seen my face?” It’s powerful, and I recognize the power. I’m trying to do better, fight better, be more open with those who already know my face and name. That wears at me too. But you know, even as I crave that meaning, I can see that just being seen is enough for some people. And I have eyes. I’ll watch. I see you. I see all of you. It’s the least I can do ... isn’t it ?

The Day My Life Changed- A Personal Essay "My life changed on December 2013, on a cold winter night, when my dad was in a horrible state of mind." It's about time I share a little bit more of myself with you. I don't know you, but maybe this essay I wrote in high school will inspire you. I left a lot of details out of my essay because I wanted to make sure I was remembering what happened correctly and presenting it in the most positive way. I tend to remember details out of order. When I was in school I needed to write a personal narrative written in chronological order. At the time I was worried I wouldn't pass my exam. I wasn't ready until now to share my story. I don't feel under pressure now so I feel like I can share all the extra details with you. Note: Tap on the picture (or click if on a computer) to read it. The picture looks blurry to me. Maybe it is just my device. Please let me know if you cannot read it for some reason. Before I Left: Before I left, I remember that the yelling got progressively worse. My dad was not in his right mind. My mom was concerned for me and my three siblings. At My Mom's Friend's House: I can remember feeling extremely sad and angry that I was away from home. I wanted things to go back to the way they always went, but at the time I didn't realize that it was insane for me to want that. I wanted to please my dad and pretend everything was fine. I didn't know any better. I was at the point in my life where I didn't care if I would live or die. My mind was in a state of fear, depression, and anxiety almost all the time. I was completely broken down. I hated myself. I believed things would not get better, ever. Despite the strong feelings I had God turned my life around so I could see that he had better plans for me. We left the house unexpectedly so eventually I needed to go back and get more clothing. I also needed to take a shower. I didn't know how long I was going to stay at my mom's friends house. I didn't want to be there. I really wanted to go home. When we went back to the house, it was snowing. The police also escorted us back to the house just in case my dad came back when we were getting more clothing. I also went back to shower. He didn't come back while we were doing what we needed to do. I made sure I got the clothing quickly. I probably showered quickly. I must have stayed away from home over a week. Added Facts: When I eventually went back my dad was moving out of the house. Just in case I wasn't clear in the essay. That was the only way we could go back. In the essay I didn't mention when my mom put me into counseling, I was extremely angry at my mom. Despite how I felt I learned to trust my mom and the counselors. It was very important for me to get help. My mom knew what I needed. Now I see how much it helped me. I am a different person now. Also because my mom listened to God, I am alive. Well, that is what I believe. Word Of Advice- Please Be Careful: I also want to take a moment to tell you that if you know someone, or you are that someone who is in a situation where whoever you live with is not in their right mind and you need to get out, do not tell your friends or family where you are going. The reason I say no is because if your friends and family know and they tell the person who is not ok where you are if they are angry enough, they could end up killing you and the people you are staying with. I just thought of that. Thank you for reading! God bless!

A/N: This was a personal narrative that I wrote when I was hospitalized for depression in April. I still suffer from depression, but I would like everyone to know beforehand that I have no intention of harming myself and have all the right resources to get myself help if I need it. Warning: Graphic descriptions of suicidal thoughts Left or right. Such a simple decision. Seemingly. A simple decision with huge, life-changing, life- ending implications. If I go right, I die. If I go left, I live. Should be simple, but isn’t. No one would hesitate to pick left. Picking life seems easy. But for me, standing there in the gray morning with the slight chill of spring and people rushing around me to their classes, it was the most difficult decision of my life. I wanted to die. I wanted to die so badly it was almost the only thing in my head, an idea that had grown so large it had hardly left room for anything else. It’d been there since the night before, since I’d gotten into the shower and turned the water so hot it burned. Burning myself was better than cutting myself, which was something I found myself wanting to do. I didn’t know why. I’d had a relatively good, accomplished day. Maybe it was the inevitability of Monday, of an oral presentation I felt ill-prepared for. Maybe it was the pain throbbing right through my core, that constant ache that had been there for almost four years and would seemingly never go away. Somehow I went through the routine of cleaning myself. I felt lethargic, uncaring. So what if I was clean? So what if I was dirty? So what if the water was so hot it turned my skin red? I could barely feel it. I finished, turned the water off, just stood there for a moment, listening to the water dripping down the shower stall, the voices of other people in the hallway, music from the men’s bathroom as someone else showered. I felt overwhelmed, felt like life was drowning me. I was in a river, floundering, trying to escape, and life was standing large and strong and oppressive over me, holding me under the water with two hands and laughing cruelly. I couldn’t escape. I wrung the water out of my hair, grabbed my towel to dry off. As I pressed it to my face I thought of how nice it would be if it could just smother me, stop my breathing, stop my heart, stop my suffering. Tears stung at my eyes, trailed down my face to be soaked up into the towel. I was holding my breath for some reason, but then I released it in a loud, desperate sob. Luckily, I was alone in the bathroom. No one heard me crying, and if passerby in the hallway did, they didn’t come and check on me. I was alone with my sorrow, my struggle. I dried off, all the while wondering if I would die if I just tied the towel around my neck tight enough, or the sash from my bathrobe. But no. Then they would find me naked, and just as cold as the tiles beneath my feet. I couldn’t let that happen. I put on my bathrobe, tied the sash around my waist instead of my neck, and went back to my room. One of my roommates wasn’t there, and the other had her back turned to me, watching a show on her laptop. The lights were dim. Even if she did look at me, I didn’t know if she would have been able to tell how distraught I was. At least I’d stopped crying. I got in my pajamas, turned the lights off, got into bed, and reached for my phone. I had a text from my sister saying that she felt okay after the date she’d been nervous for, but that had been almost twenty minutes ago. I texted back that that was good. I waited, hoping for the little bubble that would mean that she was typing. Nothing. That was to be expected. It was almost midnight. I was alone. Just me and my thoughts. My thoughts were hell. I curled into a ball against them, as if I was being attacked from the outside instead of the inside. There was a black hole inside my stomach, sucking, consuming, trying to turn me into nothing. I wanted to be nothing. I wanted to stop existing. I wanted to die, but I couldn’t die by just laying there. I had to take action. No. No, I couldn’t. My death would obliterate my sister, break my already grieving dad, destroy my mom. It would ruin my friends, anyone that ever loved me. My pets would never see me again and would never know why. And I… I didn’t care. I loved them, loved them so deeply, but that love went into the black hole. I didn’t care. That was new for me. Those thoughts usually helped to battle against the ones of suicide. I went on. Who would finish my stories? Who would write the books that were waiting inside my head? No one. And again, I couldn’t find it in myself to care. That scared me. I grew desperate, sought out the little things I enjoyed about life. Music: the sweet glide of a bow across strings, the entrance of the bass, the chords that hit that sweet spot in my ears that practically makes me shudder and my eyes roll back. Art: the scratch of a pencil on paper, the deep focus of work, the realistic black and white eyes staring back at me from a page, daring me to finish the rest of the face, the accomplishment of being done and having something good to look at. Food: the sweet taste of pancakes, my favorite, the sizzling of a pan as I cooked, something I loved to do, the smell of garlic permeating every room as my mom cooked an Italian dinner, my dad’s home fries, the best in the world, my sister’s sweet, decadent backing. Nature: the feel of the breeze, the rustle of it through leaves and branches and long grass, the feeling of sunlight, the thrill of watching the clouds rise into a formidable thunderstorm. Life. Breathing. Existing. Thinking. Feeling. Living. I didn’t care. I didn’t care, I didn’t care, I didn’t care. Life wasn’t just good things. Life was pain. Constant. Pain that only the strongest of pills could help, pills that I refused in fear of developing an addiction. The pain is always there, sometimes not so bad, sometimes small enough for me to ignore. But there are always times where it rises up, raging, as if to punish me for some wrongdoing that I didn’t even know I had committed. It’ll radiate through my whole body, into my fingers, my toes, render me immobile and helpless and useless. Life is loss. Loss after loss after loss. First it was my nana in my freshman year of highschool. Midterms week. January 24th. I woke up sweating, my stomach knotted, a dream of my nana burning in a fire fresh in my mind. My sister had a similar dream. We both went the whole day knowing that something terrible was going to happen. And it did. She died unexpectedly of a heart attack that afternoon. We didn’t know till the evening. My mom simply froze while she was on the phone. I reached desperately for her hand, pleading for an explanation. She hung up, and I was eager for an answer. I had yet to get one, because she started screaming, screaming like her ribs were being sundered apart and her heart torn out of her. I’ll never forget that sound. Then it was Donna. I’d gone to her daycare for years. She was like a second mother to me. Ovarian cancer took her, attacking mercilessly until she was an emaciated corpse. That was the year after my nana died. The year after that my parents separated. They’d forgotten how to communicate with each other, fallen out of love. They were both to blame, but I wasn’t angry: just sad. So, so sad. My dad left on Father’s Day. He said goodbye to me while I was curled up in his place in bed and crying and begging for him to stay. He didn’t stay. He came back, but only to help out around the house since my mom got a surgery on her foot. I was mad at him then, but I wanted to forgive, because despite everything, I loved him to pieces. I still do. He adopted me. He wanted me when the man who had helped give me life didn’t. I’ve forgiven him now, but nothing’s the same. I helped him move into his new apartment. I stay there sometimes, but it’s not right. The house was missing his presence. The losses didn’t stop there. It was like life had it out for me. Last March my parents finally got divorced. Then my mom lost her job because her boss didn’t like her. We wanted to keep the house, but that was impossible. We lost that too. As I was going off to college, I took my last look out the car window at the house. It would be the last time I would ever see it. It wasn’t even the same color. The new owners had had the outside painted already. I felt like I was standing on a rug that was being lifted up from under me and I was tumbling backwards, ready to fall. We would have gone homeless if my papa didn’t own an apartment building and two of the residents were moving out. I hate it there. It’s too small. It has no dishwasher. It’s not the town I grew up in. The town doesn’t even have a movie theater. What’s up with that? Luckily though, I was living in a dorm on campus. I wouldn’t have to be at the apartment all the time. Though my sister, who’d been by my side forever, my twin who I loved more than anything or anyone in the whole world, was going to a different college, all the way in Pennsylvania. My life was changing too fast and I felt like I couldn’t get a solid foothold. The first semester was hard. My pain tried to stop me from doing anything, but most days I gave it the middle finger and went about my business. I went to classes, did my assignments, joined clubs, hung out with friends. Naturally, there were days where the pain won and I could do nothing but lay in bed and summon the occasional moan. There were days where I would wake covered in blood, and I’d have to grab a change of clothes and get into my bathrobe before my roommates could see me. Then I’d run to the bathroom, body protesting every movement, blood dripping down my legs and pattering onto the floor. I’d clean off in the bathroom and get blood all over my hands, all the while it was still gushing relentlessly from my body. After that, I’d have to go about the rest of my day like everything was normal… after throwing my clothes in the trash of course. No amount of stain remover could even hope to get rid of that. I cried about throwing my clothes away and felt ridiculous about it. For years all I’d heard was: “It’s just a period. It’s not that bad. You’re a woman. Get used to it.” I did get used to it. I got used to suffering every day. I got used to waking up drenched in crimson. I got used to going to the bathroom, only to get blood on my hands. I got used to going through pads like they were nothing: a pack a week. I got used to crying quietly into the night because cramps that almost made me go blind with pain held sleep from me with a cruel hand. Still, I got through the semester. December. Christmas. That wasn’t going to be so bad. I was dead wrong. On Christmas Eve, my dad’s parents, my memère and pepère, sat us down, quiet, solemn, eyes bloodshot. They told us that my aunt, who had ovarian cancer just like Donna had had, had a month left to live. I took off my glasses so I wouldn’t stain the lenses with tears, and cried. We all cried. I hugged my dad hard. I was hurting for my own impending loss. I was hurting for his loss. He’d already lost a sibling, his brother when he was young. He’d had an unknown heart condition and a baseball had hit him hard in the chest. He’d died of a heart attack as a teenager. My dad still had two sisters, but now he was going to have one. I’d never seen him cry like that before. Naturally, I was angry, furious. Everything and everyone was wishing me a merry Christmas. It wasn’t merry. I’d just found out my aunt had a month left to live. She didn’t make it a month. On the drive back up to college in mid January, we got the call from my dad. She was gone. My sister got to talk to him first. Then it was my turn. We didn’t even talk the whole time. My dad and I just cried together. He yelled wordlessly, heartbrokenly, into the phone, into the emptiness that was his apartment. I felt like I was being punched in the chest. I didn’t want to hang up on him, didn’t want to leave him alone with the grief that could swallow someone whole. I had to hang up eventually though, and then I started screaming, screaming as if I was being torn apart. Because I was. I’d talked to my aunt on Thursday night, and now Saturday morning she was dead. I’d been scared to talk to her. It was on the phone, which I was glad for. I didn’t want to go down to South Carolina and see her in person, didn’t want my last visual memory of her to be how the cancer had destroyed her body, with her wife stricken and hopeless at her bedside. I was afraid that she wouldn’t sound the same, that her voice would be weak, raspy, a husk of what it once was. That wasn’t how I wanted to remember her, and that wasn’t what it was. She sounded the same. We carried on a normal conversation for a bit, ignoring the fact that she was dying. Then she spoke her last words to me. She told me to be strong, to follow my passions, to kick down any door that stood in my way. She told me she loved me. I said it back, crying silently. I didn’t want to turn into a sobbing mess while on the phone with her. Then we all said goodbye. There was no “Talk to you later.” It was final. That was it. We hung up. The silence was loaded with agony, agony we all felt in solidarity. My dad shared with us what he’d wanted to say to her but couldn’t. He’d wanted to tell her to say hello to their brother when she got up there, but he couldn’t get it out. Hearing him say it took a jagged knife to my chest. My sister, my dad, and I all hugged and cried. We lost track of time in each other’s arms. I kept thinking of how I’d never hear her again, never see her again. I hurt for myself. I hurt for her family. I hurt for her wife, who’d only been married to her for a few years. I kept thinking of how unfair it was. I missed the funeral because I had classes. My dad sent me pictures. It was beautiful. A military funeral, as she was a Major. They had her cremated. Per her request, some of her ashes were thrown into the ocean. She hadn’t lived in Rhode Island for a long time, but for her it had still been home, and she always wanted to be a part of it. I wanted to go to the funeral, but I could hear her in my head telling me not to skip out on school on her account. She wouldn’t have liked that. The semester was a mess. I tried to be happy and I could be sometimes, but there was a haze of depression following me wherever I went. I stopped going to club meetings. I stopped hanging out with friends as often. I stopped doing my art, stopped playing my bass clarinet and had my mom take it home because it was just a waste of space. I tried praying once, like my mom suggested I do, though I’m not religious anymore. I didn’t feel anything but a vast, dark emptiness. Instead of helping, praying made me feel the most alone I’ve ever felt in my entire life. I haven’t tried again. I’m too afraid to reach out and find nothing but a void and meaninglessness. I’m too afraid to feel that utterly alone again. So there I lay, Sunday night, dreading the coming day, and the day after that, and so on. I was walking through life with massive weights on my feet and I was tired. I was tired of the pain. I was tired of the loss. I was tired of everything. There were scissors on my desk. Sharp. And my wrists were ripe for cutting. There were all sorts of pills on my desk and a half a bottle of wine in the fridge to swallow them down with. Then I could lay down, go to sleep, and not wake up again. But Marilyn, my roommate, was in the room. She would notice. I didn’t move to do any of those things. I slept. Fitfully, I’d wake up to consider the pills and the alcohol and the scissors, then fall back to sleep, only to wake up an hour or so later to the same thoughts. I tossed and turned, but didn’t move from my bed. I was desperate to end it all, but there was part of me that just wouldn’t let me do it. I clung to it, and as the hours ticked by, I watched it crumble in my hands. Morning came. My alarm went off at 8:00 as usual, but I was already up. I turned it off and just laid there, considered laying there forever and dying of thirst. But then I forced myself up, began going through the motions. I was scared of my own thoughts. I was overwhelmed by life - the past, the present, the future - but somehow I also felt numb. There was no thought behind my morning routine. Though, there was one last thing I wanted to try before I ended it all. I went to the dining hall. Having suffered from mental illness for eight years, I’d taught myself to take pleasure in the small things in life, be it the taste of food, or a ray of sunlight, or the chirp of a bird. I got vanilla donuts (gluten free of course. I wanted to die, but that wasn’t a practical way to go), milk and an apple, all things I loved. I expected to be rejuvenated with each bite, for the flavor to wash away the numbness. I couldn’t taste a thing. I ate all of it, desperately, trying to find an ounce of the flavors I loved. There was nothing. I might as well not have been eating. Though I wanted to die, and was now ultimately planning on it, I went to my first class anyway, feeling numb and overwhelmed all at once. It’s a hard feeling to describe. There was no equilibrium between them. They somehow existed in the same space. I couldn’t focus in class. I was past the sorrow, the negative thoughts of the past. I was now devising a plan to kill myself, writing a note in my head: “I’m sorry. I’m not doing this because I don’t love you. I love you all so much, so much it hurts, but I’m tired. I’m just so tired. I want to sleep, sleep forever. I want time to stop. I want everything to stop. I’m sorry. I love you. Please don’t think I’m selfish.” The “I love you” was to my friends, my family. I love so fiercely, but the black hole of depression had stolen everything from me, and my fight for it back had been futile. Class ended and I quietly left the building. I usually exchanged a few words with my professor, (she was nice), but not today. Then I found myself standing outside the building and was left with the monumental choice. Left or right. Right led to my dorm room. My roommates would be in class. It would be quiet. I would be alone. I would go back there, write my note, leave it where someone would find it, and kill myself. I felt sympathy for Marilyn. We weren’t friends, but we lived well together and had taken care of each other when the other needed it. She would find my body. That didn’t deter me though. All I could think about was that silent room that I would return to, the pills and alcohol I would consume, the scissors I’d use to slit my wrists. Then I could lay down, close my eyes, and sleep. It would be silent, peaceful. No more struggle, no more pain. I could just sleep forever. I began to walk right. I made it a few feet and something stopped me. I don’t know what it was. Maybe that want to live that had crumbled to dust over the past few hours had begun to piece itself back together. I took another step, another step towards my dorm, another step towards death, another step towards relief. Then I abruptly turned around. I went left instead of right, began walking so fast I was nearly running. The college’s counseling center was in the building ahead of me. If I got there, I’d live. I’d tried to go right, towards death, but was now going left, towards life. I wasn’t done yet, and I needed help. I needed help or I wasn’t going to live to see the next day, the next hour. I rushed into the building, shaking, throat aching with pent-up sobs, blinking back tears. I got to the counseling center, went right up to the receptionist and got out frantically that I needed an emergency appointment. She handed me a paper, a checklist of what was bothering me. Right at the top: suicidal thoughts. I checked it off. I was clinging to life with one hand now, clawing with my nails. I handed the paper back to her, saw the change in her eyes as she looked at it, the seriousness of the situation falling over her. She had me sit by her instead of in the usual waiting room. I’d be alone in the waiting room, alone with my thoughts, alone with hell and the black hole sucking in my gut, a helpless victim. She made a call to any of the therapists that were available. I sat, sweaty hands clasped tightly, legs bouncing, breath rushing, chest constricting. I hardly saw the room in front of me. All I could see was my dorm room, weak light filtering through the blinds, the pills and scissors on my desk, my cozy unmade bed. It wasn’t just waiting for me. It was calling me, and a voice in my head that I knew was my own was telling me to do it, just do it. “End it. Wouldn't it be so easy? Just end it. End. It.” I couldn’t. I couldn’t now. There was no way the receptionist would let me leave. I’d sealed my fate, my fate to live, and I didn’t want it. My death bed was yearning for me, and I was scared to be heading anywhere else. I was also scared to have come so close to killing myself. I was scared of everything, I was scared of each passing second. Each breath was a pain, not because of asthma, which I’ve had since I was two, but because I didn’t want it. I didn’t want the breath I took in and blew out, or the one after that, or the next. I wanted to collapse to the floor and die . But I didn’t want to. I didn’t. It made no sense. I was stuck, so horribly stuck, stuck between two walls that were compressing, closing in on me and crushing me, squeezing and squeezing. Existing in those moments was unimaginable anguish. Thinking of killing myself was the same. Finally, the therapist, Noah, arrived. Not my usual therapist, but that was fine. I’d take anyone, any savior. We went to his office, sat across from each other. He had seen the paper. I didn’t have to tell him I wanted to kill myself. He knew. Noah asked me why. I explained the loss, the unending pain, the sensation of being swallowed by life, the strange numbness that went with it all. I was completely honest. I wasn’t ashamed. There was no room for shame amongst my terror and battling wishes to die and live. My brain had become a battlefield, a bloody, violent, loud battlefield. We talked for a bit. I cried. I was trembling. The battle was taking a toll on my body. I wanted to lay down and never move again. I told him my plans. I told him I had yet to write a note but had been planning on it. I told him I had been planning to go back to my dorm to die. Noah gave me a survey, asking about my wish to die, potential plans, a lack of will to live. There was a number next to each answer. The higher the number, the more dire the situation. The lowest number was zero. The highest was two. I was circling twos. Noah looked at the survey with a frown. He told me what I already knew, that it was terribly concerning. My score was high. That was bad. Very bad. He asked me if I trusted myself. The answer was no. Hell no. If I was left alone, I would kill myself. I would die. He called my mom before the ambulance. I let him talk to her first and explain the situation. I had no idea how to tell my mom - the brave, amazing woman that had given me life and cared for me for twenty years - that I had been so close to killing myself, that I still wanted to do it, that life was a living torment and I wanted to escape. Permanently. Noah explained it calmly, clinically. She seemingly took it well, without panic. Then the phone was handed to me and I cried into it, cried to her from about a hundred miles away. I begged for her to not think me selfish, and she assured me that she didn’t, told me she was glad I was getting help, and that she was coming up from Rhode Island. The drive from Rhode Island to Albany was three hours, four if traffic was bad. It was a bit of a journey, but she didn’t care. She was going to leave work, drive all that way to see me, to rescue me. I could hope to live till then. I could suffer existence long enough to see my mom, and I hoped her presence would imbue me with life again. Then Noah called the ambulance. Something was seriously, deathly wrong, and I couldn’t be trusted to get to the hospital on my own. I didn’t really mind. I was just slightly annoyed that there wasn’t going to be such a long span of time between my last ambulance ride and this one. Around a week and a half ago, I’d been transported after collapsing in mind-crushing pain. An ovarian cyst had burst. And now, not long after, for something vastly different, I was going to be in an ambulance again. As Noah and I were waiting for it, my sister, Lisa, texted me. She told me she was taking a mental health day and not going to classes. I laughed cynically, or maybe I sobbed. I wasn’t sure. There were tears in my eyes. How was I supposed to tell Lisa? How was I supposed to tell her over text? I couldn’t summon the strength to call her. If she was already having a bad day, (she has depression too), I didn’t want to make it worse. So, somehow, I carried on a normal conversation with her. I talked about how she was doing, completely left out anything about myself. I couldn’t possibly tell her I wanted to die. But I had to at some point today. The only good thing was that she’d already decided she wasn’t going to class. Lisa wouldn’t have to labor through classes with the knowledge that her twin sister had almost killed herself. The ambulance arrived, and so did the police, though Noah had said the police weren’t needed, that my reaching out was completely voluntary. I was stressed that they’d shown up anyway. More people to know about my crisis, but not anyone close to me: complete strangers. They questioned me a little bit, and then I went into the ambulance. The paramedic in the back with me told me that I was brave for choosing life, that she understood how hard it could be with depression. It felt good to be called brave and strong, though those were the last things I felt at the moment. I felt weak, the weakest I’d ever been. I was weak for considering death, for not powering through like I always had. I was weak for needing help. But I accepted the comments that I was brave. Maybe it would help. I got to the ER. They took my clothes, my backpack, everything but my cellphone, which was surprising. I wasn’t texting my sister now though. I wasn’t texting anybody. I couldn’t. I was trembling in the too-big gown they’d given me, not because I was cold, but because I was scared, terrified. It was a rush of people coming in and out, taking my vitals, asking for my information. I didn’t process names or faces. There were just voices, and I responded as best as I could. Then they left me in a bed out in the hallway. A resident director from my college was there. It was protocol for him to stay until a family member arrived, and my mom was on her way. His name was Michael. He seemed nice enough, but I didn’t know how to talk to him, not yet. I remembered my cellphone in my hand. It was time to call my sister, tell her where I was, what was going on, that I was probably going to be admitted. She felt awful that she’d been texting me about her own problems, asked why I hadn’t told her sooner. The answer was simply that I couldn’t. I just couldn’t. We talked for a while. I told her I loved her. She said it back of course. Then I had to hang up. Another person to ask me questions had arrived. After that, I waited, not speaking a word to Michael. I wasn’t waiting long before someone came, took my phone, and escorted me to another room. This room made me think of a prison. There was a bed in the center, bare of anything but a fitted sheet and a pillow. There was a TV, but the screen was void and blank, and there was no remote in sight. The walls were green, and the floor was the same linoleum that had covered the rest of the hospital. Something about it felt vastly empty and depressing. I sat on the bed. It was hard, uncomfortable. Michael settled himself in an armchair near the bed, and then the door was closed. I felt trapped, and my thoughts were charging at me again, threatening to take me, swallow me. There was nothing in the room that I could hurt myself with, and that was good, because again, my own voice inside my head was telling me to do it, to kill myself. I panicked. I began rambling, trying to make conversation. Maybe if I talked about something else, the hell inside me wouldn’t devour me. Still, as I spewed out words, those thoughts, that plague , was gnawing at me with sharp teeth, chomping away, trying to eat me. It was desperate, hungry, drooling over me as if my torment was the best thing it had ever tasted. Luckily, Michael was willing to talk, willing to help me keep the monster at bay. Turned out we had a lot in common. We talked about cats, movies, video games. We talked for hours until a doctor came into the room and asked him to sit outside. I was scared, but he assured me that he would be back. The doctor asked me about my suicidal thoughts, about this depressive beast. I had to let it free, had to let it eat more of me as I spoke. I told her how it had taken everything from me - my passions, my desires - and how now my life was the only thing it had left to take. I can’t remember if I cried, or if I reported everything numbly. Numb and overwhelmed, all at once. She took notes as I spoke, and again I was told that it was concerning, that I was probably going to be admitted. I was okay with that. I couldn’t trust myself. I needed help. The doctor left and Michael came back in, and we continued our talk about movies and video games. It helped distract me from the sharp teeth of the monster eating my mind and my soul. Then it was decided. I was going to be taken upstairs, admitted. I was terrified now. It was 4:00 pm. Where was my mom? As security was waving a metal detector over my body, a precaution before moving me upstairs, my mom appeared at the end of the hallway. I would have run to her if I could have. She quickened her pace as best as she could with her bad foot, and then I was in her arms, pressing my face into her hair, hugging her hard, letting myself be enveloped in her smell and her warmth and her love. Her smell was comforting. I can’t describe it other than to say it was her. “I love you, mom. I love you. I’m so sorry.” We pulled away. I can’t remember her exact words, but she told me not to apologize, told me she was so grateful I had gone to get help, that I was still here , living and breathing in front of her. I was overwhelmed with emotion: terror, love, shame, relief, and I hugged her again, tears in my eyes, a smile somehow fighting through everything to spread my lips. “Momma smell,” I murmured into her ear. It was something I’d been saying since I was little, too little to describe the comfort her scent brought except with those two words. It’s something I’ve never stopped saying, and I don’t plan to. It has meaning to me, meaning to my mom, and she responded with the usual words that conveyed the same meaning. I’d always had trouble speaking to people, so she’d learned my particular language to communicate with me. “Amber smell.” Then we moved apart, and with a nurse escorting us, began our journey up to the third floor to the mental health unit. I wanted to hold my mom’s hand, but the nurse walked fast, and I tried to keep up a little so I wouldn’t lose sight of him. It was twists and turns, then an elevator, then more twists and turns, then another elevator. Everything was a blur. More people talked to me. My mom was sent to a waiting room as they catalogued my possessions and had me sign papers. I didn’t remember faces, names. I knew I needed help, knew I needed to be here, but I was scared now, so scared. I just wanted my mom again. I wanted to keep my hoodie, my old one that had paint stains on it and said “I love my bass clarinet” with a picture of a bass clarinet and a heart. They had to cut the drawstrings out, a precaution so I wouldn’t strangle myself with them. I watched the woman checking me in do it, and then I started crying. That’s when it really hit me. I was in a mental hospital, and my beloved hoodie had to be defaced for my own safety. It would never be the same again. They took my clothes, put them on my bed in my room. I didn’t know where it was yet. Then I was allowed to see my mom again. I wanted to talk to her in a private place, but instead we had to meet in the unit’s community room, where the patients were eating dinner. I found a semi-secluded corner and sat with my mom, took her hand. I didn’t know what to say to her, and if words came out, I didn’t know how to say them, and it was just incomprehensible. Though, eventually we were able to make a plan for her to go back to my dorm and get me some things. I told her to contact one of my friends, and I would give her a list of names that that friend could tell about where I really was. Afterwards, my mom left, saying she would be back around 8:00. I was shown to my room, where the clothes I had arrived in were folded neatly on my bed. I changed, feeling a little better to be out of the hospital gown. I looked around the room. Two beds, a wide window. There was trash strewn about the room, a sign of what my roommate, whom I had yet to meet, was like. My anxiety spiked. I didn’t want a roommate. I wanted to be alone. After changing, I found my way to the lounge where a few women were sitting in the dark, trying to decide what movie to watch. I sat. I had no opinion of what we would be watching. I was hungry, not having had anything to eat since 8:30 that morning. I had missed dinner, but I was told they were going to get me food. I was terribly nervous about that. I have celiac disease, and so can’t eat anything with gluten. My reactions are severe and put me in the hospital. The movie started. It was a horror movie, badly written, awful cinematography. I didn’t care. It gave me something to occupy my mind with. Dinner came. Grilled chicken and french fries, hopefully nothing that would get me sick. Only one way to find out. I was starving. I ate it. The movie ended. My mom came back with clothes and toiletries and one of my coloring books with my colored pencils. Not my prismacolor pencils thankfully. They were expensive and I didn’t want to risk losing them. She had to say goodbye to me then. I was crying again. I couldn’t get words out. I was scared out of my mind, in disbelief. How had it come to this? How had I ended up here? How was this my life? I didn’t want to let go. I clung to her. Finally, I let go, and she left. I felt alone. Utterly alone. None of the other patients had bothered to greet me or ask me my name. Discouraged, wiping tears from my eyes, I went to my room and sat on the bed. It was uncomfortable, but better than the bed that had been in the ER. I looked at my clothes folded on my bed, picked up my other hoodie. The drawstrings were gone, and I cried again. I felt like I was being punched in the chest. I tried putting my clothes away and neatening my things as best as I could, cleaning up the trash that was on my side of the room. They called me out to check my vitals and have me take my medications. I took them, went back to my room. I sat on the bed and stared out the window. It wasn’t a pretty view, just one of the street below and a traffic light. After some time, sitting there and feeling numb, I roused myself to brush my teeth. There was a small bathroom with a toilet and a sink in the room. The door closed, but didn’t lock. As I got ready for bed, I pondered where the beast had gone. It had finally stopped chewing on me, withdrawing into the dark to leave my soul bleeding and wounded. There it waited, looming, threatening me, waiting for another chance to pounce and render me helpless. My roommate came in. She didn’t talk to me, just let out a curse and rubbed her bulging abdomen. She was pregnant. I tried saying hello, but got no response. Feeling shunned, I got into bed, wondering how I was ever going to sleep. My roommate turned off the light, got into bed as well. I didn’t take off my glasses right away. I stared at the ceiling, then rolled onto my side, watched as the traffic light below turned from red to green. It was quiet for a while, but then my roommate began talking. At first I thought it was to herself. It certainly wasn’t to me. Then I made out the word “baby”, realized that she was speaking to her unborn child. I appreciated the sentiment of it, but I also wanted silence, wanted to sleep. I was bone tired. I took off my glasses and put them in their case, then put them on the floor beside the bed. My roommate started singing. I was too afraid to ask her to be quiet. I had no idea if she was violent or not. She definitely came off as hostile to other people - that was for sure - and I didn’t want to put myself in harm’s way. There was no clock in the room, but if I had to guess, I’d say she did this for about an hour. It was finally quiet, but I still couldn’t sleep. One of the techs opened the door, checked off that we were both in the room, then closed it again. He was doing rounds, and apparently this was supposed to be happening every fifteen minutes, but it felt more like forty-five between each one. I somehow fell asleep, woke up and saw that it was still dark, fell asleep again. This continued for the whole night, until I opened my eyes and saw that the sky had lightened. I watched it grow lighter in my incredibly impaired vision, color leaking into the world. My eyelids drooped. It felt like, just as I was falling asleep again, there was a knock on the door and a voice calling us to breakfast. The room was filled with white light. I dragged myself out of bed, pulled on my white bass clarinet hoodie, and made my way to the community room. I sat down among a bunch of strangers and waited for my food. I managed to spark conversation and get a few names. The women I was sitting with seemed rather nice. Our food came. Again I was filled with dread that what I would be eating was not gluten free. I hadn’t gotten sick the night before though, so I was a little hopeful. It didn’t keep me from just picking at it though. I couldn’t find it in myself to be hungry. At least I was tasting again. I’d tasted dinner the night before too. After breakfast I filled out a menu for the next day, then waited in line for vitals and meds. Silently. No one wanted to talk to me or make me feel more at ease. I took my meds, then ended up in the lounge again. I had no clue what was on TV, but I watched it anyway. I didn’t know what else to do. At some point I was called into a meeting with my psychiatrist and social worker. The psychiatrist deduced that one of the meds I was already on had caused the suicidal thoughts, along with the stressful situations in my life. She lowered the dose of that one and added an antidepressant. I was fine with that. Anything to help me. Then my social worker asked me a question: “If I said I had a pill that would make you go to sleep and never wake up, would you take it?” No such pill existed. It was a question to gauge my mental state. My answer? “Yes.” After that, they let me go, and I returned to the lounge to watch TV. That’s when the screeching and banging started. A woman, sounding like a raving animal. The other patients in the lounge simply rolled their eyes at the noises coming from the hallway while I cowered in the chair. They explained that this patient did this almost every day, but that didn’t help to make me feel safer. There was commotion, nurses and techs trying to calm her. She was swearing, seething, shrieking. I heard people asking the woman not to punch them. I didn’t see any of this, but hearing it was enough to frighten me terribly. They got her into her room, and then there was relentless banging, sobbing. I was still scared, but I felt safer now that she was behind a closed, (and hopefully locked), door. The banging continued for some time, and once it stopped, I felt safe enough to leave the lounge. I went into the community room, and one of the other patients finally spoke to me first. She invited me to a game of Sorry , and we got a couple of other people involved. I spent a good portion of the day playing board games and card games, finally getting to know some of the other patients, beginning to feel more relaxed. Sometime in the early evening, I went back to my room to find it completely trashed. I cautiously asked my roommate about it. In her words she “went in there and fucked it up.” That didn’t make me feel good, and she’d moved my things too. I couldn’t room with someone who had no regard for boundaries and property like that. I asked one of the techs on duty if I could be moved somewhere else, and he obliged, putting me in a room with a woman I’d been growing friendly with over the past few hours. She was excited to have me as a roommate, and I felt a million times better. Visiting hours came around, and the depression crawled out from its hole. There was no one to visit me. My mom was back in Rhode Island. My sister was in Pennsylvania. My dad had no idea I was here. I called one of my friends, assured them that I was safe, and asked when they would be able to visit. They didn’t know. It was nearing the college’s finals week and they and my other friends were really busy. It was understandable, but it still put a damper on my mood. I was given something to help me sleep that night, and oh boy did it work: I nearly missed breakfast the next morning! As the days went on, I began getting used to the routine of the place: breakfast, vitals check, menus, meds, then free time till lunch at 11:30, time I usually used to sleep. Then dinner was at 4:30. Snack time was at 8:00. We had to be in our rooms by 10:00. Somewhere in the evening, they took our vitals and gave us our meds. Sometimes there were group activities and group therapy sessions. I always missed the morning ones because of my naps, but I attended the afternoon ones as often as I could. I wanted to get better. I was working towards it, and I could feel it helping: the new meds, the talks with my social worker, the group sessions. The time to sleep without fears and anxieties was good too. It was freeing. For the past months, sleep had never been just sleep for me. I’d take ages to fall asleep, my thoughts racing, sucking me into a pit. Then when I would finally fall asleep, nightmares would come, induced by stress and anxiety. I would wake sweating, feeling like I was being pounded in the chest, my heart racing. Now though, I was able to just sleep , not weighed down by stress and obligations and anxiety. And the meds were helping too. They made me dizzy, but I was content to be given them since they were helping. I’ve never had a stigma against taking medication for mental illness. It’s an illness just like any other illness, and it needs to be treated. Each day my social worker asked me the pill question. At first, my answer was always yes. Then it turned into a maybe. The hulking beast of suicidal ideation was crawling farther back into the recesses of its cave. Now the answer is no. I don’t want to die anymore. I have things to live for. I took my passions and goals back from depression’s greedy hands, took my life back from its clutches. I’d been on the brink of death, and my first few days in the hospital were filled with thoughts of “Oh shit, I would have been dead.” Then I saw the opposite side of the coin. My thoughts are now “I’m alive! Wow! I’m alive!” It’s a victory, hard-won and very much worth it. I survived the very real monster in my own mind. I won this battle, but there are more to come. Mental illness isn’t a single battle. It’s a war, a war that I must fight if I want to keep living, and I do. I really, really do. On one of my last nights in the hospital, I was sitting around with some other patients, eating Dove chocolate, the ones with the little messages written in the wrapper. Usually I found those messages meaningless, but the second one gave me pause, sent a chill through me: “When life isn’t going right, go left.” Go left. Go left . Left or right. Right was to die. Left was to live. Left had saved my life. And so from now on, when my life isn’t going right, I’ll go left. I’ll always go left.
