text: This is the Bojangles’ I grew up with. My sister Lee and I never really lived under the same roof because she was so much older. None of my childhood memories include the two of us sharing the same house because she was off to college before I was even in kindergarten. She would come home for the summer, but it was like having a cool young auntie and less like having a big sister. Still, I would follow her around everywhere because that’s what you do when you’re five and a cool young adult from far away comes to live in your house once a year. One morning when she was home from college for the summer, I woke up and she was getting ready to go to Bojangles’ for some breakfast sammiches. It was a weekday and my mom had gone to work, but my dad was working from home and instead of cooking, he figured she could run out to bring home some breakfast. Sidenote about my dad: He WILL NOT touch anything in the kitchen other than to wash dishes. My mom cooked. My dad would not. He wouldn’t even touch the microwave. One time y’all — and he would probably be mad if he read this but it makes me laugh — my mom went to Reno for a women’s bowling tournament with her team (one of her teammates later married my dad a few years ago when my mom died, just some extra info) and she cooked before she left. She divided everything up by day and she put everything in the freezer in nice bags or tupperware. I was about 9 years old at the time, and when I got up in the morning, I would take whatever out of the freezer we were supposed to eat for dinner that day and then heat it up when I got home from school. Unfortunately, I got the flu while mom was gone and I just didn’t feel like eating anything other than soup so I wasn’t fooling with those meals in the freezer and do you know my dad just…DIDN’T EAT? Instead of heating it up himself, that man ate peanut butter on bread or nothing at all for like three days. So basically, that morning my mama was at work and there was no cereal in the house? We woulda just not eaten breakfast if my sister wasn’t home from college and able to drive to Bojangles’. I woke up and saw Lee getting ready to go somewhere so of course 5-year-old me had to get up and get ready to go. Where we goin? What can I get when we get there? Can I work the radio? Very important 5-year-old concerns. Now, allow me to give you a little sense of what “going to pick up breakfast” means where I grew up. Today, if I wanna pick up a breakfast sandwich, I walk approximately half a block outside of my apartment and go to a bodega. If I don’t like that bodega, there’s another one across the street. If I don’t like that bodega, there’s another one down the block. If I don’t like any bodegas, there’s a McDonald’s about three blocks away. If I don’t like McDonald’s, I can go to a diner. If I don’t like diners, there’s Starbucks, or IHOP, or a bakery — there are a lot of options. Where I grew up, going to pick up breakfast means getting in your car and driving about 12 miles in one direction until you hit the Interstate with the usual collection of fast food restaurants you see near exit ramps, or you drive about 9 miles in the other direction until you hit the nearest town with a stoplight. Lancaster was that town. Please see below: The little red mark is roughly where I lived and the little blue mark is roughly where Bojangles’ is (was? IDK if it’s still there). The little yellow mark is also important, but we’ll come back to that later. That last time I was down in that area was for my dad’s birthday a few years ago. My mom had passed away, but he had remarried already, and she helped my sister and I plan a surprise trip to see him. Lee was afraid to fly and I hate visiting the South, so pops was definitely shocked to see us both at the airport. It was awkward being near home while being in someone else’s house since he moved in with his new wife because she was close to her family and all of his had moved away. Still, it was probably my favorite trip home — no fights, no arguments, no criticism about what I was wearing or what I was doing with my life — and my dad had the BEST time. My sister passed away that same summer, so I’m glad the last time the three of us were together it was a big party. Anyway, while I was down there, I met up with one of my mom’s high school classmates who was kind of like an auntie figure to me growing up and before our lunch at Waffle House, I drove out to my old house and then made the trip from there to Lancaster that I’d made so many times growing up. It’s actually a nice drive — lots of trees and just enough hills and curves to be interesting — and the speed limit is 55 because there’s nothing going on out there. Right before you get to Lancaster you come to the top of a hill and on the other side you see the town laid out below you. It’s not really the town — it’s just the stripmall/Wal-Mart section on the outskirts that you see in most small towns in the South — but still. Nostalgia and whatnot. I thought it was “going to town” when I was little. The morning I went to get breakfast with my sister, we were probably singing something together — she was the one who taught me how to sing in harmony — and cruising down the hill (red dot) toward town. Bojangle’s was farther in, but Lee decided we should probably stop at the grocery store because we were out of some things. That’s the yellow dot again. It’s important now. The speed limit changes when you get to the bottom of the hill, but a lot of people ignore it, especially early in the morning when there aren’t any cops around. We were probably cruising down the hill at 60. This map is recent (this morning) but when I was little, there were a lot fewer “intersections” because there weren’t as many business. There was a Duracell plant on the right (that big boxy area), a couple of little restaurants after it, and then that shopping center on the left by the yellow dot that had a Bi-Lo and a Rose’s (southern department store) and some other little strip mall outlets like Hi-Lites and/or Cato’s. I don’t really remember. But basically, since there were no businesses, there were no turning lights at all on that entire strip. You cruise down at 60, fly by all these stores, and turn off when you need to if your light is green. We got into an accident. My sister, bless her no-depth-perception-having sweet self, was a *terrible* driver. She tried to be cute one time and drive up to our mailbox so she could have me reach out and collect the mail and she scratched up the whole side of the car. She was leaving our driveway another time and totaled her first car, because she claimed the car cut off, but we all know she just misjudged how far away oncoming traffic was. And now, she was about to total another one with me in it. If you go to this intersection now, there is a dedicated turn left-turn light. When we got t-boned, it was just a regular stop light, we had green, and my sister thought she could make that left turn. There was a tractor trailer truck speeding toward us — gathering speed really, because that’s what they do on this strip before they hit the hill going out of town — and it had nowhere to go. It couldn’t swerve, couldn’t brake, and it slammed into the passenger’s side of my mom’s tiny little two-door Toyota Corolla at top speed. The truck hit my side, the car spun around, and then we slammed into that brown utility pole near the left side of the screen. The impact was on my side again. When I woke up, I remember seeing the windshield lying on the grass in front of the car and my sister’s hand patting me while she was crying “is he okay, is my little brother okay, he’s not saying anything, Dee say something, are you okay?” I guess the paramedics had been there for some time already because she was doing all this with a neck brace on that I could see out of the corner of my eye and the door on my side of the car had already been ripped off by rescue workers. They were trying to cut me out of my seatbelt and I remember one of them saying that if I had been a few inches taller, the shield over the top of the truck’s headlight would have gone through my head instead of just lightly grazing the top of my scalp. I don’t remember crying or yelling but I said something like “I’m fine” and then I tried to move because the back of my head hurt. I felt like I had a watermelon growing back there (it was probably more like the size of golf ball) and they were pushing my head back into the seat, to keep my neck still while they put a board behind me I think. I was trying to fight my way out of that because the lump on the back of my head was squished, but of course that was a bad idea since they didn’t know if I had spinal injuries. They got me out and strapped to the board and I couldn’t move anything of course. No arms, no legs, no head and neck, and that thing on the back of my head was the most intense pain I can remember about that day. I just wanted to sit up and not have something pressed against the lump on the back of my head. I think I was yelling to let me out and no one was paying attention. I was carried on the board to the ambulance and they put me on the left side. It wasn’t like in the movies where the stretcher is in the middle and the EMTs are on either side working on you. My little board and I were just laid up on the left banquette or whatever it is, the place where a loved one would sit and hold your hand on the way to the hospital. This isn’t the same ambulance, but this is the general setup. I was laid up on the grey bench. I’m not sure what else they were doing while they weren’t concerned with me. All I know is, the ambulance almost got into an accident, swerved to miss something, and I went rolling off that banquette and onto the floor of the ambulance, board and all. I was face down with random things falling from the shelves onto the board that was strapped to my back pressed up against the lump on my head. I started throwing up — projectile vomiting…with nowhere to project because I was face down. I had empty-stomach vomit, which is basically just bile I think, going all over my face and chest, up my nose, and into my eyes. They put me rightside up and then I don’t remember anything until I woke up in the hospital after they had reduced the swelling. My mom’s car was completely totaled, and when I looked at the pictures (I don’t have them to post) I couldn’t believe either of us had actually been in that car. It looked like a crushed up tin can. Not only did we live, but we didn’t have any broken bones. Other than some swelling and some scratches, we were fine aside from being sore for a few days. I had some lasting effects related to riding in the passenger’s seat though. I could only sit in the middle of the backseat for years. I don’t think I actually grew out of it so much as I eventually had to get my driver’s license, which put me in control, and I didn’t have to worry about a big truck hitting me on the passenger’s side while I just sat powerless. Anyway, I was paying bills this morning and thought “ugh, I need a better job, I’m so poor.” And it just reminded me that my parents didn’t sue anybody for pain and suffering and negligence while I was vomiting in my eyeballs and had they done so I probably would be set for life right now instead of clipping coupons and posting links to my PayPal. https://www.paypal.me/soletstalkabout Bye! PS: No we did not get our Bojangles’ sammiches that day and we actually didn’t eat there again for like a year.

Okay its really trippy but right before I fall asleep sometimes I'm hit with what I assume is a heavy dissassociation, I cant move or hear, or see my room anymore but instead I'm seeing this other world with people I know but they're living different lives. I can still feel that my body is physically in my bed but I also feel the physical sensation of this parallel life I'm seeing: light in my eyes, a hand on my back, my face against a pillow. And in this like, parallel life it still feels like I'm dissassociating but just to where it feels like ive never been in that life and I'm not in my actual body. Someone please explain why this has been happening increasingly :[

I’m trying to write again, so I wanted to try a personal narrative Pineapples. Are. My trigger. I’ve said it was. And I’ll say it again, forever. I will say it. Because, as long as I can look up and see my mother glaring at me for leaving my dishes out, Or I can hear her singing that one song for the tenth time that day, or I can see her smile at me after giving me a kiss on the cheek— I can proudly look at someone and say pineapples are my trigger My mother is deathly allergic We were out at dinner when I was younger and all of a suddenly my mom let out this really wet gasp. When you’re a child, watching your mother try to breath but can’t and being taken by an ambulance to the hospital, to be a child and hear your mother sobbing and spitting up because giant, bleeding soars were forming in her mouth and throat, to be a child and watch your mothers neck swell up to the point they have to put a mask on her face to try to help her get air, to contemplate slicing into her throat if she can’t get an airway... to see the knife as they sanitized the part of her neck under her chin that they were going to cut into, as my dad looked on with this look on his face I couldn’t understand back then, because I was a child So yes. I see pineapples at the supermarket and sometimes, when I don’t mean to, I stare at them a little longer then most, or I go out of my way to avoid them. No one asks when you say you’re trigger is a fruit— they usually look at you weirdly, sneer, or don’t even acknowledge it, just throwing it away as a joke... but not one person asks a question. Not one ever stops and goes, why? They don’t wait for your explanation. They assume, they think that you’re being insulting, that by bringing a fruit into their talk about their own triggers, it’s an insult— how dare you. Because how could a fruit compare to their own triggers , you know? Is it offensive to use a fruit, a yellow fruity thing, and call it a trigger? No one asked. They never do. I don’t expect anyone to, really. Because we all assume about something. They will just sneer, just because they can. And that’s okay with me. Because when someone asked me what my triggers are— I will say pineapples

It’s hard for me to imagine. I can imagine flat-earthers. I can imagine ghost hunters. I can imagine Truthers and Birthers. I’m talking about climate change. It’s not obvious yet. New research out of the University of Arizona examines the psychological effects of climate change. The UA researchers found “psychological responses to climate change seem to vary based on what type of concern people show for the environment, with those highly concerned about the planet's animals and plants experiencing the most stress.” This type is called biospheric concern. There is also altruistic concern, which deals with a worry for humanity in general, and egoistic concern, which regards direct impacts to the individual. The researchers make no attempt to assess the number of people experiencing negative psychological effects because of climate change. I would like an answer to this question. Not unrelated, the sea ice is the Arctic ocean is disintegrating. In a ten day period, ending February 16th, 2018, the Bering Sea off Alaska’s west coast lost 38% of its sea ice extent, in the dead of winter, when the sun does not rise. Meanwhile, off the northern coast of Greenland, which houses the second-largest ice body on the planet, capable of raising sea levels 20 feet if it is to melt. So far in 2018, an “unprecedented” 61 hours above freezing have been recorded. Amy Goodman, of Democracy Now, says: “The alarming heat wave is causing scientists to reconsider even their bleakest forecasts of climate change.” It snowed in the Sahara desert. Not that this has NEVER happened. I’m not saying that. Shut up for a second Uncle Frank. It’s happened before, yes, about 80 years ago, so the anomaly is not entirely knew, but taken within the context of other wild and weird and reoccuring weather events, it is significant. Signals are coming from all over the planet that the climate is changing abruptly. Do I need to mention the tornadoes in February, stretching from Dallas, Texas to Bangor, Maine? That’s a big storm. Multiple fatalities in multiple states. Do I need to mention that scientists have not spotted a SINGLE North Atlantic right whale calf in their usual breeding grounds? Not one calf along the coast of Georgia and Florida. 18 adults have already died in the past year, reducing the total worldwide to less than 450. Georgia DNR have NEVER seen a year without a single calf. I can’t imagine not knowing. That’s what I can’t imagine. I suppose it’s like trying to revert to belief in Santa Claus, trying to imagine Santa as real. I can’t do that, and I can’t unknow what I know and a lot of what I know, I see with my own eyes, and I don’t need a Washington Post article to teach it to me. There are no bugs around. Yeah, shut up Uncle Frank, I’m talking about Spring, Summer, and Fall. There are no bugs in winter, at least not at this latitude. In the Spring, Summer, and Fall, I can drive my car through a sunset, and well into night, and not have more than 6 bugs splash on my windshield. When I was growing, first driving, with my first car, the gas stations and convenience stores sold windshield wiper fluid that was pink and called Bug Goo, guaranteed to remove more guts than the blue stuff. The point is: bug guts were a problem that the free market attempted to solve, by brewing up a better windshield wiper cleaning fluid. It’s not necessary anymore. This winter, one week, we had zero-degree temperatures, even a few hours in the dead of night below-zero, which never happens; it never gets that cold. The next week it was 78 degrees and people were wearing shorts and tank tops to school, busting out longboards and studying outside under the trees. I can’t unknow this stuff. I know about the sudden stratospheric warming event, and the split and divided bi-polar vortex. I see in the back of my head, continually, the arctic surface air and surface water temperature anomaly maps emblazoned with these unprecedented bright reds and pinks, colors that have never appeared before, in all the years I have taken it upon myself to try to understand climate change, to know everything I might know about it, from the best minds in the world, the best scientists in the world, looking at the issue from many different perspectives: the ocean, atmosphere, biosphere, paleoclimatology, current climatology, meteorology, from political and social and economic viewpoints, to try to ease my own fears. A selfish motive. I have to know: Am I going to die (from climate change -- shut up Uncle Frank, I know we are all going to die someday!)? It didn’t start out as dramatic as that. It began by pondering whether I should be doing anything to combat climate change in my own little way. That’s why I began researching climate change. Plus, at the time, there was still some question, at least culturally and in mass media, whether climate change was even real. There was a lot of denialism. I wanted to know the truth. I began by finding people like Michael Mann and James Hansen, through the mass media. I found others, like Peter Wadhams and Kevin Anderson, or Jason Box and Stefan Rahmstorf, NASA’s Gavin Schmidt or Canada’s Paul Beckwith. I wanted the best people. The people in the know. The people out on the ice or ocean, in the air, studying the atmosphere, the people with first-hand knowledge. That’s where you go if you want to know something, to the people in the know, which means, the people with the most relevant experience. We don’t value experience -- No! We don’t, Uncle Frank . . . you think your opinion and observations about climate change carry as much weight as the warnings from Dr Jim White. Yeah, it’s a big problem, in the world, not just around climate change. Experience matters. I’d say, the most. I know these things that I cannot forget or unknow and these things depress and worry me most days and most nights. I feel extreme guilt for any sort of consumption, even the food I have to eat. All of it is based on carbon. The heat that keeps me warm when the polar vortex splits and zero-degree weather plunges into the mid-latitudes is part of the problem. The car I drive to work, to make the money I require to subsist -- feed, clothe, and house myself -- is part of the problem. The dog food I give to my dog, which is shipped from a warehouse three states away, is part of the problem. It’s a big, big problem, and I’m not just talking about the weather. We’re stuck with the weather, and it’s only going to get worse. Even if we stopped emitting carbon entirely, from this moment forward, CO2 levels in the atmosphere would continue to rise, for 5 or 7 years, at least, as the carbon we emitted yesterday works itself into the system. So, let’s say we stop emitting today, from this moment forward, and we survive the climate casino and weather weirding that has already begun, the carbon levels in the atmosphere, right now, at 410, are far too high, way beyond the holocene norm of 180-280 ppm. If we want to make things “normal,” like they used to be, stable and safe and relatively predictable, we have to return the planet Earth to carbon equilibrium, which is to say, back to 180-280 ppm We haven’t talked about feedbacks yet either, which by all indications, have already begun. We don’t need to wonder about methane releases in Siberia or the Arctic because they have already begun, massive blowholes have already formed in Siberia and you can start the ice on fire in some of Alaska’s lakes. That’s methane and that’s one of the positive feedbacks. The Atlantic meridional circulation has already begun to slow. A cold blob has formed off the southern coast of Greenland, formed by the gigatons of melt coming from the Greenland ice sheet. You remember that movie, The Day After Tomorrow, right, Uncle Frank? That was the Atlantic meridional current that stopped and froze the eastern seaboard, because the Atlantic meridional flow is an enormous heat engine that transports energy from the equator northward, ensuring that places like Great Britain have relatively warm climates for the latitude. Birds are dying. Coral reefs are dead. Whales are beaching themselves. Fish are dying. Bugs are gone. Diseases are up, killing humans and trees and deer. Baby moose are dying from ticks in northern MN. Thousands and thousand of ticks sucking the baby moose dry. Do you see? I know all these things. They are not made up. They are connected. The planet is dying, and that means so are we. Nobody, and I mean nobody, wants to talk about this. I will lose friends over this. People don’t want to know. Some people are militant about not knowing. I know, and that’s the problem. I don’t care much about me. I do care about the right whales and moose babies. I care about the birds, the animals, even the damn snakes. They didn’t ask for any of this. They didn’t cause any of this. They are certainly gonna keep dying because of this and that breaks my heart. That’s where I’m going to end -- biosphere concern. That’s me. I have biosphere concern. Through no fault of their own, that’s the basis of my biosphere concern. We have perpetrated a mass violence against this planet and its inhabitants, including ourselves, and we cannot seem to stop, not in the least, and not for even a second. I can’t imagine not knowing. I wish I could.

In writing down my odyssey , I’ve been trying to be methodical: pick up one idea, turn it around a few times and examine it, then put it down and move on to the next. Partially, it’s just easier to organize my thoughts that way, but it also gave me the opportunity to work myself up to the things that were harder to say. There’s one piece in particular I’ve been holding back, the one at the crux of the matter. Since I appear to be spectacularly incapable of telling what will be controversial, I want to be clear that this is my personal experience. Okay, here we go. Last July, I had a hysterectomy. The reason I needed one is that I’m asexual. In case you somehow missed it, I’m sex averse . I’ve talked before about what that means to me, specifically, but one of the big issues hiding in that complex set of attitudes and preferences revolved around my fertility. Pregnancy is not something I can contemplate in a detached manner like almost any other topic under the sexuality rubric; it is to me an unspeakable horror. Now, being as I’m sex averse anyway, mostly only attracted to people with whom I couldn’t reproduce if I wanted to, and barely attracted to people at that, you might not think it would be a major concern. Unfortunately, you would be wrong. My uterus was all about making the biggest fuss it could any time it could command my attention. It didn’t help that to protect my mental health, I spent any time it wasn’t being a nuisance actively suppressing the knowledge it was there, so I was often caught by surprise. The reminder that, no matter what I wanted or who I was, reproductive sexuality was part of my life was a torment. It was offensive to my person, a violation of my self perpetrated by it. It was unbearable. I’ve had to revise my definition of “unbearable” due to this ordeal. I’m a person who goes to extremes, who pushes through without complaint. I’m a person managing mental illness mainly through expecting myself to bear it. Even over the course of this, I asked too much of myself in some areas. I powered through the months of bleeding and the resulting anemia. I just took some ibuprofen and willed the pain and the migraines to go away. It could have all stopped if I’d stopped taking the pills. I couldn’t bear to stop. It wasn’t so strong or immediate as the tangible, physical side effects, but I know what it feels like to really hit that wall now. It’s not anything like “unable” to stop. When I realized that at the end of January, I also realized there was no way to go but forward. My resolve hardened, but I had a very large obstacle to surmount directly in front of me: I was unprecedented. Institutions don’t like exceptions to the rule. They don’t like being asked to take a chance on the unproven. I tried my best to articulate my “reproductive dysphoria,” but it’s hard to be the first one proposing the idea to a doctor disinclined to believe in anything but what was directly observable. And what was observable was, my symptoms did not merit a surgical intervention. Not by the standards of a doctor, and not by the standards of my insurance company. It was right about the time that an exploratory surgery was floated as my best option that I went for the escape hatch. You know, the only reason I started to see a therapist who specialized in gender identity and transitioning was to rule out gender as the cause of the Unbearableness. Everyone kept saying “are you sure you aren’t just trans?” but I was pretty sure I was right; in fact I was. When I say I “transitioned” in 2009-2010, that’s much too active a verb. What happened in 2009 is that a kid’s TV show had a main character with a sense of style that would not quit and I finally stopped wanting to dress like that and just did . What happened is that I got permission to be myself. I gave myself permission to be myself. The reason I got my surgery is because I finally stopped hand-wringing over whether I was “enough” and instead demanded that my suffering was enough. My issues were serious enough. I had had enough . I dropped anyone who refused to agree without remorse. I manipulated those around me to make them agree. I ruthlessly cut off any questioning of my decisions. And I listened to the voices who were supporting me: You are the expert, not anyone else . It’s not your fault . You don’t have to take on more than you can bear . You are allowed to take care of yourself first . You are allowed to be free of this . You deserve better . Like I said in the beginning, I thought long and hard about whether to share this story in this space. It’s a messy story that involves a lot of things I’m not proud of. It’s a story that I think, frankly, will make some people angry. It required me to be vulnerable in a way that I’m not comfortable with in general and is downright unwise on this website. But I did, because I wanted to make sure that anyone out there suffering like me had hope. I am giving you permission, here, now, for what it’s worth. You deserve to be yourself. You deserve the best.

So I was sitting there in my therapist’s office and she told me she wasn’t “sold” on asexuality as a term. So, you know me--I pull out the whole spiel about how the term came to be and how the community made strategic decisions and my opinions both as an activist and as a person. I even pull out a detailed analysis of the conclusion of that one study as hitting square on the nose what she was talking about and how undermining our language is incredibly harmful. Do you want to guess if I got to be the expert on asexuality, or if the person who couldn’t even remember the word got to overrule me? In general, one has to be honest and open with one’s medical providers. Keeping information back can impede an accurate diagnosis or an effective treatment ; the thing is, this lay squarely on the intersection of two things I prefer not to disclose to strangers: asexuality and mental illness. I thought long and hard about my strategy before going to see the doctors. I thought about how I would seem the most credible. How could I dress? What information should I give? How should I act when giving that information? The thing is, though, I was never going to be credible to those doctors. I convinced them all of the facts; even my first therapist up there agreed that I knew what I was talking about. Accepting all my givens, I was right; too bad my premises had to be faulty. I don’t even have a way to articulate the depth of my frustration. I was right about absolutely everything. I told everyone in advance what would happen. And even as those warnings came true, I was still painted as someone so deeply delusional, my own mental construction was the ultimate source of my problem. I was gaslighted into compromising and agreeing to do things that would make me sicker . And then as I fired these providers and found some who could actually accept that my reality was reality, they all asked me, “why did you wait so long?” Why did I wait so long? Because I had to save up the thousands of dollars to be able to afford to go to providers who would take me seriously. Because I had to work on my mental health until I was well enough to withstand the gauntlet I would be asked to walk. Because I had to be secure enough in myself that I wouldn’t get lost in the cadre of voices telling me how I should feel and how I should be. I learned a lot from my experience, and in many ways I grew as a person. That’s why I’m writing this series, I suppose--as a “lessons learned.” But this truth I knew going in. The rest of these short pieces I want to be a testament to hope. I overcame prejudice I never thought I would, and I did it in 6 months; that’s amazing. I’m so, so grateful. But this one bit, this is a reminder that I am someone so privileged--to live in a city with a large queer population, to have money, to have been born contrary. I held on for 15 years, but this kind of thing is hurting if not killing us, and most people don’t have the escape hatch I did. I fought the system and I won, but I won by luck. I won off the hard work of a handful of brave people. There is hope, there is , but we have to fight for it, and we must fight to protect it.

text: [CW: Mental Health, Self Harm, Reproductive Dysphoria] “You might be able to find someone to do it for you,” the doctor said to me on the way out, “but I won’t damage a healthy person your age.” It didn’t really hit me until the day after my surgery, as I was lying in bed trying desperately trying to sleep, that that’s exactly what she did . Or, rather, we need to take issue with her premise: I was not “healthy.” Mental health is health. I told her about my anxiety, which is ever-present but particularly activated by medical things. I told her how hard it was to come in to see her. I told her how a daily medicine would confront me with my phobia every day . I told her how this type of medicine in particular would be worse than almost all other types, both due to its composition and its name. I also told her about both my physical and mental suffering associated with my reproductive system. I told her how at one point it had been so serious, I had struggled not to harm myself. I told her how regardless of the layer upon layer upon layer of practical barrier to biological children, this made me incapable of carrying a child. She listened to me. I want to make that clear: she was not dismissive or patronizing. I was so pathetically grateful, I actually wrote her a thank you note. ‘Thank you for not laughing me out of your office.’ But she also handed me the instrument of my own destruction. At the time I thought I could make the choice for myself. I knew what lay on either side; the consequences of both action and of inaction. The choice is put before you: dysphoria or anxiety? Which do you choose? Honestly, I hope you’re never in the position to choose which part of yourself to burn to the ground. I hope you’re never desperate enough to set yourself on fire to in exchange for a sliver of hope . I hope that if you ever are, you’ll know it was no choice at all and blame the people who put the torch in your hand .

I was a difficult child in many ways, but there is one particular incident that stands out in my mother’s memory. When I was only 1, I apparently fell and injured myself to the point it required stitches. The ER doctor apparently underestimated the number of stitches I’d need, so he kept saying over and over “hmm, it needs one more” while my mother had to hold me down and lie to me. I don’t know how many times I’ve heard the story. It was the only thought in my head about as I soldiered through my panic attack and told the pre-op nurse not to call my mother back. Yes, part of my decision to keep this from my parents was to protect myself from their emotional reactions , but in their case specifically, it was also to protect them from a reality that would hurt them. It was hard as I recovered because I stayed with them. I would be upset about things, but I had no space to retreat to so they wouldn’t see. I had to expend energy on keeping up appearances. I couldn’t actually use that time to honestly grapple with and put to bed my emotions. My friends told me to go home so I could take care of myself, but I didn’t. On the contrary, I stayed several days longer than I planned. I didn’t want them to think I couldn’t stand being around them or that they were being bad parents. Every action was out of love--and my lies were too. As much as I have been called to recognize my own value and take care of myself, I’ve felt frustrated talking to neutral outsiders who couldn’t see why I needed to show kindness and love and understanding even though I had none leftover for me. They told me to put aside my service and instead show charity to myself. How would compromising my values be treating myself well, though? I was in the middle of society-made mess, and my choices were illusions . I was caught up feeling guilty over my personal level of culpability when in fact I was in a no-win scenario. I had no agency, but at least I had my integrity. I asked my parents to help me, but I met them halfway. I spared them as much as I could, even though it might have been better for me to make them understand the gravity of the situation. I drew my absolute boundaries and negotiated or compromised on everything else. That’s what I can live with.

I think most people would characterize me as a reserved person. In particular I tend to keep my emotions to myself, but that can be a struggle sometimes, especially when I have something very positive happening in my life. At those times I just want to tell everyone and have the whole world elated with me. I was full to bursting with this emotion immediately post-procedure, but most of the people in my life couldn’t wrap their minds around it and just share in my happiness with me. It was partially that secrets led to secrets led to secrets. I kept my distress to myself, mostly because I didn’t want to have that reaction where the person decides to pity you because they can’t possibly understand you. If you’ve been silent for years and years, how do you bring it up at all? My parents in particular were very hurt that I didn’t tell them about any of it until I was forced to say, “hey, I need a ride home from the surgery.” I tried to have an adult conversation with them about it, but in the end they couldn’t really understand. How could they? They haven’t carried it with them for over a decade like I have. They hadn’t seen it as it happened. Other family members I just didn’t tell. All my grandmother would hear is “surgery,” and she’d never stop worrying. Others would express sadness at never getting to meet my hypothetical children. To my IRL friends, I didn’t mention it except for private conversations; I didn’t mention it here either. I made only one public allusion, taking advantage of pride month, saying only how happy I was about the changes I’d seen in the world that had made “keeping a promise to myself” possible. All these years of holding myself back, keeping things to myself, staying silent--it was what I needed to do at the time. I needed to protect my most vulnerable spots from the average person’s inability to step out of their own shoes. I was already telling myself my problems didn’t matter ; I didn’t need outside confirmation. But now that my opportunity to share joy with the world instead of despair had come, I was stuck. I had laid none of the groundwork; in fact I had purposefully shielded those around me from anything that might have given them a foothold. How could I defeat their default emotions of worry, sorrow, and pity and prove to them that this was a triumph? Ultimately, I never found an answer. I’ve had a few more private conversations now, and they’ve gone well, but I think that for most of the people in my life this is going remain in the category of ‘things unsaid.’ I’m okay with that on its own level, but I mourn for its larger implications. Am I really so alien to my friends and family that they can’t understand something so incredibly important to me? It’s really brought home the magnitude of the disconnect. The only way to bridge the gap seems to be to talk about it openly and just endure the reactions. It’s not like I’m bad at answering this genre of question extemporaneously. I could do it. If I’m going to make sure I’m taking care of myself as well, though, I’m not sure I should.

here's the thing about me: i never remember anything, but then i get on the road and everything comes back to me. the stoplight turns red and my mother asks me if i remember the brightly colored plastic dishware we had when i was younger; i do. i used to eat my wheat chex out of the blue bowls, but with a metal spoon because the spoons in the set were flat plastic with a slight indent. the thought of the plastic against my lips makes me remember farther back to the taste of my aunt's tap-water in her clear plastic glasses, with the pink swirls on them. i used to wonder if all the water in santa ana tasted different, but i think it was just my aunt's filter. it was my grandma's house before it was my aunt's, or really it was theirs at the same time in the way childhood homes usually are. i want to go back there and sit in the driveway for an hour or two; i'd have asked someone to take me years ago but my dad would never let me because his street was dangerous. i think it ought to be a private pilgrimage, anyway. i heard the people who bought it trashed it and then it was empty and then drug dealers squatted there a while, and then it was empty again. i heard that the memories were stripped away and there's nothing left to it now, that i wouldn't want to see it how it is now. but it's the house my grandma locked me outside of when i was two because she had alzheimer's and she didn't want that little girl to play in her house. my grandma used to look out the window and see my dad running around in the backyard with me and ask 'is that my brother earl out there?' and my mom would say 'no, that's your son, that's ray.' then grandma would say 'oh' and my mother would bounce my sister on her knee and my grandma would lean in conspiratorially and say 'you should marry that man' and my mother would say 'i already did, this is your granddaughter here' and grandma would rest her hand on my mother's hand and say 'oh, that's nice dear.' i imagine she smiled serenely, after. in my rear view mirror i see police lights flashing on the other side of the road and i ask my mother what'd happened that time we got pulled over in that white sedan we had, the time when my sister and i were in booster seats in the back and my mom kept running her hand through her hair. mom says the car wasn't registered and the woman who pulled us over said she would have towed the car if my mother didn't have two toddlers in the back. mom couldn't get it registered because it was my grandma's car and we were driving it so my grandma wouldn't and because we couldn't afford a car of our own. grandma always found the keys, no matter where they were hidden and she'd go driving and it wasn't safe. i wonder if the road brought everything back to her too.

text: From power brokers to protesters: NYC landlords prepare for battle under Mamdani "An entire industry that once bankrolled mayors and governors — and wielded weighty influence in City Hall and Albany — is on the defensive, scrambling to prove it still has clout in a city that just elected a far-left mayor with a controversial and costly policy agenda. For decades, New York’s landlords were power brokers, not protesters. But after years of losses in Albany and at the ballot box, the industry has been increasingly sidelined — and Mamdani’s victory is the starkest indication yet of that reversal of fortune. The mayor-elect is gearing up for an unprecedented step: freezing rents for up to four years across nearly one million rent-stabilized apartments, which house about two million people — or roughly a quarter of the city’s population. Mamdani and his allies argue the dramatic move — which would be carried out through a mayor-appointed board — is needed to help renters stay afloat in an increasingly unaffordable city. Landlords are scrambling to stop him: pondering legal action, hoping lame-duck Mayor Eric Adams can kneecap Mamdani for a year or two with last-minute appointments, and sounding the alarm that many rent-regulated buildings are on the verge of collapse. Some are banking on the expectation that, eventually, conditions in their buildings will get bad enough that policymakers will be forced to act in their favor."

text: Z ohran Mamdani says Iran war speaks to a “broken kind of politics” | Newsmakers April 17, 2026 Through the first 100 days of his time as mayor of New York City, Zohran Mamdani has been focused on “pothole politics” -- a blend of high-level legislative wins and intense focus on delivering the everyday essential services that keep the city running. EXTRA Nostalgic Lookback: The Big Apple + Broadway Oh and speaking of shows performed live on stage. This one's kind of cheating since it only happened in 2021 (it's going to be five years by November next year!), but I had my one and only visit to New York City early that November, which also became my one and only attendance of a show on Broadway. It was Disney's Aladdin , and the single solitary reason I needed to see it can be given as a name: Johnathan Freeman . This was during his extended final period of playing Jafar, the role he originated in voice acting in the original animated movie, after he'd been set back by the pandemic shutting down Broadway in 2020. By early 2022, he stepped down from the role and passed it on to another actor. So this was a very freakishly fortuitous open window for me: I got to see Freeman play Jafar during his final Broadway run. I got to be right there in the same building, the same room in the same place at the same time, as the man who voiced one of my all-time favorite villains since childhood. It was absolutely surreal but a phenomenal and joyous day for me. Would not care to see New York City again - huge, populated urban areas dominated by an overwhelming amount of skyscrapers really isn't my thing - but it was worth that one day just to enjoy the show.

text: GORDON PARKS The Atmosphere of Crime (1957) When Life magazine asked Gordon Parks to illustrate a recurring series of articles on crime in the United States in 1957, he had already been a staff photographer for nearly a decade, the first African American to hold this position. Parks embarked on a six-week journey that took him and a reporter to the streets of New York, Chicago, San Francisco and Los Angeles. Unlike much of his prior work, the images made were in color. The resulting eight-page photo-essay “The Atmosphere of Crime” was noteworthy not only for its bold aesthetic sophistication, but also for how it challenged stereotypes about criminality then pervasive in the mainstream media. They provided a richly-hued, cinematic portrayal of a largely hidden world: that of violence, police work and incarceration, seen with empathy and candor. Parks rejected clichés of delinquency, drug use and corruption, opting for a more nuanced view that reflected the social and economic factors tied to criminal behavior and a rare window into the working lives of those charged with preventing and prosecuting it. Transcending the romanticism of the gangster film, the suspense of the crime caper and the racially biased depictions of criminality then prevalent in American popular culture, Parks coaxed his camera to do what it does best: record reality so vividly and compellingly that it would allow Life’s readers to see the complexity of these chronically oversimplified situations. The Atmosphere of Crime, 1957 includes an expansive selection of never-before-published photographs from Parks’ original reportage. Co-published with The Gordon Parks Foundation and The Museum of Modern Art https://steidl.de/Books/The-Atmosphere-of-Crime-1957-0017233350.html

text: did not realize my last post went through so I wrote this one. has now been edited to take out me telling the same story twice. every time I go to Chinatown I come back full of life. I get my hair cut down off of Grand St. and it's a long way but worth it. you get off the Grand St. station and everyone is very grumpy and rushed but in the way locals in immigrant enclaves are, they are getting shit done and not in the mood to cater to anyone else. it's very relaxing, to not be remotely important to anyone who sees you. they have no opinion on you except keep it moving. you do not need to try to relate or be nice. just get going. also that station's platforms are absurdly narrow and I think if that was my local station I would spend all day every day wanting to push people on the tracks. but maybe I'm projecting. on the street, nothing is designed for your comfort. it's not trendy or cute. it's small fish markets and cash only. it's smells you don't recognize out of tiny grocery stores selling specific ingredients. this is people's home and you better respect it. you can go into any restaurant and order anything and if they're a little rude the food is probably better. anyway after sandwich adventures and my haircut I passed a cheese store (Italian; NYC's Chinatown has merged with Little Italy into a zone of deliciousness) and then an Italian bakery where I became the rude New Yorker hovering impatiently for some girls to stop taking a selfie right where the line started. then a couple who had just gotten married came in, wedding dress and bouquet and boutonniere and all. going out for dessert after your wedding sounds better than going to McDonald's in a wedding dress, but both are iconic moves. after that came dumplings and the excellent Floridian family thennn I made it home despite some mishaps but stopped in the grocery store and they had a Dominican raisin cake which. okay. I had a dream about raisin cake a couple nights ago. I have never eaten raisin cake nor seen it in the store. and there it was, staring at me, exactly what I dreamed. ?? anyway I bought it. and I fucking love this town.

text: ALT Hey NY-ers, did you know we don't yet have a law on the books protecting our school libraries? Across the US, pro-censorship groups have made concerted attacks on school library systems in an effort to erase books they don't like from shelves, especially books that feature Black, queer, and trans characters. There IS a bill addressing this sitting on the New York governor's desk, the Freedom to Read Act (S.1099/A.7777), but she hasn't signed it yet. If you're local to NYC, join me and a bunch of orgs who are sick of waiting for her to pick up a pen and let Hochul know this needs to get done now.

Zohran Mamdani taps trans rabbi for mayoral transition team - LGBTQ Nation Faefyx Collington (They/Them) at LGBTQ Nation: New York City Mayor-elect Zohran Mamdani has appointed Abby Stein, a trans woman and Rabbi, to his expansive transition team. The choice – in line with many of Mamdani’s promises – has upset some folks on the right. Before being appointed to the transition team, Stein supported his mayoral bid, appearing in his “Jews for Zohran” campaign. “We know Zohran will fight to make our city affordable and safe for our families,” Stein said in an ad. ”As Jews, as rabbis, as New Yorkers, we believe that all people deserve to thrive. Zohran agrees.” [...] Stein was raised in an Orthodox Hasidic Jewish family. After suppressing her gender identity since childhood, she left the Hasidic community in 2012 and came out as trans in 2015. [...] Stein has made headlines both for her coming out story and her activism, which is primarily related to her support for Palestine and her condemnation of Israel’s genocide in Gaza. Her beliefs led to her being kicked out of the White House Pride Party in 2024 . Some right-wingers are, predictably, not happy about Stein’s inclusion on Mamdani’s transition team, which he claims includes more than 400 experts across 17 committees. Conservatives have taken issue both with her gender identity and with her pro-Palestine position. However, some supportive voices have pointed out that those are hardly negatives for Mamdani’s stances. This is a based move from NYC Mayor-elect Zohran Mamdani (D). Mamdani taps pro-Palestine trans rabbi Abby Stein to serve on his transition team, causing furor from right-wing, anti-trans, and pro-Israel apartheid zealots.

Live In NYC 1979 by Stare Kits 11/30/25. Stare Kits were a band active in the late 1970s no-wave NYC scene. They apparently arose at the same time at Ut (who opened for Stare Kits and later played shows with The Fall and The Birthday Party). While the Stare Kits themselves only played three shows, their members played in other projects. Angela Jaeger was in Disconnection. Amy McMahon (later Amy Rigby) was in The Shams and later married Wreckless Eric. Michael McMahon joined his sister in Last Roundup in the mid 1980s According to the Stare Kits' Bandcamp page , the band "embraced various musical influences such as The Slits, Buzzcocks, Jefferson Airplane and Wire. Feeding Tube Records released this just a few weeks ago and it serves as an excellent document of a snapshot of a music scene from nearly 50 years ago.

US Justice Department Launches Probe Into Anti-Israel Mob Targeting New York City Synagogue by Corey Walker The US Justice Department has started an investigation into a gathering of demonstrators who called for violence against Jews outside a prominent New York City synagogue last Wednesday night, according to a senior official. “Investigation is underway. [The Justice Department] has zero tolerance for violence/obstruction around any American house of worship,” Harmeet Dhillon, assistant attorney general for civil rights at the department, announced on social media on Sunday. ALT View on Twitter Dhillon’s comments came amid ongoing furor over last week’s protest, where demonstrators harassed those attending an event being held by Nefesh B’Nefesh, a Zionist organization that helps Jews immigrate to Israel, at Park East Synagogue in Manhattan. Protesters were recorded screaming obscenities to event attendees and blocking the entrance into the synagogue. “We don’t want no Zionists here!” the group of roughly 200 anti-Israel activists chanted in intervals while waving the Palestinian flag. “Resistance, you make us proud, take another settler out.” One protester, addressing the crowd, reportedly proclaimed, “It is our duty to make them think twice before holding these events! We need to make them scared.” Footage on social media also showed agitators chanting “death to the IDF,” referring to the Israel Defense Forces, as well as “globalize the intifada” and “intifada revolution.” Community figures described the scene as openly threatening and a stark escalation of anti-Jewish hostility in New York City. “It’s a federal crime to block access to a house of worship in the US. @CivilRights under @AGPamBondi will NOT tolerate it and we are gathering information about this incident!” Dhillon posted on X/Twitter on Friday, two days before announcing the department was investigating the incident. Jessica Tisch, commissioner of the New York City Police Department (NYPD) , addressed the demonstration while speaking to Park East Synagogue on Saturday. Tisch, who is Jewish, cited the demonstrators’ rights to assemble, protest, and speak freely but also apologized for allowing “turmoil” to take place.

text: Recess Monitor I have zero clue if this is a thing everywhere or just my elementary school. The elementary school I went too was kinder through 6th. Kindergarten was half day, you were either a morning kid or an afternoon kid. There were also two separate playgrounds, the one was for k-3 and the other was 4th-6th. I was okay at school, didn't get in trouble or anything, and one year I was asked if I would be interested in participating in a program where you would get to leave class to help watch the younger kids at a recess. I agreed. I only got to do it one time, idk if that was normal or they decided I was too much. I also don't know of anyone else who ever did it but it's not like I had tons of friends or talked to kids in the other classes really. So I get assigned a day and my teacher let me know when I could leave to go to the little kids playground. I get out there, say hi to the teacher who was watching the recess and then start talking to my friends little sister. She was third grade I think and I was sixth. She was telling me how she wanted to do the monkey bars but she could barely reach them even after climbing the little ladder part and couldn't figure out how to move forward. I sympathized because I have never been able to do monkey bars, if I did manage to reach I couldn't figure out how to swing myself forward so would get to the second bar at best. I have probably mentioned it before but I was pretty strong as a kid. I could pick up and carry 50 lb feed bags, dog food, chicken food, cat food, different grains for the horses. I could also lift and move hay-bales they were around 60lbs. I was also fiercely annoyed when teachers would ask for help from a strong boy because I am not exaggerating when I say the boys were much weaker than I was and I would often ignore the direct word of boys and do the shit myself. I literally carried 25 textbooks by myself that the teacher made two of us go get because a girl couldn't carry that much. My friend went with me and didn't want to be embarrassed by not having carried them so I gave him less than half when we got to the door of the classroom so he wouldn't be seen as weak which is stupid in my opinion but anyway he struggled carrying 8 from the door to the teachers desk. I had carried the whole 25 from past the office, out that building and up the ramp to where our classroom was like at least triple the distance he carried the 8. Still makes me so angry that teachers would always try to not let me help because I was girl. Like even after I did things alone that the boys were doing in pairs or more the teachers still would ask for strong boys like fuck you all and fuck your bullshit gender roles. (Probably one of the hardest things with the fibro is losing a lot of my strength, I can no longer lift bales and food bags and stuff and it's very hard to accept that I need help. Probably because I often felt the only reason I had any value to others was being able to help with things but that's not the point here.) So the reason I add the above tangent is because I told my friends sister that if she wanted I could help her do the monkey bars. She was like how? I was like hey you climb up, grab the bar and I'll get my shoulder under you and put my arms around your legs so you can grab and then whenever you want I'll let go. She repeatedly asked me if I was sure. (I do know that it's because she was fat, so was I well technically so am I but anyways I knew she was worried about being too heavy). I said yup, I'll let you know if I can't do it so you can be prepared for me to move away, that finally got her to agree. I will tell you now that there was no way I had any plans of saying anything even if she was too heavy for me. So she climbed up, grabbed the first bar and stepped off the ladder part, I got under her just like I said and started waking forward. She kept asking if I was okay. I assured her I was and I wasn't lying. She asked what if I'm never ready for you to let go, I told her that was fine. So I walked while she grabbed the bars, I made sure not to rush her, or walk too fast and she was so happy to reach the other side. As soon as I let her go at the end so she could get down another little kid asked me if I could do the same for her. I was like heck yeah of course I can. Once the kids realized I was serious I pretty quickly had a line of kids waiting for me to help them. The teacher came over after the about the fifth kid I was helping and told me that I didn't need to do that, that I could just watch the kids and make sure they weren't fighting or doing something dangerous. The kids waiting heard her and you could see their faces drop. (I realize now that maybe she was concerned about me, an 11 yr old kid, supporting the weight of kids that were probably past the age that many of their parents were willing to pick them up. Though also the kids were mostly supporting themselves.) I was pretty angry at her for that. We were all having a good time, the kids were not fighting or doing dangerous things in line so I was just thinking gosh how rude is she. I told her I was good and that if I got to tired I would stop. I did not stop until the recess ended. All the kids were so delighted to finish the monkey bars and many of them got back in line. It was girls and boys and I have no idea if that was the teacher's issue or, now as an adult, if she was genuinely concerned. I also do not know if that is why I never got to do it again or if it was just a one time per student thing. I thought things went well. The day after my friend told me how happy and excited his sister was, that she hadn't quit talking about it all night. I did later employ that skill with almost all of my nibblings at parks we took them too. I really think it did help them learn, the ones I babysat the most were all able to monkey bars on their own before they even started school.

text: My grandad told me the story about when he was younger, him and his friends lived really close to the Canadian/American border. Because of this, sometimes him and his buddies would drive into the states and hang out at collage parties(they were also collage aged at the time). One of their favourite things to do while at these parties was play a game where they tried to come up with the most ridiculous story the Americans would actually believe about Canada. One night, one of the Americans asked them about a hole in their windshield(a rock hit it), and my grandad responded with “oh yeah, someone thought we were a buffalo and shot at us. We need to shoot at least one buffalo a year when they’re walking by town, because that’s all the meat we get for the year.” And you know what? They bought it!!! They actually believed that we lived off of one buffalo a year that we had to shoot our selves… it would be more funny if that complete lack of critical thinking wasn’t horrifying. Moral of the story; it’s been like this for actual generations, and everyone ignored the warning signs instead of intervening. I’m so sorry for all the actually intelligent people stuck in a place like that.

An open letter from Stephen Miller's cousin Alisa Kasmer, shared by Rebecca Solnit. "A preface: I have debated about sharing this. A week ago, after the ICE raids in Camarillo, I had the worst panic attack I’ve experienced in over 30 years. (Shoutout to Zoloft for holding it together. F off, RFK, Jr.) Hours of sobbing, shaking, nausea, and complete loss of control into the early morning hours, led me to do the only thing that ever brings some clarity: write. The next day I started to write, while still reeling from the emotional hangover. A few days later, I was struck with another panic attack, this time as I was driving (thankfully close enough to home to make it back in one piece.) This sent me back into a spiral of heartbreak. I picked up where I had left off, and kept writing. Many of you know who my cousin is. Being public about it is something I’ve struggled with. I live with real fear about what posting something this raw might bring. I am living with the deep pain of watching someone I once loved become the face of evil. But I know that staying silent only deepens the ache. There’s so much more I could say, and maybe someday I will. It’s a long read. I’m wordy AF. Letter below the cut. ----- Last night, I found myself in a stage of grief I didn’t even realize I had been carrying. A grief that’s been living inside me for years—quiet, but constant. It comes from being so close to the root of something violent and vile in this country. I cried until I couldn’t breathe, hours of sobbing, gasping, shaking, sick to my stomach with a weight in my chest that was too heavy to fight. I was having a panic attack I couldn’t escape. Maybe it was ten years of anger and pain finally breaking through the surface. Maybe it was the most recent ICE raids turning my rage into sorrow. Whatever it was, something in me cracked wide open and has shaken me to my core. I think many of us are grieving. Grieving a world that feels more cruel than kind. A future that feels further away each day. I grieve for the country we could be… one with unmatched wealth, intelligence, and potential. A nation with resources to ensure everyone lives with dignity, equity, health, and safety. A nation with enough technological and medical advancements to be something truly extraordinary. But instead, those resources and that wealth are being hoarded by a few, poisoned by ego and power, devoid of empathy, starving the rest. Our privilege has been wasted on cruelty and torture, targeting the very people who make our communities whole—the hardest workers, the most vulnerable, the ones who carry this country on their backs. A society is only as strong as its most vulnerable, and ours are at their weakest. This is not by accident, but by design. Your design, Stephen. Then there’s the grief I carry inside my own family- the most personal and painful. I grieve a cousin I once loved. A boy I watched grow up, babysat, and shared a childhood with. The kid I made fun of for his obsession with Michael Jackson and Ghostbusters. The awkward, funny, needy middle child who loved to chase attention, yet was always the sweetest with the littlest family members. A kid that reminded me of Alex P. Keaton, young, conservative, maybe misguided, but lovable and harmless. Or so I thought. But I was so deeply wrong. And the realization that I didn’t know you at all? It guts me. I grieve what you’ve become, Stephen. And I grieve what I’ve lost because of it. I grieve your children I will never meet. I grieve the future family you’ve stolen from me by choosing a path so filled with cruelty that I cannot, and will not, be a part of it. I will never knowingly let evil into my life, no matter whose blood it carries—including my own. I grieve for the power you’ve been given and for those around you who have enabled it. I grieve for the family I once loved, who lifted me up, who helped me through life, who made me feel safe, who now leave me feeling unsettled and even afraid. I grieve the realization that maybe I never really knew these people at all. My heart breaks every day, over and over. But most of all, I grieve for those directly harmed by your actions For the communities here in Los Angeles, our shared home, for all of California, and the rest of the country terrorized by the cruelty you have brought upon us all. I grieve for the families shattered by cruelty dressed up as “immigration policy.” Targeting hardworking, vibrant community members who are being terrorized for simply being brown. This was never about criminals. Or “illegal” entry. And now, with the passing of this bloated, grotesque bill—stuffed with more funding for ICE than most countries spend on their entire military, I’m left speechless. Where does this hateful obsession end? What are you trying to build besides fear? Immigrants were a part of your upbringing. Is this cruelty your way of rejecting a part of yourself? People always ask me, “What happened to you?” I don’t have a clear answer. I can only surmise it was a perfect storm of ego, fear, hate, and ambition—all of it mangled into something cruel and hollow, masquerading as strength. You were born into privilege, into safety, and wealth. And somehow, you’ve weaponized all of it. I didn’t see the descent until it was too late. And now I’m left with guilt and shame. Could I have done something? My sister recently asked me, “If social media had existed back then—if we had seen the horrific videos of you in high school, would we have spoken up? Would we have intervened?” Yes, we absolutely would have. I grieve that we never got that chance. And here’s where it hurts even more: we were raised Jewish. Stephen, you and I both know what that means. We were raised with stories of survival. We learned about pogroms, ghettos, the Holocaust—not just as history, but as part of our identity. We carry the trauma of generations who were hunted, hated, expelled, murdered, just for existing. We were taught to remember. We celebrated holidays each year with the reminder to stand up and say “never again.” But what you are doing breaks that sacred promise. It breaks everything we were taught. How can you do to others what has been done to us? How can you wake up each day and repeat the cruelty that our people barely escaped from? We were taught to never forget where we came from. But you seem to have erased it all. And it devastates me. To be this close to the cruelty, through you, has left me ashamed and shattered. I try to fight your harm in every way I can. But it will never be enough. I can’t undo what you’ve done or who you have become. I can’t outmatch your reach or power. I feel helpless. The panic attacks haven’t stopped since the grief cracked open. The tears won’t stop, and the weight on my chest is constant. This isn’t about politics. This is about humanity. About decency. And you have lost yours. You’ve destroyed so many lives just to feed your own obsession and ego and uphold an administration so corrupt, so vile, I can barely comprehend it. As surreal as it all feels, this IS reality. As much as I try to disassociate from it, the truth remains—being this close to such deep cruelty fills me with shame. I am gutted. My heart breaks that this is the legacy you have brought to our family. A legacy I never asked to share with you, and one I now carry like a curse."

text: So, there's some background to be given for this one: • I have five little cousins that are 3-10 years old, all who have NEVER interacted with the Internet or other kids outside of their church and homeschool co-op. • They're also very country. These boys spend ALL day outside, going on adventures. Which is freaking awesome. • Cities and technology to them is a time machine to a medieval peasant. • Every December, my grandparents have a big Christmas gathering at their house. So this past year, I was at our family Christmas, and I'm from one of the hottest states in the US- so we had NO snow. It was like 90 degrees F or somethin. Meaning, all creatures normally dormant or less active during winter, are awake and thriving. That includes ants. So, my country-hick kid cousins play outside in the yard, as they usually do, and I don't really think anything of it. I have WAY more cousins than them, all under the age of 11, so I was happy not to be babysitting that little posse. Apparently, someone should have been keeping a better eye on them, because suddenly the two oldest kids are in the door, one looking guilty. Kid A: "Mama, can you help us?" Cousin (their mother): "What's wrong?" Kid A: "We can't get the ants out of B's hair." Silence. Cousin, rightfully concerned about her son: "Did he fall? Is he okay??" Kid A: "No, I put them there. I grabbed a chunk of the ant pile in my hands and dumped it on him." Kid B: "Yeah, it's just a little bit itchy." Of course I started to laugh my ass off, as did other relatives, but Cousin and her husband were PANICKING. Kid B was literally completely okay with it, as if he hadn't just been stung on the scalp by 50± ants. Ladies, gentlemen, and ants: average encounter.

When my great great aunt died, A, my grandmother, her ostensible caretaker, was left with a few pieces of furniture and some jewelry in the will. Well, Edward, her son, A's cousin (she helped raise him), decided he wanted that stuff. Demanded it, A refused, because it was the wish of her beloved (genuinely beloved) aunt, and in her will. Courts got involved. Well, E worked for Hoover's FBI (had a signed Merry Christmas 1948 photo and everything), and thought that would give him some traction in the RI court system. It...did not. He lost spectacularly in the first two minutes of the case when he told the judge he hadn't visited his mother in five years. A never spoke to him again, the entire extended family (quite extensive) blackballed him, and then A went home to the photo album and burned out all pictures of his face with a cigarette except for one, of him as a child, sitting with her on a fence, it's a gorgeous hand colored photo. She kept it to "remind herself that evil exists." A also forbade anyone in the family from naming their offspring Edward, and that's held. *Fun side story: I found a footlocker full of photos my grandfather and great uncle's service in WWII. At the bottom was a bag with more photos of this guy nobody recognized, in the Navy, the South Pacific, not ugly by a longshot, but nobody could place him...until we showed it to my mom's elder sister. She pinned him as Edward immediately and suggested we burn the rest of the photos on principle. I didn't, and that's how I ended up with a signed photo of J. Edgar Hoover (who was a bastard and is rotting in Hell). Anyhow, celebrate the fuckers and vile folks in your life dying and leaving your life for good and all.

text: From the New Yorker: Until recently, Jessica Winter knew little about her grandfather Earl, who died when she was 12. On an evening last summer, she was procrastinating while writing a piece that involved research on genealogical websites, and, on a whim, she began punching her grandparents’ names into search bars. She came across her grandfather’s name in the 1910 U.S. census, listed as an “Inmate,” age six, of the Home for the Friendless. It was an orphanage in Erie, Pennsylvania. “Charles Dickens himself could not have conjured up a more mordant name,” she writes. Winter remained at her laptop until three that morning, ransacking every digital filing cabinet she could locate, trying to figure out how Earl had lost his family and wound up in such a place. Her discovery sucked her into an endless feed of Progressive Era newspaper archives and Secret Service ledgers. “This fixation was catalyzed, at least in part, by the suspicion that I’d already possessed some unconscious knowledge of Earl’s fate,” she writes. Years ago, she wrote a novel about a mother who adopts the youngest of her four children from an orphanage—a feat that required Winter to immerse herself in the literature on the neurological, psychological, and social-emotional effects of child neglect, disrupted attachment, and institutional child care. “I often wondered why I was drawn to these desolate stories of cruelty and abandonment,” she writes. text: Ugh I’ve been having this weird hip pain for the past three weeks, and I still don’t know what’s causing it. It actually makes me think about how all the women in my family get these terrible leg cramps, at completely random times. No precedent, no pattern, ever. My mom has it the worst, and I grew up with her screaming and collapsing at random times, like someone just cut her strings or something. I didn’t experience one myself until I was about 13, where I just woke up in the middle of the night feeling like my leg was being wrenched from its socket and shoved back in. I started screaming because it was terrifying, and my dad woke up to try and calm me down, though it was a good five minutes before it actually went away. It happens to my sisters too, and I’ve heard the same about my grandma and my aunt. Maybe it’s normal? I dunno, I never hear anyone else talk about it. It just kind of spooks me out.

So, I found out yesterday that my aunts best friend since high school died because of the american health insurance system. I mean, she HAS been dead for over 7 years at this point and I was probably told this when it happened, but it came up yesterday because my mom and I went to walk through the old cemetary across the street from my parents house. It's mostly disused and more of a walled park than a proper cemetary, now, there can't be burials in the ground, that stopped like 50 years ago, but some families still hold the plots and put urns there. Most of the graves are from between the late 1800s and WW2, but there are a handful of plots with people who died recently (the newest one we saw was from last winter). And it turns out, this woman was burried there, because her family owned one of the plots. Mom was really taken aback when she realized this, and we're planning to go light a candle for her. So, I asked about the story. This woman, let's call her Annie, was my aunt's best friend in high school and after graduation, because it was also right after the velvet revolution and travelling was finally a thing, she went to work as an au pair in the US, and she loved it and convinced my aunt to do the same thing, starting a set of dominos that lead my aunt to the life she lives today. Annie ended up moving to the US permanently some time in the late 90s and eventually got married and started a family there (with another Czech immigrant, which is irrelevant, but kinda funny). But they were not exactly well off, not for the US. They had okay jobs and were earning more than they would in equivalent jobs back here, but one thing they didn't have was health insurance (or at least not a particularly good one), so, like many Americans, unless they were actively dying, the didn't go to the doctor much. Eventually, Annie did go to the doctor with some issues and it turned out she had cervical cancer, and pretty far along, too. I think she returned here for treatment, but she ended up dying within the year of getting the diagnosis. She was 45 and left behind two pretty young children (I don't know the exact ages, but I think she had kids close to the same time as my aunt did, so those kids are probably in their teens now). It was a big shock to my aunt, because she had a very similar life story to this friend (she had been living on-and-off in the US since the 90s and both of her husbands were czechoslovak immigrants as well). The difference being that my aunt's second husband is in a very high income bracket (he's a regional executive for a big car company), so she was able to be a stay-at-home mom for their two children until they were both double digits of age (and only really got a job after that because she was bored) and didn't have to worry about going broke if a medical situation happened, even when they lived in the US. But Annie didn't marry rich. She just wanted to make her life a little better, she pursued the damn american dream, because she believed being middle-class in the US was better than being middle-class in central europe. And maybe in the 90s, that was true. And now she's dead, from an entirely preventable and treatable illness, because couldn't afford to go to a doctor regularly. Annie's mom died two years after her, they're burried next to each other, now. Her mom watched her daughter follow her dreams, live a life that she never could have had, being born behind the Iron Curtain as she was, life that shouldn't have been any more dangerous than staying here, and she had to watch her die for that mundane, seemingly safe, dream. There's no point to this rambling. I just wanted to tell that story, because it's sad and fucked up. I know yall's healthcare system is the least of your worries, now, but damn it's all so rage inducing. This woman died because she bought the lie America was selling. And now her children will soon live longer without her than they had with her, and they're not even out of high school yet.

text: Vaggie: (telling even more family stories) When my mother forgets a word, she is the queen of coming up with new words; words that would take a third National Treasure movie to fully decipher. I was talking to her one time when I got home from college, and she said this: "You know, the time for 'Los Jibbities' is coming up, you must be so excited!" (the crew laughs) Oh, is it time for "Los Jibbities" already? I must have missed it on my calendar. (the crew laughs again) Are we celebrating something? "Of course, we should all be celebrating, shouldn't we?" Okay, so "Los Jibbities" is a happy time?
