text: On superstitions (and how they can become): When my grandparents were first married, my grandma always used to cut off the spare little bit of a lamb leg roast and throw it out before cooking it. My granddad asked why, and my grandma replied "Because that's just how it is done." When pressed, she said: "It's how my mother does it." So the next time they were visiting with family, my granddad asked, and recieved the same answer - "That's just how it is done; I learned it from my mother." Eventually, they went to visit my grandmother's maternal grandmother. My granddad, not expecting an answer, asked his question about the lamb roast. "Oh yes," says my great-great-grandma "I cut it off because otherwise the roast wouldn't fit in the pan I had."

Today on learning your family is weird: apparently other people’s mothers aren’t basically Roboute Guilliman in mascara. I still remember evenings she would sit down in front of the laptop going through how much dad had spent and how much he had taken from the bank and how much money he had left. And then she would let him know if we were on target or if he needed to be careful and for how long. Carefully updating the spending records. Money for anything nice was always budgeted for well in advance and set aside. Add to that she had Tarasha Euten’s spine of ceramite and Rogal Dorn’s sheer stubbornness. Perfect organizer. I’m sure she would have run the Imperium as well as the Avenging Accountant. With added verbal crucifixions for anyone who needed it. (She was an accountant too, so that is an affectionate nickname.) In a fight between her and Roboute I would bet on mom. Women like her or Tarasha are his kryptonite. Dad on the other hand was more like a Salamander with Jago Sevatarion’s dedication and sense of humor. I have joked if I killed somebody he would have shrugged, said they probably deserved it, and helped hide the body. Sevatar’s confrontation with Altani’s bully is actually based on something he did in RL. With those examples, no wonder I have a little trouble reading Sevatar as 100% masculine at times. He’s too good at herding the VIII. Dad was ok at noticing things, but I’ve known too many men who would only be able to find the trash to take it out if you stapled it to their nose. Who could be standing in front of a thing and complain they couldn’t find it.

text: So I wanna share a story .In around 1959 or 1960, a nursing student was begged by a friend to join her on a double date,the friend was seeing her fiance who brought a friend of his own,and reluctantly the nursing student went .The nursing student met this kind of quiet nerdy guy ,and she wasnt impressed .The four went to a movie (Either starring Marilyn Munroe or Bridgette Bardot ,no party could recall ) ,and it became quite apparent to the nursing student and the nerd ,this was just an excuse for the couple to make out .So here were two people who reallly didnt want to be there ,next to a couple fully under the power of Aphrodite ,and to top it the movie was pretty bad .So with nothing really to do the nerd and the nursing student just started mocking the film making fun of it the whole way through.They actually got compliments from folks who felt their commentary was more entertaining then the film .Well while it wasnt a great date they had fun but the pair went their seprate ways and that seemed to be that ....Sometime later,the Nerdy guy called the Nursing Student ,he had some tickets for a football game,didnt have a steady girlfriend, but rembered the nursing student and wanted to invite her. the Nursing Student didnt have anything better to do ,so she accepted and they went on a date....Which lead to another .....And another and by the end of 61 the pair were married ,and remained so for 51 years The nursing student was my grandma and the nerdy guy was my grandfather .I got premission to share this charming story ,hope you enjoyed @the-blue-fairie @themousefromfantasyland @ariel-seagull-wings @theancientvaleofsoulmaking @princesssarisa @countesspetofi

Interested in genealogical or archival research? Want to know what killed someone back before modern medical practices? Then this is the chart for you. Below are some bills of mortality from London (mostly in the 1600s) One of my great great uncles had a cause of death listed as "postvan." I was wondering what that was an abbreviation for, since it wasn't one I was used to seeing. Turns out he was one of the first people in County Clare to be killed by a postal van in like 1912, so there's that. A friend of mine lost four of his grandfather's brothers to jumping off the same train at different times (their house was near the tracks but a seven mile walk from the station). His grandfather "survived WWI but lost a foot jumping off the same train in 1919" and that was after it had already killed two of his brothers. I always like to imagine that "Kil'd by several accidents" means they died in the most slapstick, Three Stooges manner possible.

This is the cup that Mother (born in 1914) used as a little girl. By the time she showed it to me, it had a small crack that dated from her own childhood. In our house, Mother kept it on a shelf in the corner cupboard in the dining room. She told me that she was concerned that there might be germs in that crack, so only once or twice, when I was very little, she'd fill it and let me take a few sips from it. It gradually worked its way to the back of the shelf and I don't think that any of my younger brothers and sister got to use it. Daddy used to tell us that when he was a little boy, each morning his Mother served him oatmeal for breakfast. He told us that he would eat it as fast as he could so that he could see the picture that the cereal was covering in the bottom of the bowl. He told this story so well that we kids could almost share his excitement until one of us asked, "How many different bowls did you have?" "Well, just the one," Daddy replied. "You mean you were that excited to discover the same picture, morning after morning?" We couldn't believe what we had just heard. "Well, yes," he replied, "I guess I was." After his reluctant disclosure he still told the story, but not nearly so often. . .

To be fair ... this was what my father did by joining the Jesuits and it got him a standard college education, law degree, MA, and PhD...and then he realized the Jesuits just weren't quite the place for him, despite the Vietnam War still being very much on--he was a conscientious objector and would definitely have been locked up if drafted. He advised me to consider doing the same when I went off to college, and I'll admit I thought about it... But the celibacy thing did not appeal to me, so... I'm deeply in debt but also deeply in other things as well.

Family story time October—The inequity of birth order and the saga of Meyer’s Hill In 1969, my family lived—reluctantly--in South Bend, Indiana. The only person pleased to be there was my father, who had accepted the United States Air Force’s offer of an MBA from Notre Dame. My mother, my sisters and I resigned ourselves to living in a city that only seemed to have three seasons . . . August, Indian summer, and Blizzard. Having come from slightly milder climes, my sisters and I weren’t used to actual snow snow; the stuff that not only came down from the sky but also stayed on the streets and sidewalks instead of melting away by the afternoon. But South Bend got its fair share of Great Lakes humidity in the summer and more than its share in the winter. Before winter though, came the hints of chill as the seasons changed, and the pivot point was October. The Halloween season in South Bend lived up to the stereotypes, and I remember crunching my way through countless mounds of dry maple and oak leaves on my walks to school as the gnarled trees stretched long black claws up to the cloudy skies. Chill hung in the air, and the smell of damp and smoke clung to everything. Nearly everyone checked their window thermometers in the morning, and the old white plastic radio in the kitchen would call out the wind chill factors from exotic cities like Mishawaka and Kalamazoo. Sweater weather, as my mother would cheerfully tell me. Sweaters were fine, but not when coupled with Halloween costumes, a point I argued with my mother. She responded that I’d be getting pneumonia along with my treats, that being sick was no fun and did I really want to just stay home with her to pass out candy while Dad studied upstairs? I did not. Halloween was about freedom, and even though I was only nine, I’d heard enough about how crazy the night could be from friends at Muessel Elementary. Some had older brothers, so the rumors about smashed pumpkins, soaped windows and the dreaded notorious TP-ing abounded through the fourth grade. Everyone knew someone who’d been squashed, soaped or smeared, and I was dying to get out after dark to see some of this uncivilized behavior. Not do it mind you; just being witness would have been plenty to blow my little nine-year-old mind at the time. So I reluctantly agreed to wear a sweater with my costume. Money was tight that year, and it was generally understood by us girls that whatever costumes we managed would have to be home-made. I didn’t mind because mom had not only Yankee thrift in her bones but also a sense of whimsy that came out every now and then. She always put curly smiley faces at the end of her lunchbox notes and planted Morning Glories at nearly every house we lived at. She also had a generous closet and allowed us to rummage around as we decided what to make ourselves into for that year. About ten days before the big day, I dug around and decided I wanted to be a gypsy. (Note: I recognize that this term is an ethnic slur and am only using it to explain my costume at the time.) Mom couldn’t find a skirt long enough for me, so she picked up a few lengths of truly hideous fabric from the local Ben Franklin’s—a pattern called ‘Cornfield’ as I recall—and made a broom skirt out of the yellow and black cloth. It was garish so of course I adored it. I draped myself in scarves and cheap costume jewelry, feeling absolutely exotic. Heather, blonde, plump, and in the prime of girly-hood at age seven, wanted to be a princess. Mom found an old bridesmaid dress and pinned it up so my sister wouldn’t trip on the hem. They rolled a long tube of blue construction paper into a cone, and mom stapled a scarf on it to make a hat for her. It looked pretty cool if you didn’t notice her sneakers peeking out at the bottom and didn’t say anything about the plaid scarf around her shoulders. Dee was the problem. My youngest sister (at the time; Becky would arrive about six years later) was a picky little pain in the butt. She was perky, cute, and damned near inflexible about what she wanted back then. And what she wanted was to be . . . a pumpkin. A round pumpkin, not a skinny one, she announced. No putting her into an orange dress or leotard and trying to pass her off, no way. Dee wanted to be a fully round pumpkin and that was it. No cats or angels or Indians—pumpkin or bust, as it were. Mom was . . . stumped. Creative she might be, but there were limits. Putting Dee into an actual pumpkin wasn’t feasible, although the thought was incredibly tempting to me. Instead, my mother went through her stacks of Pack-O-Fun magazines looking for inspiration, well-aware that Halloween was fast approaching. Finally, she took it upon herself to make a Papier-mâché pumpkin. Since a standard balloon wasn’t nearly big enough to make a shape large enough for my sister to wear, mom shaped the body with chicken wire, rounding it, and leaving the top and bottoms open. She made this monstrosity in our basement, a dank and spooky part of the house that also housed among other things, my father’s woodworking bench, our designated toy and play area, as well as a dark and murky laundry corner, complete with ancient washer/wringer, and a root cellar that terrified us kids. Mom patiently laid layer after layer of Papier-mâché onto the wire, building what looked like a lumpy boulder entirely composed of old copies of the South Bend Tribune . In the dankness of the basement, it took forever to dry, and even after it did, it still smelled faintly of sour glue. Crafting was in Mom’s blood; although she was still a few years away from leading a Girl Scout troop, she already had made pipe-cleaner spiders and enormous string webs to decorate our porch. She’d supervised my father carving pumpkins (holding back her muttered fears that he was going to sever a vein in the process) and had them displayed along our cement steps. When it came to doing spooky, Mom was on it. Anyway, the pumpkin was barely dry in time for the big day, and matters weren’t helped by damp layers of spray paint. To this day I can still hear the clacking rattle of the ball within the can as mom shook it hard, and the heady smell of Krylon permeating the air, the aerosol drifting up the basement stairs. The shade of orange was what we would later recognize as ‘Safety Orange’ that universally recognized color most road construction crews would wear in later years. Down in the basement of 1136 Diamond Avenue, though, it seemed to glow in the dark, which made Dee love it all the more. The final fitting came the day before Halloween. My little sister, all of four feet tall with toothpick legs in green leotards carefully held her arms up as my mother slid the pumpkin frame on her. There were holes on each side, and Dee managed to fit her arms through them, but that’s when she realized the problem. “My arms are stuck!” The girth of the pumpkin was such that Dee’s arms were wedged through the holes just before the bend of her elbows, which meant they stuck out at right angles to her pumpkin body case. She was an orange globe with little forearms and hands sticking out. “Can you wiggle them?” Mom asked, and Dee did, flapping her palms and flexing her fingers. “Well okay then, you’ll be fine.” Prophetic last words, of course. Mom re-pinned up the front hem of Heather’s Princess dress so she wouldn’t trip, and then gave me the last-minute instructions as she did so. “Just around our block, that’s plenty of houses. If there’s no porch light on, don’t bother the people there and remember to say thank you!” “Okay,” I told her. “Stay together!” “Okay, okay—can we go ?” Patience was not my strong suit, and I wanted to hit those houses before all the good candy was gone. Mom sent us off and we went left, first to the Senasac’s house and then on to Mr. Shipman’s place. Once we left our porch, I led my sisters on the charge, hopping up steps and smacking doorbell buttons. Heather and I had pillowcases, the best and sturdiest of Trick or Treat bags while Dee had a smaller plastic pumpkin-shaped bucket she held by its thin black handle. She soon realized she couldn’t hold out her bucket unless she turned sideways, but the cuteness factor of her costume and little pumpkin brought out a lot of ‘awwws’ and much more candy into her bucket. “I’mma punkin!” she announced at every house, just in case someone couldn’t figure it out. We made our rounds, moving around the block, sometimes running into other groups of kids in their cowboy gear and ghost sheets. I remember seeing only one store bought costume the entire night—a boy in a Superman costume with one of those stiff plastic masks held in place with a gray elastic band stapled to its edges. I never liked those much—the eye and mouth holes were always too small and had sharp edges. They also made you sweat until you HAD to tip the mask up just to breathe. By the time we were on the backside of the block, Dee was starting to whine. “My pumpkin is heavy!” She didn’t mean her costume, although it probably was. Dee meant her treat bucket, which was nearly full and the weight of it made her tip a little sideways as she tried to keep up with us. “We’re almost done,” Heather assured her, and she was right—we were nearly at the Meyer’s house at the bottom of the block. Doctor Meyers had a big house at the end of the block, complete with a two car garage and a kennel for his hunting dog, Wally. The driveway was up an incline, so it meant trudging up to get to their front door, but it was absolutely worth it. The Meyers always set out a table with a huge bowl of candy and a sign that said, “help yourself to a piece.” Remarkably, most kids did that, only taking a single lollipop or roll of Lifesavers. We made it to the top, and I snagged a Butterfingers, which made it a great night for me. Heather took a Tootsie Pop and then Dee approached the bowl. She turned and dug with her free hand into the bowl, pulling up a strip of candy dots on paper. Now the problem was shifting it from her free hand to her pumpkin bucket, but Dee was smart for a little kid. She set the candy down on the table, turned the other way, and with her fingers, scooted the treat into her bucket. Even I was impressed, especially when she beamed at me. “Got it!” Groups were coming up behind us; the Meyers house was always a big stop on Halloween. We started back down, moving around a few groups, and about one third of the way . . . Dee tripped. A small person. in a wireframe ball. at the top of a hill. She gave a little yelp and while Heather rushed to help her, gravity moved first. Dee began to roll down the hill, towards the street, feet flailing as she picked up speed. “DEEEEEEEEE!” I ran after her bouncing orange self, trying to get in front of her and stop her rolling but it was dark, I was in a long skirt and all I could imagine was a horrific vision of my sister either being hit by a car, or rolling all the way down Diamond Avenue into the Portage Avenue intersection which was always full of cars. Terror had me moving faster than I ever have. Dee rolled and hit the curb just across from the Myer’s house, bouncing as the chicken wire met the cement edge, leaving a huge dent in the Papier-mâché pumpkin. Heather rounded on the other side of her, both of us practically in my kid sister’s face. “DON’T BE DEAD!” I shrieked at her. To be fair I was jacked up on adrenaline and fear at the time. Heather flinched, and Dee looked up at me, her eyes rolling a little. She started to huff. Those of you who’ve ever dealt with little kids know what this means. It’s the wind-up for a big cry; the warning that this person is about to BLOW, big-time. “Muh-muh-m-m-MYCAAAAAAAAAAAANDYYYYYYYY!!!” Dee erupted, her wail carrying through the night and making Wally howl in sympathy. “OhmanshutupshutupSHUTUP!” I hissed back as I helped her sit up, well-aware that kids were staring at us, and at any moment the Meyer’s door would open and THEN there’d be a grown-up involved. A grown-up who would undoubtedly call Mom. Dee was still crying, but Heather was looking around looking for the candy. She found the pumpkin bucket a few feet in the grass—the strap had snapped, but it was still about a third full. A couple of other kids rooted around the driveway and brought some of the spilled goodies back to us, dropping them into the pumpkin bucket and telling Dee she’d be okay. Dee was not buying that, not for a moment. She snuffled, stood up, and I could see by the streetlight that not only did she have a huge rip in her leotards at the knee, but she also had a massive dent in the side of her costume where the chicken wire had hit the curb. She thrust her little chin out and sniffled. “Wanna go home,” she pleaded, and so we headed for home. I knew I was dead meat. I just knew it. I walked as slowly as I could. Dee was slow because her knee hurt, and Heather was slow because she understood how bad this was. And me? I understood at nine how it felt to walk the kid’s version of the green mile. Mentally I was working hard on how best to present my case to mom, and feeling the dread grow with every step. As we got to our porch, mom opened the door for us and said, “So how did it OH MY GOD!! ” And that set Dee off again. She broke into fresh wails, sobbing with all the lung power of a Best Actress in a drama series. Heather slunk in and stood off to the side, avoiding all eye contact while mom rounded on me, finger out, pointing HARD. “You! You were IN CHARGE! YOU were supposed to LOOK OUT for your SiSTERS!” Mom yelled at me. At the rate she was jabbing that index my way it could have gone off at any minute. I sucked in a breath and launched into my wavery defense. “She fell and we tried to stop her but she ROLLED!” I yelled back, feeling the tears welling up. “It’s not my fault!” “Don’t give me that!” Mom snapped. “Muh candy is GONE IN THE BUSHES!” Dee wailed, and Mom glared at me. “Just for that, you give her YOUR candy!” she ordered. “What!?” I tightened my grip on my pillowcase. Oh the inhumanity! It wasn’t a lot, but it was MINE, and I wasn’t giving it up. Especially not to a bratty kid sister. Mom was going to yell again, but at that moment we all heard my father coming down the stairs. Slow, deliberate thumps that made us all look over at him. If we were so loud that Dad came out of his study, this was REALLY bad. We all went dead quiet. He stood a few steps up from the bottom, looking out over us in the living room, taking it all in before coming down and looking at Dee. “It’s a dangerous costume,” he rumbled. “What? No, it’s fine—” Mom interjected, but Dad just shook his head. “Not surprised she fell. You okay, Deelee?” She nodded. He looked at Mom. “Kids trip. She’s okay, and nobody died. Let it go , Kit.” Mom was still huffy, but she helped Dee out of the mangled pumpkin while Dad looked at me. “Give her . . . some of your candy,” he told me, and winked. There was no greater sense of relief than Dad’s wink. It was his way of letting you know he was on your side, and at that moment I loved him so much that I just darted over and hugged him while butting my head against his chest. He hugged me back, and things were going to be okay. We sat on the living room carpet and poured out our candy. I liked to sort it by type: lollypops, bars, jellybean packets in a heap. (I still do.) Heather went by type: chocolates together, fruit flavors off to the side. Dee dumped her bucket, and then filled it again, then dumped it out once more. I looked through my treats, trying to find the candy I didn’t mind giving away and then fished out a tube of Necco wafers. “Here, you can have these.” Heather gave her a few Dubble Bubble gum pieces, and a plastic whistle while Mom looked over everything and slyly confiscated a few of the Bit ‘O Honeys that ‘looked suspicious.’ “What happened?” she asked me quietly, coming to sit next to me on the carpet. I told her, with Heather nodding every now and then. Mom sighed. “Okay then. I guess it was bound to happen. You must have been scared.” “Not me,” Dee chirped up. “I want to do it again!” “No,” Mom told her. “No more pumpkins, no more rolling. Next year you can be a tree.” “A TREE?” Indignant now, Dee shook her head. “Trees are for the birds!” She didn’t get why we all laughed at that, but it was okay. She wasn’t dead, Mom and Dad weren’t mad at me, and I had a Butterfingers, so it was a pretty good Halloween, all told.

I went to see my gran this afternoon and she told me a few stories from her school years that had us crying with laughter. You see, my gran comes from a rich family from South Italy, like a silly rich family. And so, like all girls from a good family in her time, she went to the Catholic school, where all classes were given by nuns who never set foot out of their convent. Again, South Italy in the 50s. My gran and her classmates very much didn’t want to be there and worked for years to make the nuns’ life a living hell. A few examples of their pranks include: Cutting the rope to ring the bell as high as possible so, by the time the nuns changed it, the girls had had twice as long a break. Hiding in the bathroom and making the worse puking sounds possible. Drawing dicks on the walls and then looking utterly shocked and innocent when the nuns noticed. In the later years of school, the boyfriend of one of the girls called pretending to be the archbishop and said he would come to bless all the girls who were about to pass their exams. This sent all the nuns in a frenzy of preparations, herding the girls out of class early to put them in a row in the church and wait for the archbishop. All the girls were kneeling, head lowered piously in prayer to hide the fact they were actually shaking from laughter instead of nerves. Of course, the archbishop never came, the nuns worked themselves in a frenzy and just sent the girls back home. My gran and her friends had missed philosophy class and couldn’t be happier. One day, during religion class, a girl asked the priest, looking him dead in the eyes, how exactly the Holy Spirit had managed to get a virgin pregnant. She was sent kneeling in the corner for the rest of the hour. At the end of the class, my gran and her classmates went to the priest to give him a gift since it was the week before Christmas. My gran was crying with laughter over 60 years later when she described to me his look of dawning horror as he opened the paper wrapping and realised the book was the complete works of Karl Marx. He then grabbed his things and left the classroom banging the door closed behind him, and my gran never had another religion class in her school years. Later, when she got her college diploma (at the Catholic university of course), my gran went on a school trip in Greece. Her and one of her best friends pretended to be together, shamelessly holding hands and making lovestruck faces at each other, just to scandalise the teachers. They then proceeded to flirt with every single Greek boy they met during the whole trip, while still pretending to be in a relationship. Let’s say the teachers nearly had several aneurysms and my gran had the time of her life. I could go on for a while about my gran’s adventures but I’ll stop here before I end up with a full length novel.

text: *How to Make Alice's Dessert* "Who was Alice?" I asked my mother. She didn't know. "She was probably one of my mother's friends." she'd said, just to fill the space. Like so many childhood questions, answers only come when you are older. Pre-heat your oven to 180 Celsius or 350 Fahrenheit. Mix 2 tablespoons of butter with 2 tablespoons of sugar. Add 1/2 cup of flour and 1 1/2 teaspoons of baking powder. Add 1 cup fine coconut and 3/8 cup of milk. Blend ingredients. "Mother" was how my mother mostly referred to her own. She had a way of saying it that emptied all meaning from the word. "Mother" did not contain one ounce of mother, not even a drop of mothering. Place the batter in a 20cm (8 inch) Pyrex bowl. Pour over the top, while still hot, 1 1/4 cups brown sugar dissolved in 1 cup boiling water. Place in oven. "Mother" or Effie gave wet kisses and awkward hugs, and was seemingly unable to breach the titanium of her own containment vessel. The one day that she did, she managed to dispatch her wedding rings down the waste disposal and was later found on the front lawn, drunk and decrying the infidelities of her husband, my grandfather. Lack of loving gets passed down through the generations, they say. I like to think Alice was actually Saint Alice. Leprous, blind and nearly paralyzed, cooking up the miracle of Alice's Dessert for the Cistercian nuns of La Cambre Abbey, by feel and with love. I'd rather two servings of Alice's Dessert, prepared and plated by a leprous Cistercian lay sister destined for sainthood, any day, over a childhood without love. When the top of your batter begins to brown place tinfoil loosely over the bowl to stop it becoming too dark, and to slow the evaporation of the sauce. By 30 to 40 minutes your batter should be light, fluffy and coconutty, floating on a cauldron of sweetness. Take a knife and pierce it through its heart. If it comes out clean you're almost there. Now it's time for love. One Kindred Spirit

text: This is Avraham Lerch. He was born in Rozwadów in 1885. When he was young, his family moved to Budapest, where he was orphaned. He had two sisters, but I don't know their names. One of them moved to America, and the other died in Budapest. He married his wife Julia Teich in 1924, and they had seven children- Channa Gittel (Gita), Golda Rosa (Goldie), Sara (my great grandmother), Shmuel Yankel (Pento), Mirjam, Lea (Lili) and Pearla (who died shortly after her birth). They lived in Kosice in Slovakia, and their last name was Teich, since Avraham wasn't a Hungarian citizen. He worked as a janitor in a couple of schools, including the one his children studied in. He was very close with his children, and spent as much time as he could with them on Shabbat while Julia was resting. They didn't have much, but Avraham and Julia did their best to give the kids a happy childhood. In 1938 the war came knocking, and one day while Avraham was talking to his friend, the kids saw him crying. That's how they knew things were bad. Since he wasn't a Hungarian citizen, he was held in a camp in Budapest since 1940, and in August of 1941 he was murdered in the Kamenets Podolskiy massacre. After his death, Julia was left alone with six kids. Only Gita, Sara and Pento survived the holocaust. יהי זכרו ברוך.

text: This is Naftali Herman Grosz. He was born in Buj, Hungary in 1897. He fought in world war 1, thinking it would be the last. He was the Husband of Sara, and the father of Evie, my great grandpa Yehuda, and Clary. He was a merchant, and had 4 shoes and leather stores managed by Sara. His daughter Clary died when she was just a baby. He lived in Tótkomlós, and he was an important figure in the town council. He was religious, and wanted to get to Israel someday, but he was well off and busy with business. He sent his children to Jewish schools, and he wasn't too close to them. Before the war, he went bankrupt (since people won't pay their tab to a Jew), and one day a man broke into his house and threatened to kill him if he doesn't leave the town. He took the threat seriously, and the family moved to Budapest. They lived in a small apartment and Naftali tried regaining some of his money as a textiles merchant. They were close to the hassidic Jews in their area, and the children were part of Bnei Akiva. When the war started, he still wanted his children to have Jewish education, but the Melamed he hired was very openly Jewish, and one day he just didn't show up. They later learned that he was murdered in a concentration camp. Yehuda offered the family move to Buj, and Naftali used his status as a war vet to get on the train (where Jews were not allowed). The people in Buj were suspicious of them, so they moved back to Budapest, each family member in a different cart to blend in. After they came back to Budapest, Naftali was sent to a camp. His group was sent to the Hungary Austria border, and they all got Typhus. It's unclear if he died from the disease, or if the Nazis burn the shack they all lived in while they were inside. יהי זכרו ברוך.

text: August 9, 1969: Manson “Family” members Susan Atkins, Tex Watson, and Patricia Krenwinkel entered the home of Hollywood actress Sharon Tate and murdered her and four others. Linda Kasabian (née Drouin) was also present, but allegedly did not take part. Steven Earl Parent, who was present at the address only by unfortunate coincidence, lost his life that night, in addition to Sharon Tate and her houseguests, Jay Sebring, Abigail Folger, and Wojciech Frykowski. In an earlier incident involving Charles Manson and his followers, musician Gary Hinman was murdered after being held captive for several days, forced to turn over his property to his captors under torture and threat of death. Manson associate Robert “Bobby” Beausoleil killed Gary Hinman on the orders of Manson. Above: Lurid headlines abound in the days following the crimes at 10050 Cielo Drive in Benedict Canyon, north of Beverly Hills, the home of actress Sharon Tate and her husband, Polish movie director Roman Polanski. Above: Wilfred Parent, and his wife Juanita (néeJones) were unaware that their son Steven had gone to Cielo Drive that night to visit his friend, live-in caretaker William Garretson, hoping to sell him a used radio. He was shot by the intruders as he was preparing to leave the property. Above: Linda (née Drouin) Kasabian was given immunity for her testimony against the Manson Family defendants. She has maintained that she did not take physical part in the murders, but acted as a “lookout” only. Originally from New England, Linda Drouin (later Kasabian) is listed with her parents, Rosaire and Joyce (née Taylor) Drouin in the 1950 U. S. Federal Census, in Maine, Linda’s paternal grandparents having emigrated there from Quebec in the 1920s. Above: Colorado-born Gary Hinman was a musician living at this residence in Topanga Canyon, California in 1969, where Manson associate Bobby Beausoleil had lived with Hinman previously. During this time, Beausoleil had become acquainted with Charles Manson and his followers. Above: In late July of 1969, Beausoleil went with Manson associates Susan Atkins and Mary Brunner to Hinman's house in Topanga Canyon. After demanding money that Hinman did not have, Manson told them via phone to hold Hinman captive there at his house. When Manson arrived, armed with a bayonet, he struck Hinman, severely cutting his face and ear. Gary Hinman was held captive for three more days before being murdered by Bobby Beausoleil, on the instruction Charles Manson. Above: After a well-documented investigation and trial, the principal participants in the Manson “Family” murders were found guilty and sentenced to death. Bobby Beausoleil was convicted and sentenced to death for the July 27, 1969, fatal stabbing of Gary Hinman. Beausoleil, Manson, and the other participants who were sentenced to death were later granted commutation to a lesser sentence of life imprisonment, after the Supreme Court of California issued a ruling that invalidated all death sentences issued in California prior to 1972. Bobby Beausoleil is currently imprisoned in California. Manson died in prison in California in 2017. Linda Drouin Kasabian, who gave a handful of interviews about her participation in the 1969 murders, kept a low profile over the years and died in Tacoma, Washington on January 21, 2023, at the age of 73. (Published 2017)

text: So... I was chatting with my Gen-Z cousin yesterday, updating her about all of the family gossip--because apparently people forgot to tell her things--when I made a reference to one of the Tumblr holidays (the perfect date). And to my surprise, she caught the reference and came back with a reference to one of the Tumblr heritage posts about someone's refusal of the call/main character syndrome. When she did that, I sort of tilted my head said: "I like your shoelaces." There was a very long pause and she replied back: "Thanks, I stole them from the president." I laughed. Tumblrina confirmed. We continue the conversation but knowing that we had a similar frame of reference opened up a lot of doors and made the generational Gap disappear. Like I said she is Gen Z and I am very firmly Gen X. So I suspect that my cousin was really surprised that I would be on the queer, unprofitable social media site. Typically Facebook and Instagram are more gen X's thing. (I hate both with a burning passion). As the conversation came to a close, I told her that I would not attempt to find her. And that her secret was safe with me. Because seriously, unless you come out and use your real name or any other type of identifying information no one is going to know who you are on this wonderful hell site (affectionate). Tumblr bridging the generation gap one Tumblrina at a time.

text: For years, Mother had used a desk that had belonged to our Grandmother. When my brother and sister in law brought the desk to me, they left Mother's papers in it and I tucked them into a box and made a plan to "deal with them later." Well, today, later finally came. Among all the postcards, coupons and letters which she had saved, I found this box of unsent cards. In looking through them, I am pretty sure that these cards were in the desk when Mother moved the desk from her own Mother's house to our sun room. Mother saved them for sentimental reasons. Grandma's cards would have taken a 4 cent stamp, so these hot air balloon stamps are from much later. But she had a sentimental reason for saving these too! Mother and Daddy were born about a month apart. My youngest brother and his wife (the ones who later brought me the desk) arranged for us kids to chip in to give Mother and Daddy a hot air balloon ride to celebrate their 70th birthdays. Daddy was delighted. Mother, though, was much more cautious and was not convinced that this was such a good idea. She was astonished when Daddy said that he understood her reservations, but that if she didn't want to go, she could wave to him as he sailed upward. Mother thought about his determination to fly. Her mental picture of his being lifted off the earth, while she stayed on firm ground was too much for her to bear... If he was determined to go, she decided that she was going along! They had a wonderful time and used the photo taken at their takeoff as that year's Christmas card. She bought many of these stamps herself and received countless more as gifts. It gave her a lot of pleasure to use them to commemorate the time that she decided to throw caution to the winds.

text: You may be wondering what a recent flood in my basement could possibly have to do with my childhood, but read on! During a recent debacle, my basement flooded and many boxes and storage bins were saturated. My daughter helped me to haul the sodden boxes off the floor and we took out countless books, photos, clothes etc. For most of the soaked items all we could do was spread them out on towels and turn fans on them to dry. But I simply tossed the clothing into the washer. I wasn't sure if all of it was washable or not, but by this point it was too late to worry about these items were going to react with water. To my surprise, almost everything emerged, a little wrinkled, but unharmed. When I pulled this bed jacket out of the dryer, I could hardly believe my eyes. I believe that it is the bed jacket that Mother was wearing in this photograph that was taken of her as she held me, on my second day home from the hospital in September of 1946. During the Second World War, while all of their husbands were deployed, Mother, one of my aunts and one of their friends had shared a house. While he had been in the service, the husband of the friend had been trained in photography and he decided to set up a studio when he returned to civilian life. When I was born, he schlepped some of his equipment upstairs in the house my family had recently bought to take pictures of Mother and her new baby. It’s a good thing that I can identify the wallpaper because my older brother and I looked so much alike as babies that in some baby pictures even Mother incorrectly identified us! Over the years, the picture may have faded, but this pink satin bed jacket is the very bed jacket that the flood just unearthed in my basement!

In olden days (before we used Kleenex and other throwaway paper tissues) we used pieces of thin cloth when we had a cold. Men and boys used larger, thicker handkerchiefs. Women and girls used pretty daintier ones, Some of them were plain white, without decoration, oftentimes they had embroidery or lace edging . I have hankies from grandmothers and great aunts that are printed with flowers, seasonal themes, animals and birds. Some are brightly colored and I have even uncovered one plaid one. Mother carried this one with its wide lace edge as a bride. My grandparents who spent many winters in Florida brought souvenir hankies as gifts along with carved coconut "heads" for the boys and tiny bottles of orange blossom perfume for the girls. Of course they also brought back oranges and grapefruits and even small pots of tropical plants for Daddy's plant room. My sister contributed these hankies that are printed with a view of some impressive houses along one of the canals that were built for boats and recreation. We girls often tucked several handkerchiefs into our small purses. Our motto was "Carry one for Show, One for Blow and One for an Emergency." I suppose that our handkerchiefs were less immediately hygienic than tissues that could be thrown away along with our germs, but washed and freshly ironed, these hankies could be used (and enjoyed) over and over and over again. No waste to be disposed of either. PS Here is a handy hint for anyone who may still be using hankies. If you wash them out in the bathroom basin and then spread it out on the wall over the bathtub (to contain the drips) they will dry looking neatly pressed.

A story about my refugee Lithuanian great-grandfather because I love him so much Honestly ones of my favourite stories of him is in the 30s just before WWII. He was the oldest surviving child and his parents were pretty aged and weary and he had like 3 younger brothers and a younger sister. He wasn't very old in this story (17 or 18), but I know that his sister, Emilija, was 17. She needed a new coat. Badly. I don't know why, hers was probably too small and very old, so she desperately needed a new coat. Lithuania is very cold, it's above Poland, so very very cold. Vincas' family (Vince in my GG) were poor because Lithuania had just broken free of imperial russian and also the Great Depression was a thing, so his parents couldn't afford to buy a new one. Vincas decided to do something about that. He worked and worked and worked until he had enough money to buy her a new winter coat. He could have used his money for anything, yet he chose to get his sister a new coat. I don't have many details on the story, just that he got her a new coat, but from what I know of him he seems like the kind of person who wouldn't announce it dramatically and say "I'm such a wonderful person." In fact, I don't think he walked through the door and said "I GOT A COAT" at all. I think hebwould have been very casual and quiet about it, dropping it on her bed or handing it to her or even (in true older brother fashion) throwing it on her head and hiding until she realised what it was. I aspire to be like him one day. And I will be back with more tales of him

I know, I know, this looks like a more modern picture than I usually post, but bear with me. When I was little, I loved to watch Mother as she poured cream into the bowl of her stand mixer, lowered the beater into the bowl and turned the mixer to high. We kids watched, spellbound, as the cream gradually began to thicken and Mother added Confectioner's Sugar a little bit at a time as the mixer whirred on. Mother was very good at desserts and her kitchen- produced whipped cream almost always came together. This process was probably even more interesting to watch as we knew that at the end, once she had scraped the newly whipped cream into the serving bowl, we would be given the mixer and bowl to lick. But even for Mother, the process was not foolproof and occasionally she would be distracted (certainly not by us) and little yellow globules of butter would emerge. Add to this the fact that this method had to be performed at the very last minute (between dinner and dessert) with Daddy waiting impatiently at the dining room table. In 1954, when I was 8, a wonderful product was brought onto the market. The canister contained what was promised as real whipped cream, that was packaged under pressure. By gently depressing a nozzle supported by a short spring, we were promised that fully whipped cream would emerge. Daddy's Mother and our own Mother were absolutely delighted with the promise of this new product. They planned a family meal the culmination of which would be homemade pie topped with this new miracle product. Mother had promised Billy that he could be the first to dispense this treat. Everyone was still seated around our large dining room table, all of us and our grandparents primed for this wonderful new experience. Billy, standing at Mother's place at the table, slowly lowered the canister, pointing it toward the first piece of pie. All of us held our breath, the adults as primed as the kids. It turned out though, that Grandma, who always seemed unflappable to us kids, was the most excited of us all. Acting completely out of character, she stepped forward . "This really should be done by a grown-up." she announced, as she took the can out from Billy's outstretched hand. She quickly lowered the can, disregarding the fact that the can was no longer aimed downwards. She pressed down on the nozzle and a stream of whipped cream shot across the table and landed six feet away in Daddy's lap. This was met with dead silence. Inwardly we children might have been cheering her performance, but outwardly we were braced to see how Daddy would react. We could well imagine what his reaction would have been if Billy had done this… But Grandma was his own Mother and he simply began to laugh. All of us delightedly joined in. Even Grandma was laughing as she shook her head ruefully and handed the can back to Billy. The event still lives in family stories long after the novelty of whipped cream in a can has worn off.

This is a picture of a yardstick that my younger brother and I found at an antique mall over the weekend. Younger people might have never seen one of these before but in the 1950's most households would have had at least one and maybe several. These wooden sticks were passed out as advertisements at fairs, by political candidates and in stores. We kids could put them to use as short poles on which to hang banners during the parades we organized. When we placed them between chair backs and hung sheets from them, they could support our tents. And, of course, they could always be used as weapons during the battles that we were constantly staging. But, except for measuring things, we kids had absolutely no idea what an adult might do with them. . . . until this one night. Mother and Daddy didn't go out often but when they did, Mother was usually able, through the local YWCA, to find an older woman who for a small remuneration was willing to feed us dinner, read us stories and put us to bed. Mother always wondered why so few of these women returned for a second evening. My older brother could put me up to no end of mischief. We weren't bad exactly, just annoying. One of our favorite targets was babysitters who were, as I said, elderly and, unlike our Mother, didn't know when it was absolutely necessary to draw the line. On this one night, we were especially annoying. When Mrs. Shafer, that evening’s sitter, finally got us to go upstairs and get into our pajamas for bed, we began our game. I'd rush over and jump into Billy’s bed and when she dragged me back to my own room, he'd hide and pop out at her from under either my bed or his. Billy's bedroom was at the back of the house and mine at the front and in each of our rooms, our beds were placed so that they faced each other (well, technically I suppose it was our feet that faced each other) We started a new game then. First Billy would sit up in his bed while I lay flat and then he'd lay back down and I would pop up. I don't know how long it took us to tire of this game, but I do remember the babysitter, that poor hard-pressed older woman sitting grimly in the hall between our rooms clutching a yardstick in a very threatening way. I don't know if she would have used it if we had pressed her but in the actual event, she didn't. Either we got bored and fell asleep or our parents came home. I don't think that we saw Mrs Shafer again, but at least we had finally found out something an adult could do with a yardstick when they weren't using it for measuring.

Karen and I had both been searching for something on our journey to Montgomery. Against all odds, she found Milton. Such willingness comes gradually, slowly—until suddenly, in moments that illuminate the landscape like summer lightning, it’s no longer possible to look away. — Ann Banks in Smithsonian Magazine (2020). I've talked about this a bit already, but for those of you who don't know: my mom is very disabled. Like 100%, wheelchair-bound, even-not-moving-is-pretty-painful kind of disabled. My dad, meanwhile, is a man's man who has worked in construction and remodeling for over 40 years and will let his truck wheel catch on fire because he hasn't had the time to change the break pads yet. A do-it-yourself kind of dude. Now what I love about their relationship is that when they got married, my mom wasn't disabled yet. So the life that they thought they would have significantly changed as her condition deteriorated, but not once has my dad ever made my mom seem like a burden to us kids. Honestly, we never had to be told to help her out when she needed something. We just did it because that was the example set out for us. My mom's love language isn't acts of service and my dad does complain sometimes, so she doesn't always see it, but to me that's always been what family has been about: figuring out a way for everyone to be okay. We also rely on Mom in turn. She's been the one who budgets and plans and basically keeps this ship afloat in all honesty. She's the first person we come to with problems, and she often goes above and beyond to help us however she can. Anyway, my dad doesn't get sick very often himself, but when he does you'd best believe my mom babies the heck out of him. She'll go the extra mile, even though it hurts, to keep him warm and hydrated however she can. And it is absolutely adorable to see her take his needs more seriously than he does and forcing him to sit down so she can take care of him. Of course, my siblings and I sometimes get roped into having to do things, but that's just how our family works. I just really like how they look out for each other. Their relationship is far from perfect, but to me, they have always been a unit that relies on each other in order to make life work. There's no real point to this post other than to just share some thoughts. I think a lot of times, people look at disability as this bleak, hopeless situation, but it's really not. My mom doesn't always see it, but we all truly love her and she contributes so much to our family just by existing.

The delightful post going around right now where a woman who just got a hearing aid is recounting, astonished, the different sounds that she didn't realize people heard, reminds me of one of my favorite family stories. My parents were attending college together, and had been dating for some time, so mom invited dad to spend Christmas with them. Now, mom's father was an optometrist, and he offered to give my dad an eye exam and glasses if he needed them; dad hadn't had his eyes checked before, so agreed. He turned out to be moderately nearsighted — not enough that he couldn't navigate the world, but a significant amount — so he took the drive back to campus with a new pair of glasses. Dad's sort of the laconic, understated type; he was raised on a ranch, and he seems to take most things pretty coolly. But mom says he spent the entire drive back, hours and hours across high desert and salt flats, marveling at the amount of scenery that was now visible. At one turn, he pointed far across a valley to a distant mountain range, and said, astonished, "There are trees on those mountains!" Mom claims she abruptly felt retroactively very concerned that he'd been driving her around so much before getting those glasses.

text: I have just completed this painting which is my impression of how our house looked on a Christmas Eve in the 1950's. The car in the driveway is our 1951 (or was it '52?) Dodge Royale. The parrot in the window is Polly whom we inherited from Daddy's Godmother. His cage was perched on a radiator so he never seemed to be troubled by his location near the window. The large gray tree on the left of the painting represents an American Elm. It truly was in the front yard of the house that was next door to ours, but in this painting I have replanted it nearer to our house so that I could pay homage to those wonderful huge trees. This one was at least 3 feet in diameter. Where they had been planted on either side of a street, their branches extended high above the sidewalks and pavement. When we walked underneath them, it was like walking through a very high tunnel. We lost all of our elms to the Dutch Elm Disease in the late 50's and early 60's. People were just beginning to display outdoor Christmas lights in our small town during the years when we were growing up. Instead of the multicolored lights that were becoming popular, Mother substituted her own personal touch by buying strings of lights and replacing the many colored bulbs with these pale blue lights. In the years before my brothers were old enough to climb a ladder, she and Daddy themselves draped them along the evergreens in front of our house. But our indoor tree (which was always too large for our living room) still had multicolored and bubble lights. The lights on the tree in our living room were only multicolored, that is, until the year (around 1955 I think) when Daddy walked past a store that was selling tiny clear lights that seemed to twinkle by blinking on and off in sequence. This was the first time that he (or any of us for that matter) had seen this new variation of holiday lights. That year, of course,our family had already spent hours hanging all the colored lights and decorations on our tree as usual. But Daddy, who always held on to his childlike view of Christmas, couldn't resist this new effect. He bought those new lights and secretly brought them home. On Christmas Eve that year, after we children had gone to bed, with Mother's loyal assistance, he painstakingly removed all the decorations and colored lights from our tree. Then they strung the tiny white lights on the denuded tree, rehung the colored lights over them and replaced all the ornaments so that they hung from the branches just as if they had never been moved. My brothers and sister and I remember being stunned and enchanted by the magic of those twinkling lights that seemed to us had magically transformed our Christmas tree overnight. We Had A Riverboat In Our Livingroom on Tumblr

My grandfather, Eugene Deery of Pawtucket, RI, 29th Infantry, was on one of the first boats going in to Omaha Beach when he was knocked out of the craft by a shell. Floating in the water, he was fished out, patched up (earning a Purple Heart for his trouble) and sent in with the second wave. Other than his Commander, he was the only man from his unit to survive D-Day, and because he had previously worked as a reporter, he could type, and ended up writing letters home to surviving family members. He kept a list of the names and addresses in his dresser drawer all his life. He proceeded to fight through St. Lo, marched into Paris, and was at the Bulge (wounded again, though he'd previously lost hearing in his left ear after his buddy stepped on a landmine, he never counted that as a wound because it wasn't a bullet or shell, just noise) and served in the Occupation of Germany. He married on returning home, went on to have seven children, six surviving him. He worked as a mail carrier, butcher, and ticket taker at the race tracks of Rhode Island. A man of true Hibernian charm, he was always ready to burst into song in a strong, confident voice. He very rarely spoke of the war, and when he did it was never to praise himself, only those who had died or the "fun" they got up to. Some of this fun included the Liberation of Paris and a trip to Belfast in early 1944, when he snuck over the border into the Irish Free State to hang out with his cousins living there for the duration of his leave. He also survived the ill-fated Exercise Tiger training landing for D-Day.

text: We had a family gathering recently. We don't actually belong to any religious or spiritual...whatever. But round about this time of year we *do* have Artichoke Fest. Because artichokes are great and we are very white and love an excuse for a variety of flavored mayonnaises. At EVERY family gathering for as long as at least some of us have been old enough to live independently, my father tells the story of how he came home from his first semester of college to discover his mother threw away his Spiderman comics. They were original 1st runs because this was back in the late 60s. He still talks about this. Several times a year. I think it's less about how much those would be worth now, or even the comics themselves as something he loved, and more about how little his parents saw or valued him and his interests. It's his weird roundabout way of telling us he sees us and he would never throw away or dismiss things that are important to us. But he's in his 70s and came from a fucked up rich family so he has limited vocabulary for that. So instead we get the Spiderman Comics Story. Even his grandchild knows the story by heart at this point. We all use it as shorthand for people devaluing or overlooking important stuff about others: "It was a Spiderman Comics situation".

Okay-- you guys want another cute story about my parents and food? So my mother is an amazing baker. And as I mentioned in a reblog of the Oranges post, my parents now always keep fresh fruit in the house -- particularly bananas (my dad likes them with his breakfast). And whenever the bananas go brown, mom makes (really goddamn delicious) banana bread. My parents are very avid outdoorsy folks and do a lot of hiking. They live in a mountainous region and basically climb a mountain every weekend (most of the mountains here are under 6,000 feet, but rocky), including in the middle of winter. Because cold and exertion eat through your blood sugar, they always pack trail snacks, and they developed a tradition of bringing a mini loaf of mom’s banana bread that they share on the summit. Now a few years ago my father was having a midlife crisis and decided he was going to hike a REALLY big mountain. So he signed up for an expedition to climb Mt. Denali in Alaska -- the tallest peak in North America. The group he was going with had a trip planned where everyone would be responsible for hiking with and carrying their own gear, so you had to be prepared to hike up a big fuckoff mountain in potentially treacherous conditions with a heavy pack. My mother was not going on this expedition (she has problems with altitude sickness) but dammit, she was not going to let my dad go get himself killed by being unprepared. So in the year leading up to his climb, she kinda became his personal trainer. They hiked the local mountains a lot and in all kinds of weather conditions, practiced rope training and crevasse rescue techniques, and she made sure he practiced climbing with increasingly heavy packs until he was hauling around 65lbs of weights on his back. Sometimes she would even sit in a sled in the snow and make him pull her. When the trip finally came, dad was incredibly excited, and amused that his gear pack actually ended up being lighter than his practice pack. A number of other folks in the expedition had practiced going up a stair machines with weight on their backs, but mom had dragged dad through all the really rocky, treacherous trails around here with ice and water crossings, so he had solid balance from really moving with that amount of weight. Over the course of the climb (which took a couple weeks), half of the hikers ended up turning back (for various reasons), but despite being the oldest in the group, dad was one of the few to summit. And on the day he made it to the top, at 20,310 feet of elevation, he pulled out of his pack, wrapped in foil and mostly frozen but intact despite the long trip, a loaf of my mother’s banana bread, to eat on top of the world. Because, he said later, even if she wasn’t there with him, she was the reason he made it to the top.

text: Years after all we kids had grown, I found this unused ant farm in my parents' basement. We had always referred to this as "Uncle Milton's Ant Farm", and I couldn’t recall why. It wasn’t because it was a gift from one of our own uncles, because we had no Uncle Milton. It turns out that it was a product of Uncle Milton Industries. The box has been opened, but the ant farm has remained unused. This is probably not so much due to the reluctance of Mother to have ants introduced into our home on purpose as it was to the aversion that my brothers still carried with them for the debacle of the beehive that Daddy had set up in their bedroom one earlier summer. The A I Root Company was located in our hometown. At that time, this company was the world's largest producer of bee products. Everyone in town was familiar with stories about ‘old A I Root’ and his relationship with Wilbur and Orville Wright. The first printed report of the Wright Brothers' flight at Kitty Hawk appeared in Root's "Gleanings in Bee Culture." Daddy had worked there for a while as a young man and he, like a lot of people in town was fascinated with Root's work with bees. That fascination led him to place a small hive in the bedroom that was shared by two of my brothers. The hive was a square wooden frame that was sealed on the sides by some flexible plastic. There was a tube that ran from the small entrance to the hive at the bottom of the frame. That tube was threaded underneath their sash window frame which was then closed over the tube. At first things went well, the bees scurried up or down the tube to enter or leave the hive to sail out in their search for pollen. As the summer progressed, however, the hive became overly full. Apparently either the bees had made a LOT of honey or they had somehow multiplied, because the plastic sides began to bulge out from the wooden frame and the boys (and Mother) objected to the bees that were escaping into the boys bedroom and through their door into the rest of the upstairs of our house. The problem was complicated by Mother's absolute refusal to allow Daddy to carry the damaged hive from the boys' room through the rest of the house trailing escaping bees in his path. Daddy, as the man who would be carrying the hive, was forced to agree that that option was far from ideal. The boys' room was situated next to a low roof over our back porch. One evening, Daddy climbed up onto that roof while smoking a cigar. He blew the smoke at the end of the tube, hoping it would sedate the bees and he hit them with a fly swatter as they emerged one by one. He was not happy about the carnage, but what else, he said, could he do? When finally enough bees had left the hive that the plastic sides stopped bulging, he was able to get the hive out of the house. It occurs to me as I type this, that it might have been easier to have simply placed a large plastic bag over the hive, but maybe we didn't have any large plastic bags in those days. Or maybe Daddy simply enjoyed sitting on the roof while smoking his cigar. It is the kind of scene that would have appealed to him. In any event, his introduction of the Ant Farm one Christmas did not meet with much enthusiasm so the whole farm is still in the box, along with a bag of what is labeled "sand" and a user's manual, which has information presented in a pretty charming manner.

text: After Grandpa D's retirement, Mother's parents spent several winters in Florida. They returned each year to an area in the central part of the state where they met returning friends who had also left the ice and snow behind. Each year, like so many other travelers, they arranged for oranges to be shipped "back home." One of my early memories is watching Mother as she held the halves of sliced oranges against the juicer attachment that she fastened to the top of her 1940's era Kitchen Aid mixer. Mother obviously knew how to get the brightly colored juice out of the oranges so that as she pressed it, it trickled from the reamer down into the glass that she had placed on the counter below. The motor arm part of this mixer could be tipped up and back so that she could insert a Pyrex mixing bowl and beater on the base. She tipped the top back down so that the beater nestled in the bowl when she wanted to do regular mixing. (Image from eBay) Mother and Daddy had explained that Grandma and Grandpa had sent those remarkable oranges from their warm winter home. Where our grandparents were staying, I was told, these large orange balls of fruit were growing outside on trees! It was icy and freezing cold outside where we lived and as I looked out the window, I couldn't really imagine how it could be warm where our grandparents were, much less how they could have come across this amazing fruit. I pondered a bit and then came up with an explanation to make sense of everything. This whole thing, I decided, was clearly magic. It may have been freezing here in Ohio, but Grandma and Grandpa must be friends with a magician. All this was mysterious magic and when I was older I would be able to work the magic too. This picture of my grandparents was taken around 1950. One family story that explains this scene is that there was some kind of accident where all this fruit was dumped out and left to roll around in the street. In this version, the aging snowbird group to which my grandparents belonged, found the fruit there, picked it up and arranged this Valentine tribute. I lean toward an easier explanation. I think that some enterprising citrus salesman had created the citrus heart to encourage drivers to stop by to get a better look. Once they were stopped he hoped that they would wander into his shed and purchase his fruit. Grandpa himself had been in sales (for a printing company) and I can imagine him pulling over, intrigued by the imaginative sales approach and, knowing Grandpa, his next thought would have been: "Why not snap a photo to send home?"

text: During the years that we were growing up, Mother always told us that this had been her Grandmother's cookie jar. In those days it had an earthenware lid, which nestled loosely on top of the jar itself. Mother did, in fact, put some of our homemade cookies in it. Since our homemade cookies usually were frosted, each cookie stuck to the cookies above, below, and on either side, effectively creating cookie bricks. We were a family with 5 children and I am now realizing that Mother must have had another stash in which to hide cookies. A little keg like this wouldn’t have room for even a single batch of the cookies made from Great Grandma's sugar cookie recipe (whether stuck into bricks or not) and Mother never made the recipe without at least doubling it. Of course all of us did love cookies, so the cookie jar didn't have to hold each batch of cookies very long. But writing this has started me thinking. Mother's Grandmother (our Great Grandmother) was herself the mother of twelve. Now there is absolutely no chance that this cookie jar could hold more than a day's worth of cookies for a family of 14. So now I am wondering: is this really how Grandma Frase used this jar? Some time over the years, the earthenware lid disappeared. And I don't use it for cookies. Any cookies stored inside would be stale long before I managed to eat all of them. So I keep this jar on the corner of my kitchen counter to remind me of many happy memories. (Also, it is a handy place to stash the plastic bags from the grocery store.)

text: so at thanksgiving Dude’s aunt told the story of how they arrived in the United States, she and her parents and baby brother, as refugees after the end of WWII. Now, she explained, we’d come from a warzone-- well, the war was over, but we all remembered, all of us. They’d spent most of the war in a displaced persons’ camp in Germany; for part of the time they’d lived in a decommissioned hotel, which wasn’t just decommissioned but was actually abandoned. To say they’d lived rough, been through a lot, well. They’d seen some shit . She was very small; they fled the family farm when she wasn’t quite three, and had spent several years moving around. Her brother was born in the refugee camp. They came to the US, and they got off in New York Harbor and she remembers that it was the third of July, but she couldn’t remember if it was 1945 or 46. The man who had sponsored them, a farmer in Virginia who’d been relying on prison labor and thought he might get better results out of refugees, came to get them in an open-backed pickup truck, with hay bales for them to sit on. She can’t remember exactly but she figured there were probably a dozen people with them, including her and one other child, but everyone else was an able-bodied adult. They rode down from New York to Virginia in the back of this open truck, and didn’t arrive until the next day. It was horribly hot, they weren’t prepared, they were all wearing their best clothes which were wool; Latvia is on the same latitude as Montreal, and none of them had ever experienced summer before, not really. They arrived, finally, and had been given a house to live in, which had no furniture except a table and two chairs. Some of the rooms the floorboards were rotten so you couldn’t go in. There were no beds. So they had to take their clothes out of their suitcases-- each of them had one suitcase, with all their worldly possessions, and that was it-- and arrange those in a pile on the floor, and try to go to sleep on them. The sun went down, and they all lay down to sleep on their makeshift beds, and then-- the sky exploded. The refugees all huddled together, the women crying and screaming, the children incoherent, even the men frightened. None of them knew what was going on. To have come so far from war, and then war had followed them here! Explosions, whistling shells, heavy artillery sounds-- they all wept, and finally it stopped, and then there was silence. Nothing. No sounds of tanks, no more explosions. The next morning, the more educated among the adults took their dictionaries and tried to ask what was going on. The Americans were baffled, but finally someone managed to explain that it was customary to let off fireworks in celebration on the fourth of July. So that was the first Fourth of July dude’s aunt experienced in America. By the end of the week two of the men were dead of heatstroke, and the police came and took them away and dispersed them to other farms.

text: My family has a great history of internalized xenophobia. Actually, like many USSR families. As far as I know (but I couldn’t be shure) my Mum is Russian. But my Dad’s family is more interesting. Officially he is Russian too. And proud of it because he is a conservative Russian nationalist. But in reality: -his paternal grandfather is obviously Jewish. He had famous Jewish relatives. The problem is - this my grand-grandpa hated to be Jewish. He was deeply ashamed of it. In some documents he literally tried to cross the “Jewish” line and write “Russian” instead. (Pretty bad attempt as I could say because you could actually see what he is crossing. Whatever, spoiled document couldn’t change obvious facts). -his wife, my father’s granny, has a really weird patronymic and surname - probably Jewish. Despite that she was “Ukrainian” in her documents, it was really odd that she was hanging around with Jewish a lot, because Ukrainian people in this region was really antisemitic. -My father’s maternal grandpa… Well, he is the reason why I look like Asian in some photos and Dad looks Asian in many photos. My grand-gran was “Russian” by documents but as my father admired, his grandpa looked really Asian. My father used to say that he looks like a person with obviously Japanese descent (partly White, partly Japanese, to speak more precisely). But it’s also possible that he has some Chinese or even Korean heritage. Anyway, he was born in a city that borders China and has a port connecting the Russian Empire with Asian countries. So… only one thing is obvious: he could be anyone but not a pure Russian. -My Dad’s maternal Granny was “Russian who has some Greek roots». And of course Russian by documents. And yep, after all of that my father is “magically” Russian. I think this could be interesting for people who believe that the USSR was free from xenophobia. And for everyone who wants to know WHO some nationalists really are: sometimes they are just ashamed of their own heretech, with so deep internalized xenophobia that they completely ignore it and have hate not to the society that marginalized them, but to other people like them and their ancestors. And sometimes they have this hatred even toward their own kids who refused to be inner racists and xenophobs. -Ayman (Photo 1: Ayman’s dad. Photo 2: Young Ayman)

text: In front of the second gift shop, where we lived above, early 1970s. (Courtesy of Raymond Lee). My father, Frank Lee (1925-92), was the second of six siblings to a successful merchant and his second wife. The family had moved from their hometown of Zhaoqing, Guangdong to Tientsin and Qinhuangdao to embark on various businesses, including a night club and general store in the early 1930s. After my paternal grandfather passed away in the late 1930s, he and his oldest brother were responsible for running the family’s affairs until Frank left for an extended visit to Formosa (Taiwan) in 1948. While touring the island, he received word from my grandmother that the Chinese Communists were poised to defeat the Nationalists (Kuomintang) and govern China in 1949. Alas, he dared not return to the mainland, and settled in Taipei for the interim. After working at various odd jobs, he enrolled at the National Taiwan Normal University to train as a teacher. Upon graduation, he instructed at an elementary school in Taipei in the mid-1950s, before being reassigned as vice-principal at an overseas Chinese middle school in Tahiti in 1958. He realized, however, that this was a dead-end posting and left for America to seek his fortunes a year later. In Polynesian sarong, 1958. (Courtesy of Raymond Lee). Frank arrived in San Francisco on a tourist visa, but quickly changed his visa status to student after enrolling at John Adams Adult School. Through a mutual classmate, he met Virginia (nee Tsui), his future wife and our mother, in 1960. After a brief courtship, they wedded in January, 1961, followed by my arrival later that year. Frank taught Mandarin-Chinese at the Army Language School in Monterey during the early to mid-1960s. In 1965, he resigned this teaching position to operate a retail stall inside the Chinese Cultural Center on Grant Avenue, selling colourful batiks painted by himself. The following year, he closed the stall and opened a gift shop next to the renowned restaurant, Shanghai Low, selling his artwork, imported handicrafts from Taiwan, Japan and Thailand, and sundry souvenirs of San Francisco. Three years later, a second shop was opened on the other side of Shanghai Low. Virginia helped run the businesses, while also employed as key-punch operator at several firms downtown. Family portrait, 1963. (Courtesy of Raymond Lee). Throughout the late 1960s to the early 1980s, my parents raised my brother, Oscar, and me, all the while operating the shops, which evolved to selling jewelry and higher-end merchandise in the 1980s. Frank was active in the Chinatown Merchants Association during this time. He instilled within us the importance of industry and self-reliance when we were old enough to help out. He also home-schooled me in written and spoken (Mandarin-)Chinese during after-school hours and on weekends. I remember him as a firm but fair parent, an innovative entrepreneur, and engaged in the local community, who had various self-taught talents (besides painting, he played the erhu and snare drum), and was physically fit until the end. Regrettably, he did not live to know of his extended family, including grandchildren Nerys, Kaeden and Kendra. Written by CHSA community member, Raymond Lee. Lee was born, raised, and educated in San Francisco. Raymond and his wife reside in Winnipeg, Canada, and Saint-Denis, France. He is currently employed at the Asper School of Business, University of Manitoba.

I’m with Mom for the weekend, helping out with a little house rearrangement. She told me about how she and Dad got the loan to build it. Seems that at the time, the president of the bank in Sandstone, N, may have had bad blood with Grandpa, because they did not get along. When Dad and Mom went to the bank for a loan, he turned them down flat. When they went to the bank in the next town south, Hinckley, the owner of that bank said, “I’m sorry, but if N turned you down for a loan, then I have to as well, I can’t go against him.” Then Dad applied for a federal loan (probably the GI Bill, Mom doesn’t remember), only to have them turn him down as well. Why? Because somehow N had found out about Dad’s application and sent a letter to the Feds saying “you don’t need to loan him the money because I can take care of it.” Without, of course, actually intending to make the loan. Which is how Dad and Mom wound up going to a big bank all the way in Duluth for their home loan. And also why all their personal banking was done in Askov, the second town north of Sandstone.

text: When I was younger, I used to spend the summers with my paternal aunt Sara in Georgia, USA and once, we went to a fancy party for my uncle's boss, I was wearing a dress, cute sandals, and had my hair in pretty braids. Anyway, there weren't many girls my age and I was a shy kid so I stuck close to my older brother (older by three years) and I played with the other boys (we raced monster trucks, played don't let the balloon touch the floor, and had a burping contest--which I won) so we decided to play tag when an absolute fucking Karen calls me over and says "Your dress is so pretty!" and me being a child I said "Thank you!" with the largest smile when she brought me close to her and proceeded to scold me about playing with boys. That I was a girl and I had to act like one and I shouldn't be acting so unladylike and gross, and that i should sit down and be a good little girly girl. I didn't say anything bc it was the first time ANYONE had ever told me that. I grew up with four older brother and no sisters, my mom worked 7-7 everyday, and my dad genuinely never put gender restrictions on me. I wanted to play basketball? he bought a hoop just for me. I wanted to play with army men figures and cars? he bought me any hot wheels I wanted. If I wanted to chop my hair off and make farting noises with my armpit? my brothers taught me. So anyway, here I was just kinda walking around a little pouty and hurt when my aunt saw me, called me over, and asked what was wrong. I snitched the lady out and when I tell y'all my aunt proceeded to say "Donde se fue ese pinche vieja?!" (Where did that fucking old lady go? mean version) and I watched as my 200 lb ghetto mexican as hell aunt frighten the fuck out of that white lady and embarrass her in front of EVERYBODY. It was the funniest shit EVER. We left shortly after and my uncle was never invited to anymore parties, but the way she stood up for me even as I was a child, really stuck with me. We don't talk anymore and I don't visit but that's a story I love sharing. I bet she doesn't even remember but I'll always love her for it. I do wonder what happened to that Karen though.
